Once present, two payoffs land: (a) the operator can `/agents` to discover and invoke minsky's persona library from their own Claude session — "use the researcher agent to investigate X" routes through Claude's native sub-agent invocation; (b) when minsky's tick-loop spawns a multi-persona pipeline (task `multi-persona-pipeline-handoff-spec`), each persona is invoked via Claude's Task tool (sub-agent dispatch) instead of via a separate `claude --print --model ...` subprocess — same isolation, half the per-invocation overhead (no fresh process startup, no `~/.claude` re-bootstrap), and the handoff files at `.minsky/handoffs/<task-id>/` stay the inter-persona protocol.
  - **Success**: (1) `ls .claude/agents/*.md` lists ≥7 personas (researcher, manager, alternatives-analyst, developer, qa-engineer, reviewer, agent-improver — the core orchestrator pipeline) — count via `find .claude/agents -name "*.md" -type f | wc -l` ≥7. (2) Operator runs `claude` in the minsky repo, types `/agents`, sees the personas — manually verified end-to-end. (3) "Use the researcher agent to investigate the test-coverage gap" spawns a Claude sub-agent that reads the researcher persona's instructions; the sub-agent produces output that conforms to the researcher's handoff format (validated against `novel/handoff-spec/schema.json`). (4) The multi-persona pipeline test (`user-stories/008-per-task-backend-and-personas.test.ts`'s skipped pipeline case) activates and passes when this task ships AND `multi-persona-pipeline-handoff-spec` ships — both together enable the M2 pipeline path. (5) `.claude/agents/*.md` regenerates idempotently from `scripts/generate-claude-agents.mjs` — re-running the script with no source changes produces a zero-diff git status.
  - **Pivot**: if Claude Code's sub-agent contract is too restrictive to encode the full minsky persona surface — specifically the per-persona budget caps (rule #15), the handoff-file output contract (`.minsky/handoffs/<task-id>/<persona>-<iso-ts>.md`), or the failure-mode boundary (a failed persona files `pipeline-failed-<task>-<persona>` and stops the pipeline) — abandon the deep integration and ship a thin discovery wrapper instead: `.claude/agents/*.md` files that contain only `name` + `description` + a one-line directive "the actual implementation runs as a minsky subprocess; this entry exists so `/agents` discovery surfaces the library". Threshold for pivot: ≥2 of the 5 success criteria above cannot be met within the Claude sub-agent surface after 2 implementation attempts.
  - **Measurement**: (a) `find .claude/agents -name "*.md" -type f | wc -l` returns ≥7. (b) `node scripts/validate-claude-agents.mjs` (new) parses each `.claude/agents/*.md`, verifies YAML frontmatter conforms to Claude Code's documented schema (name, description, tools, model — case-sensitive field names per the public docs), and exits 0. (c) `node scripts/generate-claude-agents.mjs --dry-run` produces no diff against the committed files (idempotency). (d) The pipeline integration test: `pnpm vitest run user-stories/008-per-task-backend-and-personas.test.ts --reporter=json | jq -e '.numFailedTests == 0 and (.testResults[] | .assertionResults[] | select(.title | contains("pipeline"))) | length >= 3'` — ≥3 pipeline-named test cases pass. (e) End-to-end manual verification documented in `docs/claude-agents.md` Quickstart section.
  - **Anchor**: Claude Code documentation on sub-agents (`docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/sub-agents` — the `.claude/agents/*.md` format spec) composed with the actor-model pattern (Hewitt 1973, IJCAI — independent actors communicate via messages; sub-agents ARE actors, the Task tool IS the message dispatch) and the adapter pattern (rule #2 — Claude /agents is the external surface, `novel/adapters/persona-spawner/` is the existing adapter that ALREADY wraps persona invocation; this task adds a new implementation behind the same interface that targets Claude /agents instead of subprocess CLI). The dependency-as-adapter discipline keeps `multi-persona-pipeline-handoff-spec` agnostic of WHICH dispatch substrate routes the personas.
  - **Details**: (1) **Inventory step** — list every persona currently encoded somewhere in the minsky distribution. Sources: `.claude/skills/orchestrator-*/SKILL.md` (canonical persona definitions), the `subagent_*` profile descriptions in `~/.agents/profiles/` (operational scope-and-tools), and the personas mentioned in `vision.md § "Orchestrator personas"` (intent + literature anchors). Cross-reference these three into a single canonical-persona table at `novel/persona-registry/canonical.md`. (2) **Schema-pinning** — author a Claude-Code-sub-agent-compatible YAML frontmatter schema at `novel/persona-registry/claude-agent-schema.json`. Required fields per the public Claude Code docs: `name` (kebab-case, `^[a-z][a-z0-9-]+$`), `description` (one-line invocation hint), `tools` (allowlist; `*` for full), `model` (`sonnet`, `opus`, etc.). Allowed extension keys: `minsky.budgetMs`, `minsky.handoffFormat`, `minsky.failureMode` — namespaced so Claude Code ignores them while minsky's pipeline reads them. (3) **Generator** — `scripts/generate-claude-agents.mjs` (new) reads every `.claude/skills/orchestrator-*/SKILL.md`, parses its frontmatter + body, applies the schema, and writes the resulting `.claude/agents/<persona>.md`. Idempotent: re-running with no source changes is a no-op. (4) **Validator** — `scripts/validate-claude-agents.mjs` (new) checks every `.claude/agents/*.md` against the schema; wired into pre-pr-lint as a fast-stage check (`claude-agents-schema`). (5) **Pipeline-side integration** — `novel/adapters/persona-spawner/src/claude-agent.ts` (new, sibling to the existing subprocess-CLI implementation `process-spawn.ts`) dispatches a persona via Claude's Task tool when the runtime detects it's executing inside a Claude session (env var `CLAUDECODE=1` or equivalent). The choice between subprocess and Task-tool dispatch lives in `novel/adapters/persona-spawner/src/strategy.ts`, defaulting to subprocess when no Claude session is detected and to Task-tool when one is. (6) **Hot reload** — the daemon's next iteration reads `.claude/agents/*.md` fresh on each persona invocation; no restart needed. Edits to a persona file land in the next pipeline. (7) **Docs** — `docs/claude-agents.md` (new) documents the persona library, how to add a new one, how the operator invokes `/agents`, and the pipeline integration.
  - **Files**: `.claude/agents/researcher.md` (new), `.claude/agents/manager.md` (new), `.claude/agents/alternatives-analyst.md` (new), `.claude/agents/developer.md` (new), `.claude/agents/qa-engineer.md` (new), `.claude/agents/reviewer.md` (new), `.claude/agents/agent-improver.md` (new) — plus optional `pipeline-runner.md`, `indie-hacker.md`, `growth-hacker.md`, `user.md`, `strategist.md` if scope permits in slice 1; `novel/persona-registry/canonical.md` (new), `novel/persona-registry/claude-agent-schema.json` (new); `scripts/generate-claude-agents.mjs` (new), `scripts/generate-claude-agents.test.mjs` (new), `scripts/validate-claude-agents.mjs` (new), `scripts/validate-claude-agents.test.mjs` (new); `novel/adapters/persona-spawner/src/claude-agent.ts` (new), `novel/adapters/persona-spawner/src/strategy.ts` (modify to switch on runtime detection), `novel/adapters/persona-spawner/src/strategy.test.ts` (extend); `docs/claude-agents.md` (new); `vision.md` (add row to Pattern conformance index — adapter pattern, claude-agent dispatch); `README.md` (add `## More` link to `docs/claude-agents.md`); `user-stories/008-per-task-backend-and-personas.test.ts` (activate the skipped pipeline test case).
  - **Touches**: .claude/agents/, novel/persona-registry/, novel/adapters/persona-spawner/, scripts/{generate,validate}-claude-agents.mjs, docs/claude-agents.md
  - **Acceptance**: (1) Measurement criteria (a)-(d) above all pass. (2) The skipped pipeline test in `user-stories/008-*.test.ts` activates and passes (uses `describe.skipIf(!process.env.MINSKY_CLAUDE_AGENTS_ENABLED)` until the substrate ships). (3) `pnpm pre-pr-lint --stage=full` passes — the new `claude-agents-schema` lint catches malformed frontmatter at PR-time. (4) Operator-side smoke: run `claude` in a fresh shell in the minsky repo, type `/agents`, manually confirm the persona library appears. Document the smoke procedure in `docs/claude-agents.md` Quickstart. (5) Rule-#10 (deterministic enforcement): the generator + validator are deterministic; re-running produces zero diff on a no-change source.
  - **Risk**: medium. The Claude Code sub-agent schema is documented but evolving; a future minor version may break the generator. Mitigation: pin to the documented schema version + version the schema file (`schema_version: 1` in YAML frontmatter); the validator's first check is the schema_version, so a future Claude Code update that changes the schema fails LOUDLY at lint-time rather than silently. Secondary risk: persona-tool-access posture must match Claude Code's `tools:` allowlist semantics — research persona allowed `read,grep,glob` but blocked `edit,write`; developer persona allowed full toolbox; reviewer allowed `read` only. Mis-specifying the allowlist defeats the security boundary (rule #13). Mitigation: each persona's `tools:` list is reviewed in PR comments and matches the documented persona scope.
  - **Note**: composes with `multi-persona-pipeline-handoff-spec` (the handoff format that personas read/write) and `motivation-bullet-test-coverage-completion` (story 008's `it.skip` cases activate against this implementation). Order: `multi-persona-pipeline-handoff-spec` ships first (the handoff substrate); this task ships second (the dispatch substrate); the M2 pipeline becomes live when both land. Optional follow-up: P2 `claude-agents-marketplace-publication` — once the persona library is canonicalised under `.claude/agents/`, consider publishing the library as a stand-alone npm package (`@minsky/claude-personas`) so other repos can install minsky's persona library without installing minsky itself.

- [ ] `minsky-npx-install-and-run` — `npx minsky` (no subcommand, no prior install) should both INSTALL minsky on the machine AND start a daemon iteration in a single command, so a new operator's literal first interaction is one shell line
  - **ID**: minsky-npx-install-and-run
  - **Tags**: p1, milestone-m1, ux, install, distribution, operator-directive, observed-2026-05-20
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Surfaced-by**: operator 2026-05-20 reviewing README — "Add a P1 task to make it install+runnable with a super simple command eg with npx". Today the install path is `git clone + cd + pnpm install + minsky`. The existing P0 `minsky-init-one-command-bootstrap` covers `npx minsky init` (sets up the sidecar) but leaves the operator to run `minsky` separately as a second command. The simpler operator-mental-model is: type ONE line in your shell, get a running iteration.
  - **Hypothesis**: publish minsky to npm as `@minsky/cli` (or just `minsky`, if the name's available) with a `bin` entry pointing at the existing `bin/minsky`. `npx minsky` (no subcommand) detects whether `~/.minsky/config.json` exists: if not, runs the `init` flow first (interactive choices: cwd as default_host, claude/devin/aider preference, model tier), then drops into the normal start-or-attach behaviour. If config exists, behaves identically to a local `minsky` invocation. Post-fix, a new operator runs ONE command (`npx minsky`) on a fresh macOS / Ubuntu box with Node 20 and is in a live iteration within ≤3 min — beating the current 10+ min clone-and-configure flow AND beating every OSS competitor's install path (Aider's pip install, OpenHands' Docker setup, SWE-agent's conda dance).
  - **Success**: (1) `npx -y minsky` on a fresh macOS VM with only Node 20 installed reaches "live iteration in progress" within 3 min (90th percentile). (2) Same on Ubuntu 24.04 VM. (3) `npx minsky --version` prints the published version without side-effects (probe-mode for scripts). (4) The published tarball is ≤5 MB compressed (npm convention; avoid heavy deps). (5) `npm uninstall -g minsky` or `npx minsky uninstall` (composing with the in-flight `minsky-uninstall-one-command-with-stop` P0) leaves zero residue.
  - **Pivot**: if npm publication blocks on a name conflict (`minsky` is taken by some other package), publish as `@minsky/cli` and document `npx @minsky/cli` as the canonical command. If `npx` startup latency is >10 s (cold cache, slow CI runner) the operator perceives the command as broken — pivot to `brew install minsky` (macOS) AND `curl -fsSL https://minsky.dev/install | sh` (universal) as primary, with `npx` as a backup. Threshold for pivot: 3 operators in 30 days report "I thought npx hung".
  - **Measurement**: `time npx -y minsky </dev/null` on a fresh VM with cold npm cache prints wall-clock ≤180s; `node_modules/.npm/_cacache` is sized ≤50 MB after the install (no surprise bloat); `pgrep -f cross-repo-runner` returns a non-empty PID list within 60s of npx completing.
  - **Anchor**: rule #1 (don't reinvent — npm is the universal Node distribution channel); existing P0 `minsky-init-one-command-bootstrap` (composes with this — init is the prereq); Forsgren/Humble/Kim *Accelerate* 2018 (lead time — install IS the first lead-time metric); Krug *Don't Make Me Think* 2014 (one obvious path).
  - **Details**: (a) Add `bin` entry to root `package.json` pointing at `bin/minsky`. (b) Add `"files": ["bin/", "novel/", "scripts/", "lefthook.yml", "package.json"]` to limit the published tarball (exclude tests, fixtures, .minsky/, etc.). (c) `bin/minsky` already detects "no daemon → init + start"; add an "is this an npx invocation?" probe (env var `npm_lifecycle_event` or `npm_config_user_agent` — both set by npx) and prepend the `init` flow when (i) npx, AND (ii) no `~/.minsky/config.json`, AND (iii) interactive TTY. (d) Publish via `pnpm publish --access=public` from CI on tagged releases (v0.1.0+). (e) Test the published tarball end-to-end via `pnpm pack` + `npm install` in a clean Docker container; assert `minsky --version` works.
  - **Files**: `package.json` (`bin`, `files`, `version` fields), `bin/minsky` (npx-detection branch in the smart-attach block), `.github/workflows/release.yml` (new — npm publish on tag), `test/integration/npx-install.test.ts` (new — Docker-based fresh-install smoke).
  - **Touches**: `package.json`, `bin/minsky`, `.github/workflows/release.yml`, `test/integration/npx-install.test.ts`, `README.md`
  - **Acceptance**: (1) The published `minsky` package on npmjs.com has version ≥0.1.0 and `npx -y minsky` returns "live iteration in progress" on a fresh `node:20-bookworm` Docker container within 3 min. (2) README's Getting Started leads with `npx minsky` as the install line; the current `git clone + pnpm install` flow drops to a "Contributor setup" subsection. (3) `npm view minsky` shows the package size ≤5 MB. (4) Reverse path verified: `npx minsky uninstall` removes the runtime cleanly.
  - **Risk**: medium. npm publication requires the name to be free; npx startup is variable depending on the operator's npm cache; multi-platform launchd / systemd setup inside an npx-installed CLI is more complex than a local-clone install (the published tarball has to ship the distribution unit files but install them only on opt-in). Mitigation: paired Docker integration test for fresh-install; npm name reservation as a one-time admin step.
  - **Note**: depends on P0 `minsky-init-one-command-bootstrap` shipping first (init flow). Composes with P0 `minsky-uninstall-one-command-with-stop` (the symmetric reverse path).

- [ ] `pre-push-hook-stays-fast` — the lefthook `pre-push` step must stay under ~10s wall-clock; today the fix landed (`--stage=fast`), the policy needs a deterministic CI lint so a future PR can't silently regress
  - **ID**: pre-push-hook-stays-fast
  - **Tags**: p1, milestone-m1, rule-10, rule-11, dx, ci, feedback-loop, observed-2026-05-20
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Surfaced-by**: operator 2026-05-20 — "Hmm why did pre push take so long? I expected push to happen in like a second". `pre-push` ran `pnpm pre-pr-lint --stage=full` which invokes vitest (~35s for the 3,135-test suite); total wall-clock ~40s. Fixed in the same PR by switching to `--stage=fast` (~5-7s). Without a CI lint guarding the fast-stage choice, the next PR that touches `lefthook.yml` could silently revert and re-introduce the 35s tax on every developer push.
  - **Hypothesis**: ship `scripts/check-pre-push-hook-fast.mjs` (rule-#10 deterministic lint) that asserts `lefthook.yml` `pre-push.commands.pre-pr-lint.run` ends with `--stage=fast` OR an explicitly-justified equivalent (`--stage=<custom-fast-subset>`). Hard-fail any PR whose lefthook.yml lacks the `--stage=fast` flag. Wire into the CI / `pre-pr-lint` stack so the lint runs on EVERY PR. Post-fix, the same operator complaint never recurs because the lint blocks the regression at PR time.
  - **Success**: (1) `node scripts/check-pre-push-hook-fast.mjs` exits 0 on the current `lefthook.yml`. (2) A synthetic test fixture where `pre-push.commands.pre-pr-lint.run` contains `--stage=full` makes the script exit 1 with `pre-push must use --stage=fast (current: --stage=full); see TASKS.md pre-push-hook-stays-fast`. (3) Paired vitest unit tests cover both shapes + the missing-`pre-push`-block case. (4) The lint is wired into `scripts/run-pre-pr-lint-stack.mjs` manifest so `pnpm pre-pr-lint --stage=fast` exercises it.
  - **Pivot**: if a future operator argues a slower pre-push is worth it (e.g., the gate-merge becomes unavailable on some platforms), gate the lint behind an `ALLOW_SLOW_PRE_PUSH=1` env var in `lefthook.yml` AND require a PR-body justification line `<!-- rule-10: pre-push-slow-justified: <reason> -->` (same shape as rule-3-doc-first's deferral marker). Don't loosen the default.
  - **Measurement**: `time git push origin <feature-branch>` on a clean tree should print ≤10s wall-clock (excluding network); empirically verified at 5-7s after this PR. `grep -c "stage=full" lefthook.yml` returns 0; the new script's CI invocation runs in <100ms.
  - **Anchor**: rule #10 (deterministic enforcement — `vision.md` § 10: every constitutional rule must be enforced by a deterministic CI check, not by "the agent will remember"); rule #11 (no flaky load-bearing gates — `vision.md` § "Constitutional rules" entry 11: pre-push is a load-bearing gate and a slow one is a flaky one in the operator-experience sense); Forsgren/Humble/Kim *Accelerate* 2018 (lead time + tight feedback loops are the most-correlated practice with high-performing teams).
  - **Details**: (a) Add `scripts/check-pre-push-hook-fast.mjs` — pure parser over `lefthook.yml` + assertion that `pre-push.commands.pre-pr-lint.run` contains the substring `--stage=fast`. YAML parse via `js-yaml` (already a dep) OR a hand-rolled regex over the `run:` line (single source of truth: the line above the `run:` MUST be the `pre-pr-lint:` key). (b) Paired tests in `scripts/check-pre-push-hook-fast.test.mjs` covering 5 shapes: current valid, `--stage=full` (rejected), missing `pre-push` block (rejected with actionable message), nested under wrong key (rejected), opt-out via `ALLOW_SLOW_PRE_PUSH=1` env var (accepted with one-line warning). (c) Wire into `scripts/run-pre-pr-lint-stack.mjs` STACK_MANIFEST so it runs in both `--stage=fast` and `--stage=full`. (d) CI parity row in `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (when GHA is re-enabled) AND in the consolidated `pre-pr-lint` job. (e) Document the policy in `vision.md` § Pattern conformance index with a new row.
  - **Files**: `scripts/check-pre-push-hook-fast.mjs` (new), `scripts/check-pre-push-hook-fast.test.mjs` (new), `scripts/run-pre-pr-lint-stack.mjs` (add to manifest), `vision.md` (pattern conformance index row), `lefthook.yml` (already fixed in this PR — the lint exists to lock it in).
  - **Touches**: `scripts/check-pre-push-hook-fast.mjs`, `scripts/check-pre-push-hook-fast.test.mjs`, `scripts/run-pre-pr-lint-stack.mjs`, `vision.md`
  - **Acceptance**: (1) `pnpm pre-pr-lint --stage=fast` runs the new lint and reports `[ok] pre-push-hook-stays-fast`. (2) A synthetic PR that reverts `lefthook.yml` to `--stage=full` is blocked by the lint with the actionable message above. (3) `time git push` on a feature branch with one trivial commit completes in ≤10s wall-clock on the operator's box.
  - **Risk**: low. One pure script + paired tests + manifest wiring + one pattern-conformance row. No code path changes — the lint adds a gate, doesn't change runtime behaviour.
  - **Note**: composes with `commit-hook-chain-node-version-and-platform-resilience` (the existing P0 about commit hooks breaking quietly) — both are feedback-loop hygiene tasks.

<!-- Observations filed 2026-05-18 from live daemon session (P1 findings). -->

- [ ] `graphql-repo-slug-mismatch-non-fatal` — `GraphQL: Could not resolve to a Repository` errors appear for repos that exist (`fyodoriv/minsky`) because the gh token context doesn't match; cosmetic but noisy — 5 errors per walk
  - **ID**: graphql-repo-slug-mismatch-non-fatal
  - **Tags**: p1, milestone-m1, reliability, observability, gh-auth
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-18 live daemon: 5 GraphQL errors across walks for `fyodoriv/minsky` and `fyodoriv/mirror-setup` — both repos exist (verified via `gh api graphql` from interactive shell). The launchd process resolves a different gh credential than the interactive shell (same root cause as existing P1 `orchestrator-gh-graphql-401-token-source-divergence`).
  - **Details**: the cross-repo runner calls `gh pr list` with GraphQL to check for duplicate PRs before spawning. When the launchd gh token doesn't have GraphQL access, this fails but is non-fatal (the runner continues). Fix: make the duplicate-PR check fall back to REST API when GraphQL fails, or suppress the error and proceed (the check is advisory, not blocking).
  - **Files**: `novel/cross-repo-runner/bin/minsky-run.mjs` (GraphQL call sites), paired tests
  - **Hypothesis**: after fallback to REST, 0 GraphQL errors per walk while duplicate-PR detection still works.
  - **Success**: 0 `GraphQL: Could not resolve` in daemon.log over 3 consecutive walks.
  - **Pivot**: if REST fallback is complex, suppress the error to a debug-level log (not stdout) and skip duplicate-PR detection when GraphQL is unavailable.
  - **Measurement**: `grep -c 'GraphQL.*Could not resolve' ~/.minsky/daemon.log` → 0 per walk (was: ~5).
  - **Anchor**: 2026-05-18 live daemon log. Composes with existing P1 `orchestrator-gh-graphql-401-token-source-divergence`.

- [ ] `daemon-log-lacks-iteration-detail` — daemon.log shows experiment-file writes but not the iteration verdict, duration, task picked, or agent used; diagnosing spawn-failed requires reading the jsonl experiment store separately
  - **ID**: daemon-log-lacks-iteration-detail
  - **Tags**: p1, milestone-m1, observability, logs, ux
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-18 live daemon: to determine that devin ran for 5.6min and produced a `validated` verdict, the operator had to `cat .minsky/experiment-store/cross-repo/*.jsonl` — the daemon.log only shows `✓ wrote experiment` and `✓ appended iteration record`, not the verdict or duration.
  - **Details**: after each iteration, the daemon should log a one-line summary: `iteration #N: task=<id> agent=devin verdict=validated duration=335s pr=null`. This is the same data already in the jsonl — just surface it in the daemon.log for glanceability.
  - **Files**: `novel/cross-repo-runner/bin/minsky-run.mjs` (add per-iteration summary log line)
  - **Hypothesis**: after adding the summary line, `grep 'iteration #' ~/.minsky/daemon.log` shows every iteration with its verdict, duration, and agent in one place.
  - **Success**: `tail -5 ~/.minsky/daemon.log` during a run shows the last iteration's verdict + duration without needing to read the experiment store.
  - **Pivot**: if the log format is too noisy, make the summary a `--verbose` option.
  - **Measurement**: `grep -c 'iteration #' ~/.minsky/daemon.log` matches the jsonl record count.
  - **Anchor**: 2026-05-18 live daemon. Card & Mackinlay 1999 (glanceable — the operator should see iteration health without digging).

- [ ] `observer-launchd-exits-on-nonzero` — the observer-watch.sh script exits immediately when any minsky command returns non-zero (stale PID, already running) despite removing `set -e`; the launchd agent dies after one failed restart attempt
  - **ID**: observer-launchd-exits-on-nonzero
  - **Tags**: p1, milestone-m1, observer, reliability, launchd
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-18 live session: observer launchd agent started, detected daemon down, tried to restart, `minsky --daemon` returned "daemon already running (PID XXXX)" (stale PID), and the observer exited despite the `set -uo pipefail` (no -e) fix. Root cause: launchd captures the exit code and `pipefail` causes the pipe chain to propagate non-zero from `minsky --daemon`.
  - **Details**: the observer script needs to be fully defensive: every minsky command should be wrapped with `|| true` or explicit error handling. Additionally, the `KeepAlive` key in the launchd plist should be set to `true` so launchd restarts the observer if it exits unexpectedly.
  - **Files**: `scripts/observer-watch.sh` (defensive error handling), `~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.minsky.observer.plist` (add KeepAlive)
  - **Hypothesis**: after making the observer fully defensive, it survives all minsky command failures and runs for the full 4h watch period.
  - **Success**: observer runs for ≥1h without exiting unexpectedly; `launchctl list | grep minsky.observer` shows the service active for the full period.
  - **Pivot**: if bash is too fragile for the observer, rewrite it as a node.js script that inherits minsky's error-handling patterns.
  - **Measurement**: `wc -l /tmp/minsky-observer.log` ≥ 60 lines (one per minute for 1h) after the fix.
  - **Anchor**: 2026-05-18 live session. Armstrong 2007 (let-it-crash at the right boundary — the observer must NOT crash on expected non-zero exits from the system it watches).

<!-- Moved from P0 2026-05-18: M2 tasks deprioritized until M1 ships (see MILESTONES.md). -->
- [ ] `native-agent-teams-with-tiered-adapter` — minsky drives Claude Code's native "agent teams" (lead + independent teammates + shared task list + mailbox + hooks) as its multi-worker backend when available, behind a capability-tiered agent adapter that still supports non-native agents (Devin, etc.) via the existing process-fan-out — native always preferred
  - **ID**: native-agent-teams-with-tiered-adapter
  - **Tags**: p0, operator-directive, orchestrator, agent-teams, claude-code, adapter, rule-1, rule-2, rule-9
  - **Milestone**: M2
  - **Touches**: `novel/tick-loop/src/**`, `novel/tick-loop/bin/tick-loop.mjs`, `novel/tick-loop/README.md`, `scripts/orchestrate.mjs`, `docs/agent-teams-integration.md`
  - **Competitive-goal**: replacing minsky's hand-rolled `claude --print` fan-out + TASKS.md polling with the native, file-locked, mailbox-coordinated agent-teams primitive raises autonomous-merge throughput and lowers coordination overhead / duplicate-work rate on the `self-metrics-competitive-benchmark` scorecard; the tiered adapter is the moat — minsky orchestrates ANY agent (native-optimal for Claude Code, still-functional for Devin/others) which no single-vendor competitor can claim.
  - **Surfaced-by**: operator directive 2026-05-17 (verbatim in the comment above) + the agent-teams doc as of 2026-05-17. Minsky today hand-rolls exactly what agent-teams provides natively: parallel independent Claude instances (the `claude --print` worker fan-out), a shared task list (TASKS.md + `.minsky/orchestrate.jsonl`), task claiming (the `(@agent-id)` claim convention), and a quality gate (the pre-PR/gate-merge step). Agent-teams supersedes the bespoke layer with a supported primitive.
  - **Details**: deliver in slices, each its own gate-green PR. (a) **Capability detection** — a pure `detectAgentTeamsSupport()` that checks: running agent is Claude Code, `claude --version` ≥ `2.1.32`, and `CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS` enabled (set it in the spawned env / `settings.json` `env` when minsky owns the launch). Returns a capability tier matching the operator's 2026-05-17 feature table: `native-agent-teams` (experimental; 3–5 teammates; shared task list + inter-agent messaging + lifecycle hooks + built-in file-locked task claiming — the *coordinated* tier, closest to minsky) > `native-agent-view` (the `claude --bg` / agent-view fan-out; 10+ isolated sessions, auto git-worktree per session, monitoring-only, fire-and-forget — the *high-parallelism / swarm* tier) > `native-subagents` > `process-fan-out` (the current hand-rolled `tick-loop` worker fan-out — the non-native fallback this P0 shrinks per rule #14). (b) **Agent adapter seam** (rule #2 Strategy boundary) — `AgentTeamBackend` interface with methods `createTeam`, `spawnTeammate(role, prompt, model)`, `assignTask`, `claimTask`, `message(to, body)`, `onTeammateIdle/onTaskCompleted`, `shutdownTeammate`, `cleanupTeam`. Three implementations: `ClaudeAgentTeamsBackend` (drives the native coordinated primitive — lead = the minsky orchestrator session, teammates = workers; reads team state from `~/.claude/teams/{team}/config.json` `members[]` and the task list under `~/.claude/tasks/{team}/`; never hand-edits the auto-managed config), `ClaudeAgentViewBackend` (drives `claude --bg` / agent-view: spawns N isolated sessions, each in its own Claude-Code-managed git worktree, monitors them, no inter-agent messaging — the native equivalent of minsky's current process-fan-out and the right substrate for swarm/high-parallelism mode; delegates worktree + session lifecycle to Claude Code so minsky deletes its bespoke worktree manager — rule #14 scope-shrink), and `ProcessFanoutBackend` (the existing `tick-loop` worker spawn, retained only for Devin/aider/opencode/non-native agents). (c) **TASKS.md ⇄ native task-list bridge** — minsky's TASKS.md remains the source of truth; a bidirectional sync maps P0/P1 items to native agent-teams tasks (respecting dependencies + the file-lock claim semantics) so existing scout/priority discipline is preserved while teammates self-claim natively. (d) **Gate as native hooks** — wire minsky's pre-PR/lint/gate as `TaskCompleted` + `TeammateIdle` hooks (exit 2 to block + feed back) so the existing rule-#10 gate enforces on the native path with zero new gate code. (e) **Selection policy** — the orchestrator picks the highest tier for the running agent AND the workload: coordinated/research/cross-layer work on Claude Code → `native-agent-teams`; **swarm / high-parallelism mode** (operator wants ≥10 parallel, burn-OK) on Claude Code → `native-agent-view` (native worktree-isolated `--bg` fan-out); subagent-only agents → `native-subagents`; Devin / aider / opencode / unknown → `process-fan-out`. A `MINSKY_SWARM_MODE`/`--swarm` operator switch forces the high-parallelism tier (native-agent-view when on Claude Code, else process-fan-out) with a configurable agent count; pin/override env forces any tier for debugging. (g) **Swarm-mode operational continuity** — until `ClaudeAgentViewBackend` lands, swarm mode is delivered by the existing `process-fan-out` at high `--spawn-additional-workers` (operationally proven 2026-05-17: 20 Claude-Opus workers via `com.minsky.opus-sonnet-run --spawn-additional-workers=19`, `MINSKY_PRE_PR_STAGE=fast`). This P0 is therefore the *native upgrade of an already-working capability* (rule #14: replace the hand-rolled fan-out + bespoke worktree manager with `claude --bg`/agent-view), not a new capability — selection must keep the process-fan-out swarm working with zero regression during the migration. (f) **Display/runtime** — default `teammateMode: in-process` for headless/launchd runs (split-pane needs tmux/iTerm2, unavailable under launchd); document the `--teammate-mode in-process` invariant for daemonized minsky. New code = detection + the adapter + the TASKS.md bridge + hook wiring + selection; the parallel-work, messaging, and task-claim mechanics are delegated to the native primitive (rule #1 — do not reinvent what Claude Code now ships). Honor agent-teams limitations: one team per lead, no nested teams, no session-resume of in-process teammates (the orchestrator's existing restart/backoff covers respawn — compose `[[runany-self-restart-bounded-timelimit]]`).
  - **Files**: `novel/tick-loop/src/agent-team-backend.ts` (new — the `AgentTeamBackend` interface + tier enum, pure), `novel/tick-loop/src/claude-agent-teams-backend.ts` (new — native impl + paired tests with an injected exec/fs seam for `claude --version`, team config, task dir), `novel/tick-loop/src/process-fanout-backend.ts` (new — wraps the existing worker spawn for non-native agents), `novel/tick-loop/src/detect-agent-teams-support.ts` (new + paired tests: version-gate, env-gate, agent-identity), `novel/tick-loop/src/tasks-md-team-bridge.ts` (new + tests — TASKS.md ⇄ `~/.claude/tasks/{team}/` sync honoring deps + claim locks), `novel/tick-loop/bin/tick-loop.mjs` + `scripts/orchestrate.mjs` (select tier, drive the chosen backend, wire the gate as `TaskCompleted`/`TeammateIdle` hooks), `novel/tick-loop/src/index.ts` (re-exports), `docs/agent-teams-integration.md` (new — tier table, the in-process-under-launchd invariant, the Devin/non-native fallback), `novel/tick-loop/README.md` (section).
  - **Hypothesis**: today minsky's bespoke fan-out duplicates ~all of agent-teams' coordination (independent contexts, shared task list, claiming, messaging) with weaker primitives (no file-locked claim → duplicate-work P0 exists; no mailbox → no teammate cross-talk; no native idle/complete hooks). After this, on Claude Code minsky runs a real agent team with file-locked claims + mailbox + hook-enforced gates → measurable drop in duplicate-work rate and coordination latency; non-native agents (Devin) keep working unchanged via the fan-out tier, so capability coverage is strictly ≥ today.
  - **Success**: on Claude Code ≥2.1.32 with the env enabled, `minsky` (orchestrator) creates a native team, spawns ≥3 teammates that self-claim TASKS.md-bridged tasks with zero duplicate claims over a 1h run (vs the baseline duplicate-work incidence), the minsky gate blocks a deliberately-bad `TaskCompleted` via the hook, and cleanup leaves no orphaned team/tmux; on a forced non-native agent (Devin/aider profile) the same run completes via `process-fan-out` with identical TASKS.md outcomes (tier-fallback proven); selection always picks the highest tier the running agent supports.
  - **Pivot**: if the experimental agent-teams primitive is too unstable for an unattended 10h daemon (task-status lag / shutdown slowness / no in-process resume per its documented limitations), gate the native tier behind a stability flag and default the daemon to `process-fan-out` while keeping `native-agent-teams` for attended runs — never regress non-native support, never block on the experimental feature; keep the adapter so flipping the default is one config change when the feature stabilizes.
  - **Measurement**: `node scripts/bench-coordination.mjs` (new or extend the competitive harness) over a fixed task set on each tier → {duplicate-claim count, mean task-claim latency, gate-block efficacy, run wall-clock}; assert native tier has 0 duplicate claims (file-lock) vs the fan-out baseline; a contract test asserts `detectAgentTeamsSupport()` returns the right tier for {Claude≥2.1.32+env, Claude<2.1.32, env-off, Devin, aider}; an integration test drives a 3-teammate native team end-to-end against a fixture repo and asserts a hook-blocked bad completion.
  - **Anchor**: operator directive 2026-05-17 (verbatim above; "prefer native solutions always when possible"); Claude Code agent-teams doc 2026-05-17 (config at `~/.claude/teams/{team-name}/config.json`, tasks at `~/.claude/tasks/{team-name}/`, `CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS`, `teammateMode`, `--teammate-mode`, `TeammateIdle`/`TaskCreated`/`TaskCompleted` hooks, subagent-definitions-as-teammates, documented limitations); vision.md rule #1 (compose/don't reinvent — agent-teams is the upstream primitive for minsky's hand-rolled layer), rule #2 (Strategy boundary — the `AgentTeamBackend` adapter is pure, the exec/fs is the I/O), rule #9 (pre-registered HDD). Composes [[runany-self-restart-bounded-timelimit]] (teammate respawn on the no-resume limitation), [[daemon-duplicate-work-detection]] (native file-locked claiming subsumes it on the native tier), [[runany-dynamic-model-or-local-fallback]] + [[runtime-token-limit-auto-pivot-local-and-back]] (per-teammate model/provider selection flows through the adapter), and [[self-metrics-competitive-benchmark]] (the coordination metrics feed the scorecard).
  - **Slice (c) status 2026-05-23**: TASKS.md ⇄ native task-list bridge is **deferred** per the costed analysis in `docs/research/claude-native-task-standard.md` (the deliverable of `investigate-claude-native-task-standard`). Native task standard is experimental + session-restart-wipes-state (GH #33764) + `-p`-mode-incompatible (GH #20424). Slice (c) unblocks when `revisit-claude-native-task-standard-when-persistence-fixed` (P2 conditional, filed 2026-05-23) re-evaluates positively. Remaining slices (a) capability detection, (b) adapter seam, (d) hooks-as-gate, (e) selection — unaffected and shippable. Slice (b)'s `ClaudeAgentViewBackend` (`claude --bg`) is the native tier that's actually shippable today since it doesn't depend on the experimental task-list contract.
  - **Slice 2 shipped 2026-05-23**: `novel/tick-loop/src/log-detected-tier.ts` (pure formatter exporting `formatTierLogLine` + `TIER_LOG_PREFIX`) + 6 sibling tests + wired into `bin/tick-loop.mjs` at startup so the detected tier shows up in `daemon.log` / `tick-loop.out.log` keyed by `tick-loop: agent-team-tier=<tier>`. This connects slice 1's `detectAgentTeamsSupport` (which was dead code) to operator-visible output. Honesty boundary: slice 2 only LOGS the tier; it does NOT branch on it. The bin passes `claudeVersion: null` deliberately — the `claude --version` probe is a let-it-crash hazard at boot and lands in slice 2.5 (probe + cache). Selection policy and real backends land in later slices.
  - **Slice 2.5 shipped 2026-05-23**: `novel/tick-loop/src/probe-claude-version.ts` (bounded async probe — default 2s timeout, fail-soft on every error path) + 12 sibling tests + wired into `bin/tick-loop.mjs` at startup so the detector now receives the real `claude --version` output instead of `null`. End-to-end verified on operator's machine: `MINSKY_CLOUD_AGENT=claude` + `CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1` now promotes the tier from `native-subagents` to `native-agent-teams` (claude 2.1.138 >= 2.1.32 OK). Without the env flag, stays at `native-subagents` (the documented safe fallback). Every failure path (missing binary / timeout / ENOENT / non-zero exit) returns null and degrades to slice-2 behaviour without crashing the supervisor — rule #6 boundary stays at the runtime-spawn layer, not the version-probe layer.

- [ ] `daemon-cross-iteration-prompt-cache` — share `claude --print` prompt prefix across iterations to hit Anthropic's prompt cache
  - **ID**: daemon-cross-iteration-prompt-cache
  - **Tags**: p1, daemon, supervisor, throughput, prompt-cache, optimization, follow-up
  - **Milestone**: M2
  - **Touches**: `novel/tick-loop/src/daemon.ts`, `novel/tick-loop/bin/tick-loop.mjs`, `docs/prompt-cache-discipline.md`, `scripts/measure-prompt-cache-hit-rate.mjs`
  - **Estimate**: 1w
  - **Hypothesis**: Discovered via 2026-05-05 speed-comparison: operator's foreground Claude session ships PRs in median ~4 min vs daemon's ~23 min — a 6× gap. Major contributor: the daemon spawns a fresh `claude --print` per iteration, so Anthropic's prompt cache (5min TTL, ≥1024 tokens) never hits. The operator's continuous session benefits from prompt-cache reads at 1/10th the cost of fresh tokens (per Anthropic's Sept 2025 caching docs). Pre-registration: with cross-iteration prompt-prefix sharing live (e.g., daemon emits the brief with cache-control breakpoints at the policies, glossary, and pattern-index sections; subsequent iterations within the 5min TTL window reuse the cache), per-iteration token cost drops by ≥40% AND/OR per-iteration wall-time drops by ≥20%. If neither moves, prompt cache isn't the bottleneck — pivot to investigating the tool-call thrash hypothesis (operator-driven session vs daemon's spawn issues different number of tool calls per task).
  - **Details**: (a) Audit `buildDaemonBrief` output for cache-friendliness — order content from most-stable (rule citations, glossary anchors, file-level policies) at the top to most-volatile (current task block, today's iteration log) at the bottom. Anthropic caches the longest-common-prefix; volatility breaks the chain. (b) Add `cache_control: { type: "ephemeral" }` annotations at the policy/glossary/pattern-index boundaries when constructing the API call. (c) Wire daemon to use the Anthropic SDK directly (not `claude --print`) so it can set `cache_control` headers — OR document why `claude --print` already supports this (likely yes via system prompt caching). (d) Measure baseline iteration cost via the new `tick-loop.iteration.tokens_in_delta` span attribute (filed as slice 2 of `daemon-self-optimize-speed-tokens`); ship the cache change; remeasure. (e) Note: prompt cache is per-session — within a 5min TTL window, multiple spawns hit the same cache. Daemon's tick interval is 5min by default, so rapid-fire iterations would benefit; the pivot is the tick interval (already shipping in CI today via the watchdog).
  - **Files**: `novel/tick-loop/src/daemon.ts` (re-order `buildDaemonBrief` for cache-friendliness), `novel/tick-loop/bin/tick-loop.mjs` (if SDK swap needed; investigate first), `docs/prompt-cache-discipline.md` (the operator-facing explanation), follow-up: `scripts/measure-prompt-cache-hit-rate.mjs` (rolling-7d cache-hit telemetry).
  - **Verification**: replay 24h of daemon iterations against an Anthropic-API-stub that records prompt-cache attempts; assert ≥80% of iterations within a 5min window land cache reads (vs 0% baseline).
  - **Measurement**: `tick-loop.iteration.tokens_in_delta` rolling-7d median drops by ≥40% post-merge (baseline measured at substrate-merge time of `daemon-self-optimize-speed-tokens`'s slice 2). AND `tick-loop.iteration.duration_ms` rolling-7d median drops by ≥20%.
  - **Pivot**: if `claude --print` doesn't expose `cache_control` and the SDK swap is too invasive, pivot to "shrink the brief instead" — apply the `optimization-discipline gate` (#207) recursively for 4 weeks targeting the brief itself; if that gets the duration delta but not the token delta, retire the cache approach with a documented "stuck behind CLI tooling — revisit on Anthropic SDK upgrade".
  - **Acceptance**: (1) brief content re-ordered for cache-friendliness; (2) cache hit rate ≥80% within 5min windows (measured via the SDK-level cache_creation_input_tokens vs cache_read_input_tokens fields); (3) per-iteration token cost ≥40% lower; (4) per-iteration wall-time ≥20% lower; (5) `docs/prompt-cache-discipline.md` documents the contract.
  - **Anchor**: 2026-05-05 operator-vs-daemon speed measurement (6× median gap); Anthropic prompt-caching documentation (Sept 2025; 1024-token min, 5min TTL, 90% cache-read discount); rule #1 (don't reinvent — Anthropic ships the cache, we use it); rule #2 (Strategy seam — `claude --print` is the seam; SDK swap is a one-file change at `bin/tick-loop.mjs`); rule #9 (pre-registered HDD); composes with `daemon-self-optimize-speed-tokens` (offensive — compress brief), this task (offensive — cache the prefix).
  - **Risk**: medium. Requires either a documented `claude --print` cache-control surface OR an SDK-level swap. Mitigation: investigate first (slice 0 — research, 1d); only commit to implementation if `claude --print` already supports it.
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-05 operator directive — "Measure speed of implementing a minsky feature here in claude code vs implementing it through minsky itself. Open tasks to improve it." Speed-comparison showed daemon at 6× operator's median open→merge; prompt cache miss is the leading single contributor (per Anthropic's 90% cache-read discount math).

- [ ] `daemon-parallel-worktree-launch` — launch N non-conflicting Minsky processes on the same repo via Claude Code's native `--worktree` flag + `flock`-based TASKS.md row claim
  - **ID**: daemon-parallel-worktree-launch
  - **Tags**: p0, daemon, supervisor, throughput, parallel, worktree, follow-up
  - **Milestone**: M2
  - **Touches**: `novel/tick-loop/**`, `scripts/check-tasks-md-touches-glob.mjs`, `.gitattributes`, `pnpm-workspace.yaml`
  - **Pick-next**: yes — operator-promoted 2026-05-05 ("Add p0 to implement parallel launch of multiple non conflicting minsky processes on the same repo").
  - **Estimate**: 1w (substrate slice in 1d; the rest ratchets)
  - **Hypothesis**: The daemon currently runs serially — one tick = one `claude --print` spawn. Operator throughput is bounded by p95 single-iteration wall-time × tasks-in-queue. With N parallel workers each owning its own git worktree + `flock`-claimed TASKS.md row, throughput multiplies (up to the API rate-limit ceiling). 2026 research surveyed 14+ existing tools; Claude Code's native `--worktree` flag (Cherny, Feb 2026) provides the per-process isolation primitive for free. Pre-registration: post-implementation, with N=3 workers, daily merged-PR count ≥ 1.5× the serial-baseline measured over a 7-day window. If <1.2×, parallel coordination overhead dominates — pivot to N=2 OR retire to serial-only with documented reason. **Decision: build-on-top-of Claude Code `--worktree` + thin `flock` claim layer.** Reject Conductor / Crystal / Vibe Kanban (GUI-first); reject Composio agent-orchestrator (would replace Minsky's own loop); reject Redis/etcd locks (single-host overkill per Kleppmann 2016).
  - **Details**: (a) Substrate slice — `novel/tick-loop/src/worker-claim.ts` exposes `acquireTaskClaim(taskId, opts): { release, expiresAt }` using `flock(2)` on `.minsky/locks/task-<id>.lock` with `LOCK_NB`; lease TTL written into the lock body so external operators can see who holds what. (b) Slice 2 — `novel/tick-loop/bin/tick-loop.mjs` learns `--worker-id <n>` and `--workers-total <N>`; per-worker spawn invokes `claude --worktree daemon-<worker-id>-<task-id>` (Anthropic's flag, auto-cleanup on session end). (c) Slice 3 — TASKS.md task-row `**Touches**: <glob>[, <glob>…]` field + pre-spawn glob-overlap check against open daemon PRs (`gh pr list --json files`). Refuse to start the worker if any open PR's files overlap any `Touches:` glob of the candidate task. (d) Slice 4 — sweeper: every tick, run `git worktree prune --expire 1.day.ago`, unlink `.git/index.lock` files older than 5 minutes (the Claude Code #11005 mitigation), expire stale TTL claims. (e) Slice 5 — register Mergiraf as `merge=mergiraf` in `.gitattributes` so cross-worker rebase storms self-resolve on overlapping files (33 languages, tree-sitter-backed). (f) Slice 6 — `pnpm-workspace.yaml` adds `enableGlobalVirtualStore: true` so per-worktree `node_modules` doesn't blow disk. (g) Slice 7 — observability: per-worker `tick-loop.worker.<id>.iteration` spans; new `claim_collision`, `stale_lock_recovered`, `worktree_orphan_pruned` counters in dashboard. Document the failure modes inventory (see Research notes below) in `novel/tick-loop/PARALLEL_WORKTREE.md`.
  - **Files**: `novel/tick-loop/src/worker-claim.ts` + `.test.ts`, `novel/tick-loop/src/worker-claim.flock-helper.mjs` (thin native-flock wrapper or `proper-lockfile` adoption), `novel/tick-loop/bin/tick-loop.mjs` (`--worker-id` / `--workers-total` flags + per-worker dispatch), `novel/tick-loop/src/daemon.ts` (pre-spawn `gh pr list` glob-overlap check), `scripts/check-tasks-md-touches-glob.mjs` (lint that every task block carries a `**Touches**:` field), `.gitattributes` (mergiraf driver), `pnpm-workspace.yaml` (`enableGlobalVirtualStore`), `novel/tick-loop/PARALLEL_WORKTREE.md` (failure-modes doc), `novel/dashboard-web/src/parallel-workers.ts` + tests (per-worker tile).
  - **Verification**: launch 3 workers on a 5-task TASKS.md; assert each worker picks a different task within ≤500ms via `flock` (no two workers can hold the same `.minsky/locks/task-<id>.lock`). Then crash one worker mid-iteration; assert the other 2 finish, and the next tick reclaims the abandoned task without manual intervention. Replay the 2026-05-05 #198/#199 collision pattern: with the pre-spawn glob-overlap check live, the second worker's spawn refuses with `collision-prevented: PR #198 already touches files X,Y`.
  - **Measurement**: `node scripts/parallel-worker-throughput.mjs --window=7d --workers=3` returns ≥1.5× the serial-baseline merged-PR/day rate. Concrete: baseline serial daemon (today) = ~10 PRs/day; target with N=3 ≥ 15 PRs/day. AND `grep -c 'collision-prevented:' .minsky/tick-loop.out.log` is ≥1 the first time two workers pick overlapping tasks (proves the pre-spawn check fires). AND the rolling-7d ghost-lock recovery counter is non-zero (proves the sweeper found and recovered at least one stale `.git/index.lock`).
  - **Pivot**: if N=3 produces <1.2× serial-baseline, pivot to N=2 — the API rate-limit (per Anthropic's published 2-4 concurrent ceiling) is the real bottleneck. If `flock`-based TTL leases prove too coarse (workers crash and steal each other's tasks under 30m), switch to `fcntl(F_SETLK)` byte-range OFD locks (POSIX, finer granularity) before retiring. If Mergiraf produces ≥3 wrong auto-merges/week, demote it to advisory-only and re-add a human/CI gate at the merge step. **Don't retire parallelism** — even N=2 is a strict improvement; the floor is "at least one extra worker available for `daemon-fix-own-pr-on-ci-failure` while the primary keeps shipping".
  - **Acceptance**: (1) `acquireTaskClaim` ships with paired tests covering happy-path / crash-recovery / stale-TTL-eviction; (2) `--worker-id`/`--workers-total` CLI flags route correctly; (3) `**Touches**: <glob>` field lints clean across TASKS.md (CI gate); (4) pre-spawn glob-overlap check refuses with `collision-prevented:` reason when an open daemon PR overlaps any `Touches:` glob; (5) `git worktree prune` + stale `.git/index.lock` sweeper run every tick; (6) Mergiraf registered as `.gitattributes` driver; (7) `pnpm-workspace.yaml` enables `enableGlobalVirtualStore`; (8) `novel/tick-loop/PARALLEL_WORKTREE.md` documents the 12 known pitfalls (pre-commit hooks per worktree, submodules, ghost locks, etc.); (9) parallel-worker dashboard tile ships; (10) launching 3 workers shows zero claim collisions across 100 iterations in CI.
  - **Anchor**: operator 2026-05-05 — "Add p0 to implement parallel launch of multiple non conflicting minsky processes on the same repo. I assume we need bulletproof git worktrees solution. Extensively research existing solutions and try adopting them"; rule #1 (don't reinvent the wheel — adopt Claude Code's native `--worktree`); rule #2 (Strategy seam — claim layer fed by injected I/O); rule #9 (pre-registered HDD); Kleppmann 2016 ("How to do distributed locking" — single-host `flock` is sufficient); Beyer SRE 2016 Ch. 6 (silence-is-failure for stale locks).
  - **Risk**: medium. Git is fundamentally a single-process tool — concurrent operations across worktrees can corrupt `.git/`. Mitigations: serialize `git fetch` via `flock .git/fetch.lock`; sweeper recovers ghost `.git/index.lock` (Claude Code #11005); per-worker branch namespace `daemon/<worker-id>/<task-id>` makes branch collisions impossible; Mergiraf reduces post-merge rebase pain. Pre-commit hooks-per-worktree (issue #808) and submodule-in-worktree (issue #3032) are real but rare in this repo's surface.
  - **Research** (2026-05-05): full research report in branch `feat/file-daemon-parallel-worktree-launch` git history; key findings: (i) Claude Code's `--worktree` flag (Cherny, Feb 2026) is the per-process isolation primitive — auto-cleanup on session end. (ii) `flock(2)` on a per-task lockfile is the right coordination primitive for single-host (per Kleppmann). (iii) Mergiraf (mergiraf.org) is a tree-sitter-backed semantic merge driver — 33 languages, 428/7415 kernel conflicts auto-resolved in published benchmarks. (iv) pnpm's `enableGlobalVirtualStore` cuts per-worktree `node_modules` from ~2GB to ~50MB. (v) Known sharp edges: pre-commit hooks install per-worktree (`pre-commit/pre-commit#808`), submodules + worktrees + pre-commit corrupts the submodule repo (`pre-commit/pre-commit#3032`), Claude Code background ops leak `.git/index.lock` (`anthropics/claude-code#11005`). (vi) Rejected: Conductor / Crystal / Nimbalyst / Vibe Kanban / Superset (GUI-first); Composio agent-orchestrator (would replace Minsky's own loop); dagger/container-use (Docker dep we don't need); Redis/etcd (overkill); CRDTs (overkill — `flock`+fsync gives strict serializability free); git-spice/git-branchless (different problem — solves stacked-PR rebase, can be added later). (vii) Anchors: [Cherny worktree announcement](https://www.threads.com/@boris_cherny/post/DVAAnexgRUj/), [Claude Code subagents docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents), [dagger/container-use](https://github.com/dagger/container-use), [Mergiraf](https://mergiraf.org/), [pnpm git-worktrees guide](https://pnpm.io/next/git-worktrees), [git-worktree(1)](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-worktree), [Kleppmann distributed locking](https://martin.kleppmann.com/2016/02/08/how-to-do-distributed-locking.html).
  - **Open-questions** (must be answered in slice-1 implementation iteration): (a) lease TTL value — what's p99 tick duration in current `tick-loop.out.log`? Need histogram. (b) worker count per host — published wisdom says 2–4 concurrent Claude sessions before rate-limits dominate; does Minsky's API class admit more? (c) `Touches:` spec — extend the markdown task-block (low-friction) or add a sidecar `tasks.lock.json` (machine-clean)? (d) Mergiraf trust level — auto-resolve silently, or always require human/CI gate? (e) telemetry — does the existing observability surface emit `claim_collision` / `stale_lock_recovered` / `worktree_orphan_pruned` counters? Without those, no measurable hypothesis (rule #9).
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-05 operator directive — "Add p0 to implement parallel launch of multiple non conflicting minsky processes on the same repo. I assume we need bulletproof git worktrees solution. Extensively research existing solutions and try adopting them. Do more research".
  - **Composes-into**: parent task `claude-orchestrator-local-worker-fanout` (P0, below) — this block provides the worker-spawn substrate (`flock` claim, `claude --worktree`, glob-overlap check). The parent adds role-pin + orchestrator entrypoint + cross-layer chaos.

- [ ] `claude-orchestrator-local-worker-fanout` — Claude (top layer, brain) fans work out to N local-model workers in git worktrees; chaos-engineered cross-layer stability
  - **ID**: claude-orchestrator-local-worker-fanout
  - **Tags**: p0, operator-directive, orchestrator, parallel, worktree, chaos-engineering, role-separation
  - **Milestone**: M2
  - **Operator-refinement (2026-05-16, supersedes the local-worker default below)**: workers are now **Claude Sonnet (`claude-sonnet-4-6`)**, not local-Qwen — local 30B was too slow + context-overflow-prone for a 24h sprint (15-min watchdog timeouts; aider context-window crashes). Architecture the operator wants: **Opus = orchestrator/brain** (queue decomposition, code-review of worker output, vet+merge decisions), **Sonnet = worker pool** (fast implementation), operator/Opus-session monitors and improves continuously. Until Slice 1 (role-pin) + Slice 2 (`minsky orchestrate --workers=N` entrypoint) ship, the orchestrator runs from the operator's Claude Code session (a documented valid mode per Open-questions (a)); the durable goal is the autonomous spawned-orchestrator. Re-grade the Hypothesis cells with worker-provider=`claude-sonnet-4-6` (not local-Qwen): cell (b) becomes "worker iterations consume ≥80 % on Sonnet"; cell (a) orchestrator-Opus-monopoly still holds. Slice 2's per-worker spawn = `--worker-id <i> --workers-total <N> --role=worker` with `MINSKY_LLM_PROVIDER=claude-only MINSKY_STRATEGIC_PIN_MODEL=claude-sonnet-4-6` (NOT `pnpm minsky`, which overrides the provider — see #budget-guard-correctness Slice C). This is the highest-priority P0; do it first.
  - **Touches**: `novel/tick-loop/**`, `novel/sidecar-bootstrap/**`, `scripts/self-diagnose.mjs`, `scripts/chaos-*.mjs`, `docs/orchestrator-fanout.md`
  - **Progress (2026-05-16)**: the orchestrator shipped as a **decomposed two-launchd-agent realization** (more robust than a monolith — separately supervised, let-it-crash): (1) `com.minsky.opus-sonnet-run` = the Sonnet worker pool (`MINSKY_LLM_PROVIDER=claude-only` + Sonnet pin, tick-loop direct); (2) `com.minsky.orchestrator` = the conductor (`scripts/orchestrate.mjs`, #584) — self-scheduling 20-min loop: self-heal the worker daemon + Opus-review-gated merge authority (`scripts/local-gate-merge.mjs` #581/#582 + Opus-review brain #583 — deterministic `--stage=full` gate THEN Opus reviews intent/risk; only gate-green AND Opus-approved PRs `gh pr merge --admin`). Both KeepAlive (survive crash + operator session); $0 infra (Opus on the Claude subscription; GitHub Actions is disabled so the local gate replaces CI); ledger `.minsky/orchestrate.jsonl`. Slice-C `claude-only` honored (#579). `--once` runtime-validated 2026-05-16T21:58Z (clean tick; correctly skipped 4 non-gate-green PRs, no false-merge). Remaining for full task: explicit `--workers=N` fan-out + assignment seam + cross-layer chaos invariants (Slices 3-5) — workers' job now that the loop is autonomous; and raise worker PR gate-pass rate (most Sonnet PRs currently fail `--stage=full` vs main).
  - **Pick-next**: yes — operator 2026-05-10, "I want claude to be the brains of this (top layer) and everything below to be run by local models but with full list of features of claude … extremely stable about this, with chaos engineering and other minsky principles".
  - **Estimate**: 4d (composes three existing blocks; net new code is the role-pin + entrypoint + cross-layer chaos invariants)
  - **Composes**: this block is the parent that wires three already-filed substrates plus one already-shipped layer into one coherent operator surface:
    - parent of `daemon-parallel-worktree-launch` (P0, line 426) — provides `flock` claim, `claude --worktree`, glob-overlap check, mergiraf, sweeper. Worker-spawn shape lives there.
    - parent of `task-aware-model-router` (P1, line 544) — provides the 4-bucket classifier + provider router. This task adds the **role-pin override** (orchestrator iterations always Opus; worker iterations always local-first under `MINSKY_LLM_PROVIDER=local-preferred`).
    - parent of `spawn-n-workers-with-watchdog-monitoring` (P2, line 717) — provides the watchdog substrate (kill stuck child, rebase stuck PR, body-patch). This task adds the **cross-layer** chaos slice (orchestrator-death-recovery; worker-hang-detection-from-orchestrator-side).
    - builds on `llm-provider-selector` (`novel/tick-loop/src/llm-provider-selector.ts`, shipped via #358/#365/#401) — the existing `decideProvider` is the per-process gate; this task threads the role into its input.
  - **Hypothesis**: Operator 2026-05-10 wants the two-layer architecture: (i) **orchestrator** runs from a Claude Code session (Opus reasoning over the queue, decomposition, code-review of worker output, merge decisions); (ii) **workers** are local-Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B via aider in `git worktree`s, one per claimed task. Today every iteration calls `decideProvider` with the same inputs, so a worker iteration that exits the budget-paused band routes back to Claude — burning the cap that should belong to the orchestrator. Pre-registration: post-fix, with N=4 workers + 1 orchestrator running for a 24h window, **(a)** orchestrator process consumes ≥90 % of the Claude tokens spent in the window (the 5h-cap envelope is defended for the brain), **(b)** worker processes consume ≥80 % of their iterations on local-Qwen (provider attribute on `tick-loop.iteration` spans), **(c)** sustained merged-PR throughput ≥ 1.5× the serial-baseline (same ratchet as #daemon-parallel-worktree-launch), **(d)** zero operator-manual-intervention events triggered by cross-layer failure (orchestrator crash mid-fanout, worker hang during harvest). If (a) <70 %, the role-pin is leaking — workers are stealing Claude budget; pivot the router to a hard env-gated check rather than a soft preference. If (b) <50 %, local-Qwen is failing too often on the worker bucket distribution; pivot worker default to Sonnet/Haiku-cheap-cloud rather than local. If (c) <1.2×, parallel coordination dominates; pivot to N=2. If (d) ≥1/day sustained 7 days, the chaos invariants are insufficient; pivot to per-worker process-supervisor (launchd KeepAlive per worker) before adding more N.
  - **Details** (slices ratchet; each slice is one PR with paired tests + rule-#9 self-grade):
    - **Slice 1 — role-pinned provider input** (1d): extend `decideProvider`'s input shape with `role: "orchestrator" | "worker"` and a per-role default-provider table. Orchestrator role: hard-pinned to `claude-opus` regardless of budget state, with the existing circuit-break-and-notify path being the *only* exception (when Opus is genuinely unavailable, the orchestrator pauses rather than degrading — degraded brains across an N-worker fanout is worse than waiting). Worker role: defaults to local-Qwen if `MINSKY_LLM_PROVIDER=local-preferred` (the env var shipped via #401), with auto-promote to cloud per #task-aware-model-router slice 4's quality-gate. Ship paired tests for the 8 cells (2 roles × 4 budget states).
    - **Slice 2 — orchestrator entrypoint** (1d): `pnpm minsky orchestrate --workers=N [--dry-run]` in `novel/tick-loop/bin/orchestrate.mjs`. The orchestrator spawns N background workers (each = `pnpm minsky --worker-id <i> --workers-total <N> --role=worker`), tails their `.minsky/workers/<id>.log`, and harvests their PR URLs as they ship. The orchestrator's own iteration is a Claude Code session that picks the next decomposition target from TASKS.md and emits N non-overlapping `Touches:`-globbed task assignments to the workers via `.minsky/workers/<id>.assignment.json` (a Strategy seam — the workers read assignments, not pull from TASKS.md, eliminating the row-claim race for the assigned set; the `flock` claim from #daemon-parallel-worktree-launch remains for the *unassigned* tail of TASKS.md so workers don't starve when the orchestrator is between fanouts). Operator opens Claude Code, types `pnpm minsky orchestrate --workers=4`, and the brain-vs-hands separation is live.
    - **Slice 3 — cross-layer chaos invariants** (1d): three new invariants in `scripts/self-diagnose.mjs` + paired tests, following the `self-diagnose-on-start` (#self-diagnose-on-start) authoring pattern:
      - `orchestrator-budget-monopoly`: rolling-1h ratio of orchestrator-Claude-tokens to worker-Claude-tokens ≥ 9.0 when ≥1 worker is alive. Violation = role-pin is leaking.
      - `worker-local-utilization`: rolling-1h ratio of worker iterations on `local-qwen` provider ≥ 0.8. Violation = local stack is broken or auto-promote is over-firing.
      - `orchestrator-detached-worker-finish`: when the orchestrator's PID exits (clean or crash) with workers still alive, each worker MUST either (a) finish its claimed task and ship a PR within its existing 30-min iteration TTL OR (b) self-terminate cleanly via SIGTERM trap. Violation = a worker zombied past orchestrator death. Tested by `scripts/chaos-orchestrator-kill.mjs` (new, paired with `chaos-budget-exhaust.mjs`'s pattern).
    - **Slice 4 — chaos test harness for the inverse** (0.5d): `scripts/chaos-worker-hang.mjs` — pauses one worker's `aider` child via `SIGSTOP` and asserts the watchdog (#spawn-n-workers-with-watchdog-monitoring) detects the hang within 60s and the orchestrator reassigns the worker's `Touches:` glob to a fresh worker without touching the original PR. Paired with the existing `daemon-claude-print-hang-watchdog` (P0).
    - **Slice 5 — operator UX + dashboard tile** (0.5d): the dashboard surfaces orchestrator vs worker token-spend split (composes with `dashboard-web-rich-runs-view`'s "Token budget" section) and a per-worker provider mix bar.
  - **Files**: `novel/tick-loop/src/llm-provider-selector.ts` (slice 1 — extend input with `role`), `novel/tick-loop/src/llm-provider-selector.test.ts` (slice 1 — 8 new cells), `novel/tick-loop/bin/orchestrate.mjs` (slice 2 — new entrypoint), `novel/tick-loop/src/orchestrator.ts` + `.test.ts` (slice 2 — pure decomposition + assignment dispatch), `novel/tick-loop/src/worker-assignment.ts` + `.test.ts` (slice 2 — `.minsky/workers/<id>.assignment.json` Strategy seam), `package.json` (slice 2 — `bin: { "minsky-orchestrate": "..." }`), `scripts/self-diagnose.mjs` (slice 3 — three new invariants), `scripts/self-diagnose.test.mjs` (slice 3 — paired tests), `scripts/chaos-orchestrator-kill.mjs` + `.test.mjs` (slice 3), `scripts/chaos-worker-hang.mjs` + `.test.mjs` (slice 4), `novel/dashboard-web/src/orchestrator-tile.ts` + tests (slice 5), `docs/orchestrator-fanout.md` (operator-facing — flow diagram + the role-separation rationale + the failure-mode inventory).
  - **Verification**: paired tests at every slice. Integration test (`test/orchestrator-fanout.integration.test.ts`): boot 2 workers + 1 orchestrator against a fixture TASKS.md with 4 non-overlapping tasks; assert orchestrator-token spend ≥ 90 % of cloud tokens, worker-token spend ≥ 80 % on local. Chaos: kill the orchestrator at iteration 2; assert workers finish their claim + ship PR + self-terminate within 30 min. Inverse chaos: SIGSTOP one worker's aider child; assert the watchdog reassigns within 60s.
  - **Measurement**: `node scripts/orchestrator-fanout-ratio.mjs --window=24h --json` returns `{"orchestrator_claude_tokens": Oc, "worker_claude_tokens": Wc, "worker_local_iterations": Wl, "worker_total_iterations": Wt, "merged_prs": M, "operator_interventions": I}`. Acceptance thresholds (rolling 24h, post-slice-3): `Oc / (Oc + Wc) ≥ 0.9`, `Wl / Wt ≥ 0.8`, `M ≥ 1.5 × serial_baseline`, `I = 0`. AND `node scripts/self-diagnose.mjs --json | jq -e '[.[] | select(.id | startswith("orchestrator-") or startswith("worker-"))] | length == 0'` exits 0 in the steady state. Cross-link with #task-aware-model-router slice 0's `iteration-routing-baseline.mjs` — same span attribute set, this script aggregates by role.
  - **Pivot**: see Hypothesis cells (a)–(d). Layered pivots: if role-pin leaks (a<70 %), retire the soft preference and switch to a hard env gate (`MINSKY_ROLE=worker` makes `decideProvider` *refuse* to route to Opus). If local-Qwen worker success drops (b<50 %), retire local for worker default and use Haiku-as-default-worker — keeps the orchestrator-Opus-monopoly while abandoning the "local-only-on-electricity" goal. **Don't retire role separation**; even if both pivots fire, the brain-vs-hands split is the load-bearing decision.
  - **Acceptance**: (1) `decideProvider` accepts `role` and returns role-correct provider in all 8 cells with paired tests; (2) `pnpm minsky orchestrate --workers=N` ships and harvests PRs; (3) three new self-diagnose invariants ship with paired tests; (4) `scripts/chaos-orchestrator-kill.mjs` and `scripts/chaos-worker-hang.mjs` ship with paired tests; (5) integration test passes; (6) measurement script exits 0 in steady state; (7) `docs/orchestrator-fanout.md` documents the role-separation rationale + the 6 known cross-layer failure modes (orchestrator crash, worker crash, mlx-server crash, claude exhaustion mid-fanout, worktree corruption, mergiraf wrong-merge); (8) operator can launch the whole flow with one command from inside Claude Code.
  - **Anchor**: operator 2026-05-10 directive (verbatim above); rule #1 (don't reinvent — composes existing substrates rather than designing a novel orchestrator from scratch); rule #2 (Strategy seam — `worker-assignment.ts` is the seam between orchestrator and workers; fed by injected I/O); rule #6 (stay alive — orchestrator-detached-worker-finish invariant codifies it); rule #7 (graceful degrade — orchestrator pauses rather than degrading on Opus-unavailable); rule #9 (pre-registered HDD — Hypothesis/Success/Pivot/Measurement above); rule #13 (security-privacy — local-routing on workers keeps more code on-device, reducing data-egress surface); Basiri et al. 2016, "Principles of Chaos Engineering", *IEEE Software* (steady-state hypothesis — the three new invariants); Hewitt 1973 (actor model — orchestrator and workers as actors with `.minsky/workers/<id>.assignment.json` as the message-passing seam); Beyer SRE 2016 Ch. 6 (silence-is-failure — `orchestrator-detached-worker-finish` invariant prevents zombie workers); the load-bearing post cited by #task-aware-model-router (97/88/61/29 bucket-quality numbers — workers' bucket distribution is dominated by single-file/multi-file slices, where local matches cloud).
  - **Risk**: medium-high. (a) **Role-pin race**: if the orchestrator's Claude session is itself spawned from inside Claude Code (the operator's session), the parent Claude session and the orchestrator child both contend for the same 5h cap. Mitigation: the orchestrator's `claude --print` invocations carry a distinct project key so token accounting splits cleanly; if the operator's parent session burns the cap, the orchestrator pauses (existing budget-guard); the workers continue on local. (b) **Worker context-window overflow**: aider+Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B has a smaller effective context than Opus; `daemon-aider-brief-shrinker` (P0, line 177) is a precondition. (c) **Orchestrator-as-single-point-of-failure**: if the orchestrator crashes, a fresh fanout can't be scheduled. Mitigation: slice 3's `orchestrator-detached-worker-finish` invariant ensures workers don't zombie; the operator restarts the orchestrator from inside Claude Code. (d) **Cross-layer telemetry drift**: orchestrator and worker spans must agree on the `role` attribute. Mitigation: spans emitted by a shared helper that reads `MINSKY_ROLE` once at boot; integration test asserts the attribute is non-empty on every span. (e) **Mergiraf wrong-merge across cross-worker overlaps**: if mergiraf auto-resolves a conflict incorrectly between two worker PRs, the merged main is wrong. Mitigation: orchestrator's review gate (it reads each worker PR before merging — that's the brain's job).
  - **Open-questions** (must be answered in slice-1 implementation iteration): (a) does the orchestrator run from the operator's Claude Code session (interactive) or from a separate `claude --print` session spawned by `pnpm minsky orchestrate`? Trade-off: interactive gives the operator visibility but ties the fanout's life to the operator's terminal; spawned gives daemon-like persistence but consumes a Claude session slot. (b) when both orchestrator and workers are alive, who edits TASKS.md? Proposal: only the orchestrator writes to TASKS.md; workers write to their assignment file and emit findings as PR-body Self-grade lessons that the orchestrator picks up post-merge. (c) `MINSKY_ROLE` semantics — does it default to `worker` (safer — never accidentally burns Opus) or to `orchestrator` (operator-friendly — `pnpm minsky` from inside Claude Code Just Works)? Proposal: default `orchestrator` when launched interactively (`isatty(stdout)`), default `worker` when launched as a `--worker-id` child.
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-10 operator directive — *"I start minsky from claude code … on the way where it's optimal minsky spawns more local agents that run on my local machine to work on parallel tasks of minsky in git worktrees. Main minsky worker (claude) handles those workers. Everything is extremely stable about this, with chaos engineering and other minsky principles. Essentially I want claude to be the brains of this (top layer) and everything below to be run by local models but with full list of features of claude."* The composition of #daemon-parallel-worktree-launch + #task-aware-model-router + #spawn-n-workers-with-watchdog-monitoring captures the substrate; this block adds the role-pin, the operator entrypoint, and the cross-layer chaos invariants.

- [ ] `dashboard-web-rich-runs-view` — dashboard shows everything operator needs to know about the daemon's runs without asking the agent
  - **ID**: dashboard-web-rich-runs-view
  - **Tags**: p1, dashboard-web, observability, operator-ux
  - **Milestone**: M2
  - **Touches**: `novel/dashboard-web/**`
  - **Estimate**: 1w
  - **Hypothesis**: Operator request 2026-05-05: "update web dashboard to be full of useful information about minsky's runs so that I don't have to ask you, just open it". Currently the dashboard surfaces the 10 success metrics + the activity feed (PR #154 — 20 most recent iteration spans). That's not enough — the operator still asks me "how is it going?" because the dashboard doesn't show: (1) **rolling shipped-PR count** (last 24h / 7d / 30d), (2) **per-task progress** (which TASKS.md tasks are claimed / in-flight / blocked / completed-this-week), (3) **daemon health summary** (process up? last successful iteration? noop rate?), (4) **CI status of every open daemon-authored PR** with the failure summary if red, (5) **self-diagnose findings** (invariant violations live), (6) **5h-window token usage** (the BudgetGuard signal — am I about to be paused?), (7) **recent commits** (5 most recent on main, with daemon vs operator split). Pre-registration: post-fix, the operator opens the dashboard and answers "how's it going?" themselves — no agent question needed. Measurable as: zero "how is it going" questions in operator-agent transcripts over a rolling 7d window where the supervisor was active.
  - **Details**: (a) New sections on dashboard's `/` route: "Throughput" (3 numbers: PRs-shipped-24h / 7d / 30d), "Active tasks" (TASKS.md with badges: claimed / blocked / completed), "Open PRs" (every open daemon-authored PR with CI verdict + last activity), "Self-diagnose" (live invariant findings; green if zero), "Token budget" (BudgetGuard's 5h-window snapshot from token-monitor). (b) Each section is a Strategy seam fed by a new `getX(): Promise<XSnapshot>` injected into `createServer({...})` — same pattern as `getValue` / `getActivity`. (c) The renderer stays pure; the I/O happens in `start.ts`. (d) Auto-refresh interval drops from 5s (current activity feed) to 15s for the heavier sections (rule-2 — don't burn the server; the data doesn't change that fast). (e) Dark theme + glanceable card layout already exists (PR #153) — extend, don't redesign.
  - **Files**: `novel/dashboard-web/src/throughput.ts`, `tasks-overview.ts`, `prs-overview.ts`, `diagnostics.ts`, `token-budget.ts` + paired tests; `novel/dashboard-web/src/render.ts` (extends the existing render with new sections); `novel/dashboard-web/src/start.ts` (Strategy injection).
  - **Verification**: paired tests for each new section's pure renderer; live verification — operator opens `http://localhost:8181/` and answers "how's it going?" without checking git or gh.
  - **Measurement**: `curl -s http://localhost:8181/ | grep -c -E '(Throughput|Active tasks|Open PRs|Self-diagnose|Token budget)'` ≥ 5 (the 5 new sections rendered). AND a 1-week observation: zero operator-agent "how's it going" / "what's status" / "is supervisor running" questions in the conversation log.
  - **Pivot**: if the dashboard becomes too dense (operator scrolls past sections / can't find what they need), pivot to a per-section toggle (default: throughput + active tasks + supervisor health visible; the rest collapsed). Don't ship more than 5 sections by default — Card & Mackinlay 1999's glanceable-display threshold is ~5 visual elements.
  - **Anchor**: Card & Mackinlay 1999 (glanceable display); Wilkie 2018 (RED method — Rate / Errors / Duration as the lens); Beyer SRE 2016 Ch. 6 (every state operator cares about must surface); rule #2 (each section is a Strategy seam; the renderer stays pure).
  - **Risk**: low. Pure additive; the existing seam pattern handles it.
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-05 user request — "update web dashboard to be full of useful information about minsky's runs so that I don't have to ask you, just open it". Promotes from "operator asks me" to "operator opens the dashboard". The "how is it going?" question I answered earlier this session is the load-bearing data point.

- [ ] `worker-model-per-machine-config` — local-LLM worker model is per-machine: env var → gitignored `.minsky/local-config.json` → repo default; pin this Mac to `mlx-community/Qwen3.6-27B-4bit` (Opus-quality dense)
  - **ID**: worker-model-per-machine-config
  - **Tags**: p0, local-llm, per-machine, model-pin, recency-checked, follow-up
  - **Milestone**: M2
  - **Touches**: `novel/tick-loop/src/local-llm-bootstrap.ts`, `novel/tick-loop/src/llm-invocation.ts`, `novel/tick-loop/src/local-config.ts` (new), `.minsky/local-config.json` (per-machine, gitignored — example committed in `docs/local-llm-fallback.md`).
  - **Pick-next**: yes — operator 2026-05-10, "I'm getting qwen3.6:27b. Update minsky's tasks so that this model will be used by default for local runs on THIS machine. Note that on other machine I might want a different model".
  - **Estimate**: 1d (Slice A: resolution chain + test; Slice B: bootstrap/invocation consolidation + recency anchor).
  - **Composes-into**: parent task `claude-orchestrator-local-worker-fanout` (P0, line 446) — the worker-role provider default reads the resolved model from this resolution chain rather than the hard-coded `DEFAULT_LOCAL_LLM_MODEL` constant. Composes with the existing `decideProvider`/`MINSKY_LLM_PROVIDER=local-preferred` env (#401 shipped).
  - **Hypothesis**: today the worker model is hard-coded twice — `DEFAULT_LOCAL_LLM_MODEL = "mlx-community/Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B-Instruct-4bit"` in `local-llm-bootstrap.ts:242` and `DEFAULT_AIDER_MODEL = "openai/mlx-community/Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct-4bit"` in `llm-invocation.ts:121`. The two strings already disagree (Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B vs Qwen2.5-Coder-32B), which is a divergence bug latent in main. A second, distinct symptom: per-machine hardware is heterogeneous (M1 Max 32GB unified ≠ M3 Max 64GB ≠ DGX Spark) and the right model differs by tier; pinning one constant in the repo forces the lowest common denominator. Pre-registration: post-fix, **(a)** bootstrap (downloader, cache probe) and aider invocation read from the *same* resolved model id — divergence between the two strings is impossible by construction (one resolution function, two well-typed accessors); **(b)** on this M1 Max 32GB, `pnpm minsky` resolves to `mlx-community/Qwen3.6-27B-4bit` (the per-machine pin); **(c)** on a hypothetical second machine without the per-machine file and without the env var, `pnpm minsky` resolves to the repo default unchanged from today (no behaviour regression for un-configured machines); **(d)** the worker bucket-1 (single-file slice) success rate on Qwen3.6-27B-Dense is within ±5 pp of the prior Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B baseline once #task-aware-model-router slice 0's measurement substrate lands — the recency-checked Terminal-Bench 59.3 (Opus-parity) and SWE-bench Verified 77.2 numbers should hold up. If (d) fails (>5 pp regression), pivot per-machine pin back to the MoE; the operator's "Opus-like" preference does not override observed merge rate.
  - **Details**:
