- [ ] `agentbrew-sync-missing-example-code-secure-to-openhands` — agentbrew syncs ~200 skills to `~/.openhands/skills/` for OpenHands SDK to load at agent boot, but `example-code-secure` is missing — it lives in `~/.claude/plugins/marketplaces/dev-tool-plugins-registry/code-secure-plugin/skills/example-code-secure/` but never gets propagated. The `~/.agents/agents/security-reviewer.md` (and `~/.claude/agents/security-reviewer.md`) reference `skills: [example-code-secure]`; OpenHands SDK's `register_file_agents` crashes with `ValueError: Skill 'example-code-secure' not found` whenever Conversation.run() is called. Today this is worked around by a manual stub at `~/.openhands/skills/example-code-secure/SKILL.md`; agentbrew should sync the canonical file directly
  - **ID**: agentbrew-sync-missing-example-code-secure-to-openhands
  - **Tags**: p2, scout-finding, agentbrew, openhands-sync, observed-2026-05-24
  - **Milestone**: M2
  - **Competitive-goal**: closes the "OpenHands SDK crashes when ANY agent references example-code-secure" failure mode. Without this, every operator who has the `security-reviewer` agent and tries to spawn OpenHands gets a hard crash before the agent can run.
  - **Hypothesis**: agentbrew's `~/.claude/skills/ → ~/.openhands/skills/` sync rule excludes skills from marketplace plugins (the ones under `~/.claude/plugins/marketplaces/`). The fix is to add marketplace-plugin skills to the sync set so every `skills:` reference in any agent definition resolves correctly. Falsifiable: if agentbrew already syncs marketplace skills and `example-code-secure` was sync'd correctly but then removed for some other reason, the fix is different.
  - **Success**: `find ~/.openhands/skills/example-code-secure -name SKILL.md` returns a non-stub file; the OpenHands SDK conversation construction succeeds without a `Skill 'example-code-secure' not found` error; the existing stub at `~/.openhands/skills/example-code-secure/SKILL.md` (manually created 2026-05-24) is replaced by the canonical synced file.
  - **Pivot**: if agentbrew can't sync marketplace skills (e.g. policy reasons), the alternative is to add a `~/.openhands/agents/` sync rule that filters out agents whose `skills:` references can't be resolved — but that requires agentbrew to validate skill availability, which is heavier than just syncing the missing skill.
  - **Measurement**: `node -e "console.log(fs.existsSync(require('os').homedir() + '/.openhands/skills/example-code-secure/SKILL.md'))"` returns true AND the file is NOT the placeholder stub (check for the "Placeholder stub" header text); `python3 -c "from openhands.sdk import LLM, Agent, Conversation, Tool; from openhands.tools.terminal import TerminalTool; conv = Conversation(agent=Agent(llm=LLM(model='ollama_chat/qwen3:0.6b', base_url='http://localhost:11434', api_key='x'), tools=[Tool(name=TerminalTool.name)]), workspace='/tmp')"` exits 0.
  - **Anchor**: scout finding from PR #786 (`feat/openhands-ollama-local-llm-support`); rule #17 (proactive healing — observation IS the fix); the agentbrew skill-sync invariant should be "every `skills:` reference in any agent definition under `~/.agents/` or `~/.claude/agents/` resolves to a real SKILL.md in `~/.openhands/skills/`" — that's a 1-line shell invariant the agentbrew test suite should enforce.
  - **Files**: agentbrew sync configuration (in the agentbrew repo, not this one); `~/.openhands/skills/example-code-secure/SKILL.md` (currently a stub — to be replaced or removed); no minsky-repo files (this task is filed here because minsky's smoke test discovered it, but the fix lives upstream in agentbrew).

- [ ] `openhands-vs-claude-m110-corpus-live-ab` — close M1.14 by running `bin/minsky competitive --backend openhands` against the M1.10 corpus and publishing the delta vs. `--backend claude`. Today the substrate ships (PR #782) and openhands is the default, but no live A/B run has compared the OpenHands-via-shim path to the legacy Claude Code direct CLI path on the same task corpus
  - **ID**: openhands-vs-claude-m110-corpus-live-ab
  - **Tags**: p2, m1, m1-14, openhands, competitive-benchmark, live-ab, observed-2026-05-24
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Competitive-goal**: closes M1.14 — "OpenHands as the canonical agent runtime ... `bin/minsky competitive --backend openhands` produces a comparable scorecard against `--backend claude` on the M1.10 corpus". Today the openhands default is unbenchmarked vs. the legacy default; without numbers the operator can't prove the wrap is at parity or better. After this lands, the scorecard answers the falsifiable question "is the OpenHands-via-Claude-SDK path actually better than the bare Claude-Code-CLI path on real Minsky tasks?"
  - **Hypothesis**: OpenHands' published 65.8% SWE-bench Verified score (Apr 2025) is the relevant prior; on Minsky's M1.10 corpus the OpenHands-via-shim path is at parity (±2pp on verdict rate, ±15% on iteration duration) or better than the legacy Claude Code direct CLI. Falsifiable: if openhands-via-shim is ≥10pp WORSE on verdict rate, the shim/SDK introduces a meaningful regression vs. the bare Claude Code CLI and the default flip needs to revert.
  - **Success**: `bin/minsky competitive --backend openhands --backend claude --corpus m1-10 --iterations 30` produces `competitive-scorecard.json` with both backends' rows populated; verdict-rate delta is within ±2pp; iteration-duration delta is within ±15%. Scorecard published in M1.10 corpus + an entry in `docs/validated-learnings.md` recording the result.
  - **Pivot**: if openhands-via-shim is ≥10pp WORSE on verdict rate, revert the 2026-05-24 default flip (claude becomes default again until 2026-06-01 when the canonical CLI ships and we can re-test with the cleaner contract). Document the regression cause in `competitors/openhands.md`.
  - **Measurement**: `bin/minsky competitive --backend openhands --backend claude --corpus m1-10 --iterations 30 --output competitive-scorecard.json` exits 0; `jq '.backends[] | {id, verdict_rate, iteration_duration_p50_ms}' competitive-scorecard.json` shows both backends with non-null rows; the verdict_rate delta is within ±2pp.
  - **Anchor**: operator 2026-05-24 vision directive ("complete OpenHands integration today"); MILESTONES.md M1.14 closure criterion; All-Hands AI, *SOTA on SWE-bench Verified with Inference-Time Scaling and Critic Model*, all-hands.dev/blog, 2025-04-15 (the OpenHands 65.8% number we're inheriting via wrap).
  - **Files**: `scripts/competitive-benchmark-multi-backend.mjs` (new — extends existing `bin/minsky competitive` with `--backend <id>` repeatable flag); existing M1.10 corpus reused; `competitive-scorecard.json` output schema gets a `backends[]` array; new `docs/validated-learnings.md` entry recording the A/B result.

- [ ] `path-c-deletion-prioritisation-by-consumer-count` — when continuing the Path C reshape phase 4+ (delete sweep 1 of redundant Minsky packages), run a consumer-count audit FIRST and prioritize zero-consumer packages. Per the validated learning from PR #784 (persona-spawner deletion took 30min not the predicted 1 week because consumer count was zero — an isolated package is the cheapest to delete)
  - **ID**: path-c-deletion-prioritisation-by-consumer-count
  - **Tags**: p2, m2, path-c, reshape, deletion-prioritisation, observed-2026-05-24
  - **Milestone**: M2
  - **Competitive-goal**: closes the "Path C reshape velocity gap" — today the plan has ~13 deletion candidates with ad-hoc ordering; if we prioritize by consumer count (zero first, then ≤2, then ≤5) the early wins are mechanical 30min PRs that build momentum, vs. the alternative of starting with the heaviest migration first and getting stuck.
  - **Hypothesis**: of the ~13 remaining deletion candidates in `docs/plans/2026-05-22-path-c-openhands-reshape.md`, at least 3 have zero external consumers (matching persona-spawner's pattern). Identifying and removing those first gets phase 4 to ≥30% complete in <1 day of work. Falsifiable: if a consumer-count audit shows zero zero-consumer packages remain, the prioritization rule yields no quick wins and the plan needs to start with the cheapest non-zero candidate instead.
  - **Success**: a one-time audit script (`scripts/path-c-consumer-count.mjs`) lists each Path-C deletion candidate with its external-import count; the result is committed as `docs/plans/path-c-deletion-priorities.md`; ≥3 zero-consumer packages are queued for the next deletion sweep.
  - **Pivot**: if the audit finds zero zero-consumer candidates, fall back to "cheapest first by LOC count" — sort the candidates by `wc -l` of their src/ trees and take the smallest as the next deletion target instead.
  - **Measurement**: `node scripts/path-c-consumer-count.mjs --json | jq '.candidates[] | select(.consumer_count == 0) | .package' | wc -l` ≥ 3; `docs/plans/path-c-deletion-priorities.md` exists and lists each candidate with its consumer count.
  - **Anchor**: validated learning `openhands-integration-shipped-2026-05-24` (the substrate-first pattern); PR #784 (persona-spawner deletion — predicted 1 week, actual 30 min when consumer count = 0); Beer 1981 *Brain of the Firm* (System 4 — the long-term adaptation layer chooses interventions by cost-of-change, not by ideal architecture).
  - **Files**: new `scripts/path-c-consumer-count.mjs`; new `docs/plans/path-c-deletion-priorities.md`; updates to `docs/plans/2026-05-22-path-c-openhands-reshape.md` § Phase 4 (link to the priorities doc).

- [ ] `migrate-skill-loaders-to-openhands-agentskills` — Minsky agents read `.claude/skills/*` via custom loaders today. OpenHands has AgentSkills-spec compat — the same format Claude Code already uses. Per the Path C plan phase 2, replace Minsky's custom skill loaders with OpenHands' native AgentSkills loader so the sync target moves from `.claude/skills/` to `.openhands/microagents/` mechanically (agentbrew already handles this transformation)
  - **ID**: migrate-skill-loaders-to-openhands-agentskills
  - **Tags**: p2, m2, path-c, openhands-native, agentskills-spec, observed-2026-05-24
  - **Milestone**: M2
  - **Blocked by**: openhands-vs-claude-m110-corpus-live-ab
  - **Competitive-goal**: closes the "rule #1 silently-violated by maintaining our own skill loader" gap. OpenHands ships AgentSkills-spec compat as a first-class primitive; Minsky's custom loader is duplicate functionality. Migrating closes the rule-#1 violation and shrinks Minsky's surface by ~one more redundant adapter.
  - **Hypothesis**: existing Minsky skills written to the AgentSkills-spec port to `.openhands/microagents/` mechanically — no rewrite required because the spec is identical. After migration, the agentbrew sync target switches to `.openhands/microagents/` and the OpenHands SDK loads skills natively at agent boot. Falsifiable: if ≥5% of Minsky's existing skills need re-authoring to fit OpenHands' loader (not pure format conversion), the AgentSkills-spec compatibility claim is wrong and this is a port rather than a migration.
  - **Success**: `agentbrew sync` writes to `.openhands/microagents/`; OpenHands SDK loads ≥10 of Minsky's existing skills successfully at agent boot (verified via `--verbose` startup log); zero skill files needed substantive re-authoring (only whitespace / frontmatter trivial diffs).
  - **Pivot**: if ≥5% of skills need re-authoring, treat this as a port (>2 weeks) rather than a migration (<1 day) — document the actual format delta in `competitors/openhands.md` and split into per-skill-category migration tasks.
  - **Measurement**: `find .openhands/microagents -name '*.md' | wc -l` ≥ existing `.claude/skills/*` count; `OPENHANDS_SUPPRESS_BANNER=1 python3 -c "from openhands.sdk.skills import load_project_skills; print(len(load_project_skills('.')))"` returns the same count; manual diff confirms zero substantive rewrites.
  - **Anchor**: rule #1 (don't reinvent — OpenHands AgentSkills-spec covers Minsky's skill-loader scope); rule #14 (delegate to agents; adopt their native capabilities; shrink to fit); validated learning `openhands-natively-covers-personas-and-skills` (recorded 2026-05-22 in `docs/validated-learnings.md`); All-Hands AI, OpenHands SDK V1 docs, openhands.dev, 2025.
  - **Files**: new agentbrew config update to target `.openhands/microagents/`; deletion of any `novel/*/src/skill-loader.ts` files (audit first — likely 0 today, but the agentbrew sync code that writes to `.claude/skills/` would need to switch targets); `competitors/openhands.md` updated with migration notes.

- [ ] `autonomous-task-authoring-between-ticks` — the daemon doesn't currently author new tasks autonomously between iterations; agents file P1–P3 tasks WHILE running iterations (rule #17 scout discipline), but when the queue empties the daemon waits instead of running an audit pass that produces new work. Close the loop: every Nth tick (or whenever `pickHostTask` returns null), the daemon invokes the audit-pass equivalent (sweep / project-audit / standing-audit-gap-loop) and seeds the next ticks
  - **ID**: autonomous-task-authoring-between-ticks
  - **Tags**: p2, m2, mape-k, task-authoring, self-improving-pillar, autonomy
  - **Milestone**: M2
  - **Competitive-goal**: closes the operator's "comes up with tasks" pillar (2026-05-22 vision directive). Today this pillar scores 🟡 (agents author tasks *inside* iterations only) — the operator's vision says the factory should also author tasks *between* iterations. After this lands, the daemon's tick loop is self-feeding: it runs `audit-pass` when the queue empties and seeds the next N ticks from the audit findings.
  - **Hypothesis**: running an audit-pass when `pickHostTask` returns null produces at least 1 new actionable task on ≥80% of empty-queue ticks; the daemon never idles for >1h on a non-trivially-empty repo. Falsifiable: if audit-pass produces 0 new tasks in ≥5 consecutive empty-queue ticks on a repo with non-zero TASKS.md history, the audit-pass policy is wrong (likely scope-discipline collision with rule #12).
  - **Success**: on 5 consecutive `pickHostTask → null` events, the daemon invokes audit-pass; ≥4 of 5 produce at least 1 actionable task; mean idle-to-next-task latency drops from current (∞ — daemon waits for operator) to <5 min.
  - **Pivot**: if audit-pass output competes with rule #12 (scope discipline says next move is stability work when queue empties) — i.e., audit-pass keeps proposing features when rule #12 says ship stability — re-scope this task to "rule #12-aware audit-pass: only audits stability surfaces (failure-mode tables, chaos coverage, MTTR, observability gaps) when the queue empties, never proposes new features." Pivot threshold: ≥30% of audit-pass output on empty-queue ticks is feature work that rule #12 rejects.
  - **Measurement**: `node scripts/audit-pass-empty-queue-coverage.mjs --window=10ticks` returns `{ "empty_queue_ticks": N, "audit_pass_invocations": N, "new_tasks_produced": M, "idle_to_next_task_p50_minutes": X }` with empty_queue_ticks == audit_pass_invocations and idle_to_next_task_p50_minutes < 5.
  - **Anchor**: operator 2026-05-22 vision directive ("comes up with tasks"); rule #12 (scope discipline — stability work when queue empties); MAPE-K (Kephart & Chess 2003 — Plan phase generates new actions from Analyse phase observations); validated-learnings — entry to be written when this ships and the 5-tick measurement runs.
  - **Files**: new `novel/tick-loop/src/audit-pass-trigger.ts`; new `scripts/audit-pass-empty-queue-coverage.mjs`; instrumentation in `novel/cross-repo-runner/src/task-finder.ts` (the `pickHostTask → null` callsite); user-story file `user-stories/015-autonomous-task-authoring.md`.

- [ ] `throughput-at-scale-benchmark` — the "code factory" pillar of the 2026-05-22 vision is unmeasured today; the cross-repo runner walks N hosts at 3 iterations/host but we don't have a benchmark that exercises `--hosts-dir` against ≥5 fixture repos and measures PRs/day, iterations/day, or merge-rate at scale. Add a fixture-host stress benchmark to `bin/minsky competitive` so the throughput claim is falsifiable
  - **ID**: throughput-at-scale-benchmark
  - **Tags**: p2, m1, throughput, code-factory-pillar, competitive-benchmark, scale
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Competitive-goal**: closes the operator's "code factory" pillar (2026-05-22 vision directive). Today this pillar scores 🟡 — the mechanism (cross-repo runner) ships but throughput-at-scale is unmeasured; the M1.10 scorecard reports per-competitor SWE-bench numbers but has no minsky-throughput row. After this lands, `bin/minsky competitive --throughput` reports PRs/day on a ≥5-repo fixture-host fleet over a 24h window, which is a falsifiable claim worth publishing.
  - **Hypothesis**: minsky running `--hosts-dir` against ≥5 fixture repos for 24h produces ≥10 PRs/day at ≥80% draft-acceptance rate (no scope-leak, no force-push, no destructive op). Falsifiable: if PRs/day < 10 or draft-acceptance < 80%, the multi-host scheduler isn't yet "factory-grade" and the pillar stays 🟡 until a stability ratchet ships.
  - **Success**: `bin/minsky competitive --throughput --fixture-hosts=5 --duration=24h` produces `competitive-scorecard.json` with a `minsky_throughput_prs_per_day` row ≥10 and a `minsky_draft_acceptance_rate` row ≥0.80; scorecard is reproducible (run twice within ±10% of each other).
  - **Pivot**: if running ≥5 fixture repos in parallel exceeds the operator's machine-utilisation budget (rule #15 — default 70%, swarm-window cap 80%), reduce to ≥3 fixture repos and adjust the success threshold to ≥6 PRs/day. Pivot threshold: rule-#15 budget breaches occur on ≥2 consecutive 24h windows.
  - **Measurement**: `node scripts/throughput-benchmark.mjs --fixture-hosts=5 --duration=24h` writes `minsky_throughput_prs_per_day` to the M1.10 scorecard; `bin/minsky competitive` includes the row in the next weekly auto-refresh.
  - **Anchor**: operator 2026-05-22 vision directive ("code factory" implies scale + throughput); rule #15 (machine-utilisation budget); M1.10 competitive benchmark substrate (already shipped — this adds one falsifiable row to the existing scorecard).
  - **Files**: new `scripts/throughput-benchmark.mjs`; extension to `bin/minsky competitive` for `--throughput` flag; new fixture-hosts dir under `test-fixtures/throughput/` (5 git-init fixture repos with seed TASKS.md); user-story file `user-stories/016-code-factory-throughput.md`.

- [ ] `vision-md-line-width-chunking` — chunk `vision.md` so no line exceeds 1000 chars; current state has 89 lines >1000 chars (max 14107) which crushes any context window that loads the file whole
  - **ID**: vision-md-line-width-chunking
  - **Tags**: p2, scout, docs, vision-md, line-width, context-window, rule-15
  - **Hypothesis**: replacing the 89 ultra-wide single-paragraph constitutional clauses with paragraph-broken equivalents reduces total file size and lets agents load `vision.md` into a smaller context-window budget while preserving 100% of the content. Falsifiable: a diff that adds only paragraph breaks (no semantic edits) should round-trip through a markdown parser with identical AST nodes except for paragraph splits.
  - **Success**: `awk '{ if(length > 1000) c++ } END { print c+0 }' vision.md` returns `0`; total byte size drops to ≤ 500 KB (currently 1.2 MB); a markdown parse comparison confirms semantic equivalence.
  - **Pivot**: if breaking constitutional clauses into paragraphs accidentally changes their meaning (a clause cites a multi-sentence binding rule that must read as one unit), revert that specific clause and document why in `vision.md` near it. Never drop below ≥10 lines unchunked if all of them have semantic-unit reasons.
  - **Measurement**: `awk '{ if(length > 1000) c++ } END { print c+0 }' vision.md` → 0; `wc -c vision.md` ≤ 524288.
  - **Anchor**: scouted while landing `docs/acknowledge-canonical-doc-structure` PR (2026-05-23 canonicalize-docs sweep); rule #15 (milestone alignment gate — vision.md is one of the seven surfaces that must stay readable to enforce the gate).
  - **Details**: `vision.md` is the project constitution and is intentionally long (~717 lines). Per the load-project-context rule shipped via agentbrew, agents should treat it as system-prompt-level context. However, 89 of those lines are single ultra-wide paragraphs (max 14107 chars on one line). Most modern markdown editors and agents wrap on prose; the absence of paragraph breaks is likely an artifact of editor settings rather than a deliberate constitutional choice. Action: walk the file from top to bottom; for each line >1000 chars, identify natural sentence/clause boundaries (after `.`, `;`, or section markers like `—`) and insert paragraph breaks. Do not change wording, citations, rule numbers, or pattern conformance assertions. Run a diff afterward to confirm only whitespace and paragraph-break additions.
  - **Files**: `vision.md` (the only file modified); any test in `tests/specifications/` that asserts on `vision.md` line counts (none today, but check before committing).
  - **Acceptance**: (a) `awk` returns 0 for lines >1000 chars; (b) `wc -c` ≤ 524288 bytes (≤512KB); (c) markdown structural diff (e.g. `pandoc -t json` round-trip) shows no semantic differences beyond paragraph splits; (d) all CI gates (`pnpm verify`, `pnpm check`) still pass; (e) no rule numbers / citations / pattern names change.

- [ ] `revisit-claude-native-task-standard-when-persistence-fixed` — re-run the adopt/bridge/reject analysis when GitHub #33764 closes AND Agent Teams exits experimental status; output is a dated section appended to `docs/research/claude-native-task-standard.md` with a new recommendation
  - **ID**: revisit-claude-native-task-standard-when-persistence-fixed
  - **Tags**: p2, conditional, agent-teams, rule-14, follow-up, observed-2026-05-23
  - **Blocked**: needs-external-progress — GitHub issue #33764 (Agent Teams state wiped on session restart) is open as of 2026-05-23; revisit blocks until that issue closes AND `code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-teams` removes the "experimental" warning. The monthly-watch standing-loop task `watch-claude-native-task-standard-monthly` (P3) is responsible for detecting the unblock event.
  - **Milestone**: M2
  - **Competitive-goal**: if Anthropic ships persistence + stabilizes Agent Teams, Minsky may delete `daemon-duplicate-work-detection` (~200 LOC) by delegating multi-agent claim coordination to native flock. Currently a +550 LOC net regression per the costed analysis in `docs/research/claude-native-task-standard.md`; unblock conditions invert the math.
  - **Touches**: `docs/research/claude-native-task-standard.md` (append dated revisit section), `TASKS.md` (this block + possible re-file of `native-agent-teams-with-tiered-adapter` slice (c) if revisit recommends bridge).
  - **Details**: When unblocked, repeat the §4 owned-surface-delta analysis in `docs/research/claude-native-task-standard.md` with current Anthropic docs + the now-resolved GH issues. Re-cost Option A (adopt) and Option B (bridge) against the current Minsky LOC baseline (~3,800 LOC of TASKS.md-aware code as of 2026-05-23). Append the new analysis as a dated subsection. If recommendation flips to Option B (bridge), file `native-agent-teams-with-tiered-adapter` slice (c) as a new P1 implementation task with the costed sync-code budget.
  - **Hypothesis**: when both unblock conditions resolve, Option B (bridge) becomes net-negative LOC because `daemon-duplicate-work-detection` (200 LOC including tests) becomes deletable AND the experimental-flag risk disappears. Pre-registration: Option B's delta moves from +550 LOC (current) to ≤-50 LOC (post-resolution) — that's the threshold for recommending bridge.
  - **Success**: a dated `### Revisit 20YY-MM-DD` heading appended to `docs/research/claude-native-task-standard.md`; if recommendation = adopt or bridge, the corresponding implementation task is filed in TASKS.md in the same commit; if recommendation = continue-rejecting, the watch-loop continues.
  - **Pivot**: if even after #33764 resolves the experimental flag remains, treat this task as still-blocked (the persistence fix without the stability flag-removal is not enough). Append a "still blocked, watch continues" dated section instead of a recommendation.
  - **Measurement**: `grep -c "^### Revisit" docs/research/claude-native-task-standard.md` rises by exactly 1 per revisit invocation; the appended section contains the recosted Option A / Option B LOC deltas vs the prior baseline; if recommendation = adopt/bridge, `grep "native-agent-teams-with-tiered-adapter\|adopt-claude-native-task-standard" TASKS.md` returns at least one P1 implementation task.
  - **Anchor**: `docs/research/claude-native-task-standard.md` § 6.2 (this task's filing record); vision.md rule #14 (delegate / adopt-native / shrink — the bar this watch task re-runs); vision.md rule #6 (let-it-crash — the rule that disqualifies the experimental dependency today); GitHub issue #33764 (the trigger condition for unblock); operator directive 2026-05-17 ("claude has a standard for tasks — investigate it" — the original directive this watch loop continues).

- [ ] `monitor-openhands-agent-canvas-launch` — track OpenHands' Agent Canvas Initiative launch (June 1, 2026) and reassess Minsky's bring-your-own-agent positioning
  - **ID**: monitor-openhands-agent-canvas-launch
  - **Tags**: p2, competitive, market-watch, orchestrator-tier, observed-2026-05-22
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Competitive-goal**: OpenHands' Agent Canvas Initiative (announced 2026-05-11, GitHub issue #14374) launches 2026-06-01 with three changes that structurally overlap Minsky's positioning: (a) Dockerless installation — removes the "Docker required" weakness in `competitors/openhands.md`; (b) bring-your-own-agent (Claude Code, Codex, OpenHands SDK) — structurally identical to Minsky's per-machine `~/.minsky/config.json` agent selection; (c) self-hosting on VMs as first-class — narrows the operator-machine-identity moat. If Agent Canvas ships clean, OpenHands becomes a stronger orchestrator-tier competitor and the README's "How Minsky compares" table needs a refresh.
  - **Touches**: `competitors/openhands.md` (refresh § "What it is" + § "Strengths" + § "Weaknesses" with post-launch architecture), `README.md` § "How Minsky compares" (update the row "Where it runs" + "Credentials" if Agent Canvas's BYO-agent model changes the identity boundary), `competitors/README.md` (refresh moat #2 + moat #5 wording if needed).
  - **Details**: 2026-05-22 OpenHands deep-dive surfaced the Agent Canvas Initiative. Trigger: 2026-06-01 (launch date). Do: (a) read the announcement post on openhands.dev/blog when it lands; (b) install the Agent Canvas alpha (github.com/OpenHands/agent-canvas) and run it on a test repo; (c) compare its credential / identity / sandbox model to Minsky's; (d) refresh `competitors/openhands.md` + the README comparison table; (e) if Agent Canvas materially overlaps Minsky's moats, file a follow-up task to sharpen Minsky's differentiation (e.g., emphasize the daemon-survives-terminal-close vs Canvas's request-response shape; emphasize constitution + MAPE-K vs Canvas's LLM-advisory).
  - **Hypothesis**: Agent Canvas's BYO-agent + Dockerless + self-host-on-VM changes are structurally similar to Minsky's per-machine agent config + no-Docker-needed + daemon-on-operator-machine. If Canvas adopts ALL THREE cleanly, the operator-machine-identity moat narrows from "unique" to "shared with the OpenHands fork". Minsky's remaining distinctive moats become: constitution + deterministic CI, MAPE-K self-improvement, cross-repo fleet, TASKS.md surface — still a 4-moat differentiation, but the headline "daemon-not-framework" framing weakens because Canvas is daemon-like in its self-hosted-on-VM mode.
  - **Success**: Within 14 days of 2026-06-01, `competitors/openhands.md` + `README.md` § "How Minsky compares" are refreshed with the post-launch architecture. The moats table in `competitors/README.md` reflects which moats survived intact, which narrowed, which were absorbed (per the vision.md delegate-contribute-absorb framework).
  - **Pivot**: If Agent Canvas absorbs 3+ Minsky moats cleanly (daemon, BYO-agent, operator-identity all match), and OpenHands has 70K+ stars + $18.8M funding + better SWE-bench, the moat differentiation falls below the 3-moat threshold required to claim "distinctive". At that point, Minsky should either pivot toward what Canvas CAN'T do (most likely: constitution + MAPE-K self-improvement + cross-repo fleet), or contribute its TASKS.md surface back to Canvas as a plugin / community pattern.
  - **Measurement**: `git log --since="2026-06-01" --until="2026-06-14" -- competitors/openhands.md | grep -c "^commit"` — exit non-zero if zero commits land in the 14-day window post-launch.
  - **Anchor**: rule #1 (don't reinvent — Canvas may absorb Minsky's shape; we need to know fast); vision.md § "Delegate → contribute → absorb" framework; competitors/openhands.md § Roadmap; All-Hands AI, *OpenHands Index Three Months Out*, openhands.dev/blog/openhands-index-3-months-out, 2026-05-11 (Agent Canvas Initiative announcement); GitHub OpenHands/OpenHands issue #14374, 2026-05-11.

- [ ] `benchmark-minsky-via-claude-on-humaneval` — run HumanEval Pass@1 on Minsky-via-Claude and publish the score as the agent-tier comparison baseline for the M1.10 corpus
  - **ID**: benchmark-minsky-via-claude-on-humaneval
  - **Tags**: p2, milestone-m1, m1-10, metrics, competitive, gap-closing, orchestrator-tier, observed-2026-05-23
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Competitive-goal**: closes the "no headline benchmark number" gap surfaced by competitors/README.md § Honest gaps. MetaGPT publishes HumanEval Pass@1 = 0.859 (ICLR 2024); Minsky has no equivalent number. Adding one lets the M1.10 scorecard compare Minsky-via-Claude's HumanEval score to MetaGPT, and quantifies whether the orchestrator-tier delta (24/7 daemon + MAPE-K + constitution) helps OR hurts versus a fixed-pipeline orchestrator.
  - **Touches**: `scripts/benchmark-humaneval.mjs` (new — the runner), `novel/competitive-benchmark/src/competitors.ts` (add a `minsky-via-claude` entry with `kind: "local-harness"` + the resulting reading), `competitors/minsky-via-claude.md` (new research file), `bin/minsky` (add `benchmark humaneval` subcommand).
  - **Details**: 2026-05-23 strategic competitive deep-dive surfaced the gap. Implementation: (a) clone openai/human-eval; (b) run mini-SWE-agent-style harness with Minsky as the orchestrator and Claude (Sonnet 4.6 or Opus 4.6) as the agent; (c) record pass@1 across the 164 HumanEval problems; (d) add to corpus as `kind: "local-harness"` with a `harnessId` per the corpus contract. Expected outcome: Minsky-via-Claude scores ≥ Claude-Code-via-Anthropic's published 0.727 (Claude inherits its own SWE-bench score; Minsky's orchestrator-tier delta should not REGRESS the agent's pass-rate). If Minsky-via-Claude scores BELOW 0.727, the orchestrator-tier delta is actively HURTING — that's a pivot signal worth knowing.
  - **Hypothesis**: Minsky-via-Claude's HumanEval Pass@1 score is within ±5 percentage points of bare Claude Code's published score (0.727). Within this band, the orchestrator-tier delta is neutral-to-positive — Minsky doesn't help on stateless per-task code generation (HumanEval doesn't reward long-horizon work) but doesn't hurt either.
  - **Success**: `bin/minsky benchmark humaneval --agent claude --model claude-opus-4-7` produces a JSON report with pass@1 ∈ [0.677, 0.777] (Claude's published score ± 5pp); the result is added to `competitors.ts` as a `minsky-via-claude` entry; the M1.10 scorecard's `humaneval-pass-at-1` axis now spans MetaGPT (0.859), Minsky-via-Claude (the new number), and any future orchestrator-tier additions.
  - **Pivot**: if Minsky-via-Claude scores >7pp BELOW bare Claude Code's 0.727, the orchestrator-tier substrate is regressing the agent's task quality. Investigate where the regression comes from (likely the brief generation step that frames the task for the agent). Worst case: surface the regression in the README's "Honest gaps" section instead of hiding it.
  - **Measurement**: `bin/minsky competitive --json | jq '.competitors[] | select(.id == "minsky-via-claude") | .resultSource.values."humaneval-pass-at-1"'` returns a number in [0.677, 0.777].
  - **Anchor**: rule #4 (visible — every claim has a primary-source number); operator directive 2026-05-23 ("Do another deep dive on competitors ... Propose vision + user stories + architecture changes"); competitors/README.md § Honest gaps; Chen et al., "Evaluating Large Language Models Trained on Code", arXiv 2107.03374, 2021 (HumanEval); MetaGPT ICLR 2024 (orchestrator-tier reporting convention).

- [ ] `gaia-benchmark-evaluation-substrate` — add `bin/minsky benchmark gaia` to compare to AutoGen's claimed GAIA SOTA + close the orchestrator-tier GAIA gap
  - **ID**: gaia-benchmark-evaluation-substrate
  - **Tags**: p2, milestone-m1, m1-10, metrics, competitive, gap-closing, orchestrator-tier, observed-2026-05-23
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Competitive-goal**: AutoGen claims GAIA SOTA as of March 2024 (Microsoft TRANSPARENCY_FAQS.md). The 2025 HAL leaderboard shows Claude Sonnet 4.5 at 74.55%; Claude Opus 4.7 High at 70.91%. Minsky-via-Claude on GAIA would close the orchestrator-tier GAIA gap. Adding a `gaia-benchmark-score` metric to the catalogue unlocks comparison.
  - **Touches**: `novel/competitive-benchmark/src/metrics.ts` (add `gaia-benchmark-score` metric), `scripts/benchmark-gaia.mjs` (new — the runner), `bin/minsky` (add `benchmark gaia`), `competitors/minsky-via-claude.md` (extend with the GAIA reading).
  - **Details**: GAIA (Mialon et al. 2023, arXiv 2311.12983) is the canonical "General AI Assistant" benchmark — long-horizon tasks across web browsing, reasoning, tool use. AutoGen's GAIA SOTA framing is from March 2024 (pre-Sonnet 4.5); current SOTA is Claude Sonnet 4.5 at 74.55%. Implementation: (a) pull the HAL leaderboard's validation set (165 questions); (b) score Minsky-via-Claude with the existing handoff substrate; (c) record the result. Key insight: GAIA is THE benchmark for the orchestrator-tier moat (long-horizon work), unlike HumanEval which is per-task code-gen.
  - **Hypothesis**: Minsky-via-Claude on GAIA scores within ±5pp of bare Claude Sonnet 4.5's 74.55% — if it scores significantly BELOW, Minsky's orchestrator-tier substrate is HURTING long-horizon work (which would be the worst outcome since long-horizon IS the moat). If it scores SIGNIFICANTLY ABOVE (≥+5pp), Minsky's MAPE-K + multi-iteration substrate is genuinely adding value on the type of task the moat targets.
  - **Success**: `bin/minsky benchmark gaia` produces a JSON report with overall accuracy ∈ [0.696, 0.796] (Claude Sonnet 4.5 baseline ± 5pp); the result lands in the M1.10 corpus as a new `gaia-benchmark-score` metric.
  - **Pivot**: if Minsky-via-Claude scores >7pp BELOW bare Claude Sonnet 4.5 on GAIA, the orchestrator-tier substrate is HURTING long-horizon work — this is the worst possible outcome (since long-horizon is the moat). Pivot: trace which iteration's overhead is causing the regression (likely the persona-spawning latency); consider a "fast-path" code-gen path that bypasses persona handoffs for benchmark-tier tasks.
  - **Measurement**: `bin/minsky competitive --json | jq '.competitors[] | select(.id == "minsky-via-claude") | .resultSource.values."gaia-benchmark-score"'` returns a number in [0.696, 0.796].
  - **Anchor**: rule #4 (visible); rule #9 (the GAIA score IS the test of the orchestrator-tier moat — if Minsky doesn't beat bare Claude on GAIA, the moat doesn't help users); operator directive 2026-05-23; competitors/README.md § Honest gaps; Mialon, Fourrier, Swift, Wolf, LeCun, Scialom, "GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants", arXiv 2311.12983, 2023; HAL: GAIA Leaderboard, hal.cs.princeton.edu/gaia (current SOTA numbers).

- [ ] `explore-multi-agent-ensembling-experiment` — investigate whether the Augment Code pattern (Sonnet driver + o1 ensembler) lifts Minsky-via-Claude's benchmark scores
  - **ID**: explore-multi-agent-ensembling-experiment
  - **Tags**: p2, milestone-m2, m2-experiment, metrics, competitive, gap-closing, orchestrator-tier, observed-2026-05-23
  - **Milestone**: M2 (gated — not M1 work; M1 is stability + measurability)
  - **Competitive-goal**: closes the multi-agent-ensembling gap. Augment Code's 65.4% SWE-bench Verified (open-source SOTA at submission) combines Claude Sonnet 3.7 driver + OpenAI o1 ensembler. Minsky uses ONE agent per task. Question: does adopting the pattern (and modifying the persona-spawner adapter to support ensembling) raise Minsky's HumanEval / SWE-bench / GAIA scores by ≥5pp?
  - **Touches**: `novel/adapters/persona-spawner/src/index.ts` (extend to support ensembling), `experiments/multi-agent-ensembling-2026-05-23.yaml` (the experiment record), `scripts/benchmark-humaneval.mjs` (re-run with ensembling on/off).
  - **Details**: Two-spawn ensembling: spawn Claude as the driver, spawn o1 (or GPT-5) as the ensembler, both see the same task brief; the ensembler reviews the driver's diff and proposes a synthesis. Augment Code's published methodology is at github.com/augmentcode/augment-swebench-agent — Python implementation reads "fork of Anthropic's SWE-bench post + sequential-thinking MCP tool". Implementation steps: (a) extend persona-spawner adapter with `ensemble: { driver, ensembler }` config; (b) run the experiment WITHOUT ensembling first (control); (c) run WITH ensembling; (d) compare. Expect this to ROUGHLY DOUBLE per-task cost (two spawns vs one) — measurement must include cost-per-merged-pr delta.
  - **Hypothesis**: Multi-agent ensembling lifts Minsky-via-Claude's HumanEval Pass@1 by ≥5pp at ≤2x the per-task cost. Augment Code's 65.4% SWE-bench with ensembling vs Claude 3.7's published bare 0.69-0.73 range suggests a ~2-5pp lift; the open question is whether this scales to HumanEval (which is per-task code-gen, not multi-file refactor like SWE-bench).
  - **Success**: HumanEval Pass@1 with ensembling > HumanEval Pass@1 without ensembling by ≥5pp AND cost-per-merged-pr stays within 2.5x of baseline. Documented as a flag in `~/.minsky/config.json` (`ensembling: true|false`); default off until M2 lands.
  - **Pivot**: if ensembling raises pass@1 by <2pp OR cost-per-task >3x baseline, the pattern is NOT WORTH the operational complexity for Minsky's surface. Reject the pattern; close this task; document the rejection in competitors/augment-code.md § "What we learn / steal".
  - **Measurement**: A/B benchmark with the same HumanEval problem set: `bin/minsky benchmark humaneval --ensembling on` vs `bin/minsky benchmark humaneval --ensembling off`. JSON diff of `pass_at_1` and `total_cost_usd` must satisfy `pass_at_1_delta >= 0.05 AND cost_ratio <= 2.5`.
  - **Anchor**: rule #1 (don't reinvent — explore the proven Augment Code pattern before inventing new); rule #9 (pre-registered HDD); operator directive 2026-05-23; competitors/augment-code.md § "What we learn / steal"; Chen & Flaherty, "#1 open-source agent on SWE-Bench Verified by combining Claude 3.7 and O1", augmentcode.com/blog, 2025-03-31.

- [ ] `enterprise-deployment-readiness-audit` — audit what would make Minsky deployable at enterprise scale (CrewAI 60% Fortune 500 is the bar)
  - **ID**: enterprise-deployment-readiness-audit
  - **Tags**: p2, milestone-m2, m2-distribution, gap-closing, observed-2026-05-23
  - **Milestone**: M2 (gated — M1 is stable + measurable + one-command; M2 is when distribution becomes the next concern)
  - **Competitive-goal**: closes the enterprise-distribution gap. CrewAI Enterprise has 60% of Fortune 500 + 1.4B+ agentic executions (per crewai.com/blog/crewai-oss-1-0). Devin Enterprise is multi-tenant (Cognition Cloud) + customer-dedicated VPC. Minsky has ~1 deployment. This task scopes what would have to change for Minsky to be deployable on 10+ enterprises without the operator-machine-identity moat becoming a liability.
  - **Touches**: `docs/enterprise-deployment-options.md` (new — the audit output), `vision.md` § "What Minsky uniquely does" (cross-reference if the audit changes the moat framing), possibly: `novel/cross-repo-runner/` (multi-operator mode if the audit identifies it as the blocking gap).
  - **Details**: 2026-05-23 strategic deep-dive surfaced the gap. Audit dimensions: (a) **Identity model** — Minsky's operator-machine-identity moat (user-story 012) works for individual operators but not for "Minsky deployed to a team of 50 developers". What would multi-operator mode look like? Per-operator daemons + shared TASKS.md? Centralised orchestrator + per-operator agents? (b) **Audit logs** — Minsky writes `orchestrate.jsonl` per host; enterprise audit needs centralized log shipping (OpenObserve already in the stack). (c) **Secret management** — Minsky reads operator's `~/.config/gh/hosts.yml`. Enterprise needs vault integration (1Password Connect, AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). (d) **Compliance posture** — SOC2 / ISO 27001 readiness; the operator-machine-identity moat HELPS here (data stays on-prem) but the launchd/systemd model needs documentation. (e) **Pricing model** — Minsky is MIT-licensed today; enterprise distribution typically requires a commercial license or SaaS hosting.
  - **Hypothesis**: ≤3 architectural changes (multi-operator mode + vault integration + audit log shipping) are sufficient to make Minsky enterprise-deployable. Changes >3 indicate the operator-machine-identity moat IS the wrong moat for enterprise distribution; that's a vision-level pivot, not a feature-list extension.
  - **Success**: `docs/enterprise-deployment-options.md` enumerates the architectural changes; the changes are filed as M2 tasks (not committed-to but visible); if the audit identifies ≤3 changes, file them as P1 M2-tagged tasks; if >3 changes, file ONE meta-task that asks "should Minsky pursue enterprise distribution at all".
  - **Pivot**: if the audit identifies that the operator-machine-identity moat fundamentally conflicts with enterprise distribution (e.g., enterprises require a centralized control plane that voids the moat), STOP pursuing enterprise distribution. Reframe Minsky's positioning as "the operator-tier orchestrator" — leave enterprise to CrewAI / Devin Enterprise. The honest gap stays in the corpus.
  - **Measurement**: `docs/enterprise-deployment-options.md` exists; it enumerates ≤3 changes (success) OR documents the moat conflict (pivot).
  - **Anchor**: rule #1 (don't reinvent — CrewAI Enterprise + Devin Enterprise are the reference shapes; understand their trade-offs before designing); operator directive 2026-05-23; competitors/README.md § Honest gaps; competitors/crewai.md § Production architecture; Cognition Labs, "Enterprise Deployment Overview", docs.devin.ai/enterprise/deployment/overview, 2026.

- [ ] `corpus-add-autogen-microsoft` — add Microsoft AutoGen to the orchestrator-tier corpus once a primary-cited HumanEval, MBPP, or GAIA number is available
  - **ID**: corpus-add-autogen-microsoft
  - **Tags**: p2, milestone-m1, m1-10, metrics, competitive, corpus-add, orchestrator-tier, observed-2026-05-23
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Competitive-goal**: covers Microsoft's enterprise multi-agent orchestration framework (AutoGen, github.com/microsoft/autogen, very large adoption per Microsoft Research). The Wu et al. 2023 paper (arXiv 2308.08155) reports MATH 69.48% and ALFWorld 77% as primary numbers, but the M1.10 catalogue's orchestrator-tier metric is `humaneval-pass-at-1`. Microsoft's TRANSPARENCY_FAQS.md claims "SOTA results on GAIA as of March 1, 2024" without an exact percentage; the Magentic-One follow-up paper has specific GAIA numbers but is a SEPARATE system built on top of AutoGen.
  - **Touches**: `novel/competitive-benchmark/src/competitors.ts` (add the entry), `competitors/autogen.md` (new research file). Optionally: add `gaia-benchmark-score` metric to `novel/competitive-benchmark/src/metrics.ts` if AutoGen's GAIA number is the primary path.
  - **Details**: 2026-05-23 orchestrator-tier sweep found AutoGen has substantial primary documentation (Microsoft Research paper, TRANSPARENCY_FAQS, AutoGenBench tool) but no headline `humaneval-pass-at-1` number — HumanEval results are model-dependent (run via AutoGenBench tool against the model of choice). Two acceptable resolution paths: (a) cite the Microsoft 2023 paper's MATH/ALFWorld primary numbers and add new `math-whole-test` or `alfworld-success` metrics to the catalogue; (b) wait for a Magentic-One-style follow-up that publishes a headline HumanEval number for "AutoGen on stock models". Pivot-resistant: don't fabricate an AutoGen HumanEval number by running AutoGenBench locally — that's a `local-harness` result kind which requires a `harnessId` per the corpus contract.
  - **Hypothesis**: Microsoft publishes a Magentic-One-class follow-up paper with headline HumanEval / GAIA numbers within 6 months. If they don't, add AutoGen with the MATH 69.48% number via a new metric.
  - **Success**: `bin/minsky competitive --json | jq '.competitors[] | select(.id == "autogen-microsoft")'` returns a competitor record with `resultSource.kind == "published"` and a Microsoft-primary citation.
  - **Pivot**: if 6 months pass without a Microsoft-primary HumanEval / GAIA number, add AutoGen with the MATH 69.48% reading via a new `math-whole-test-accuracy` metric (Hendrycks et al. MATH dataset; Wu et al. arXiv 2308.08155 as primary). Adopting MATH as a sibling to HumanEval also unlocks future per-orchestrator math-reasoning comparisons.
  - **Measurement**: `bin/minsky competitive --json | jq '.competitors | map(select(.id == "autogen-microsoft")) | length'` ≥ `1`.
  - **Anchor**: rule #4 (visible — primary citations only); rule #6 (stay alive — without periodic vendor-publication checks, this task ages out); Wu, Bansal, Zhang, Wu, Li, Zhu, Wang, Saied, Awadallah, Yang, "AutoGen: Enabling Next-Gen LLM Applications via Multi-Agent Conversation Framework", microsoft.com/en-us/research/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/LLM_agent.pdf, 2023 (current primary); operator directive 2026-05-23.

- [ ] `biome-1-to-2-upgrade` — upgrade biome 1.9.4 → 2.x to unlock type-aware nursery rules (`noFloatingPromises`, `noMisusedPromises`, `useAwaitThenable`, `noUnnecessaryConditions`, `useExhaustiveSwitchCases`) and per-domain rule packs
  - **ID**: biome-1-to-2-upgrade
  - **Tags**: p2, lint, lint-hardening, biome, observed-2026-05-23
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Competitive-goal**: keeps Minsky's lint stack at parity with the 2026 industry-standard TypeScript stack (pocketarc.com/typescript, "Hardcore TypeScript + Biome + ESLint" 2026 — the type-aware nursery rules are the highest-ROI bug catchers shipped in biome 2.x; without them Minsky relies on tsc + biome 1.9.4's recommended set, which doesn't catch unhandled promises or async-without-await type-aware violations).
  - **Touches**: `biome.json` (migrate to biome 2.x config format — top-level `$schema` URL update, `linter.domains` block for `types: "all"` / `project: "all"`, possible reorganization of rule overrides), `package.json` (bump `@biomejs/biome` to `^2.x`), all `// biome-ignore lint/...` comments may need to be re-verified.
  - **Details**: biome 2.x is breaking vs 1.x — the config schema changed enough that the existing `biome.json` won't validate against the 2.x schema. The migration path: (a) `pnpm dlx @biomejs/biome migrate --write` to auto-migrate biome.json; (b) explicitly opt into the nursery `types` and `project` domains (`linter.domains: { types: "all", project: "all" }`) to enable type-aware rules; (c) re-run `pnpm exec biome ci .` and triage the new findings — expect 50-200 new violations on `noFloatingPromises` alone; (d) lower-severity-prone rules (e.g., `useExhaustiveSwitchCases`) start at WARN per the rule-#10 ratchet. Pivot threshold: if the post-migration violation count exceeds 1000, defer to a multi-PR drain.
  - **Hypothesis**: biome 2.x catches ≥5 floating-promise bugs the 1.9.4 substrate misses today. Real-world data: every `// TODO: unhandled async` comment in the codebase OR every existing `useAwait`-warned function (currently 155) is a candidate for type-aware promise tracking.
  - **Success**: `pnpm exec biome --version` returns `Version: 2.x.y`; `pnpm exec biome ci .` exits 0 (with all new rules either passing or explicitly at WARN per the ratchet); the upgrade ships in one PR with a 1-day-old vitest run as the rollback signal.
  - **Pivot**: if the migration produces >1000 new errors that can't be auto-fixed, split into multiple PRs (one per rule family). If the type-aware rules slow `pnpm pre-pr-lint --stage=fast` past the 2-minute target, move the type-aware subset to `--stage=full` only (CI runs it, daemon doesn't).
  - **Measurement**: `pnpm exec biome --version | grep -E "^Version: 2"` exits 0 AND `pnpm exec biome ci .` exits 0 with ≥1 enabled rule from the nursery `types` domain visible in `biome.json` after migration.
  - **Anchor**: rule #1 (don't reinvent — biome is the established lint engine; we move forward with its versions, not fork or stay stuck); rule #10 (deterministic enforcement — the 2.x type-aware rules expand the lint surface, exactly what rule #10 wants); operator directive 2026-05-23 ("install all those recommendations to minsky"); Code With Seb, "Advanced Linting & Type Safety: The Strictest ESLint + TypeScript + Prettier Setup in 2026", codewithseb.com, 2026 (the 2026 stack baseline); PocketArC, "Hardcore TypeScript + Biome + ESLint: Maximum-strictness static analysis", pocketarc.com/typescript, 2026 (biome 2.x type-aware nursery rule recommendation list).

- [ ] `lint-warnings-drain-to-zero` — drain the 199 biome warnings (155 `useAwait` + 38 `noEmptyBlockStatements` + 6 misc) introduced by the lint-hardening PR to zero (or to a documented-exemption set of ≤5)
  - **ID**: lint-warnings-drain-to-zero
  - **Tags**: p2, lint, lint-hardening, biome, warnings-drain, observed-2026-05-23
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Competitive-goal**: rule #10's ratchet — every new rule should converge to zero violations within 60 days of its introduction, otherwise it's training the operator (and the agent) to ignore lint output. The 199 warnings currently land at WARN; this task is the explicit drain trajectory.
  - **Touches**: ~50-100 files across `novel/**/*.test.ts`, `novel/**/*.ts`, `scripts/**/*.mjs` — wherever the 155 `useAwait` and 38 `noEmptyBlockStatements` violations live. No biome.json change in this task (that's `biome-1-to-2-upgrade`'s job).
  - **Details**: the 2026-05-23 lint-hardening PR (this set of changes) added 13 new biome rules. Most code is clean; 199 warnings remain spread across the corpus. The drain strategy: (a) `useAwait` violations are usually `async` annotations on functions that don't actually need them (mocks, test fixtures, simple thunks) — most can be fixed by removing `async` OR by adding `await new Promise(r => setImmediate(r))` for the small subset that legitimately need to be async without an awaitable call site (yields the event loop). (b) `noEmptyBlockStatements` violations are mostly intentional no-op stream impls (`new Readable({ read() {} })`) and abstract method placeholders — these should get a `// biome-ignore lint/suspicious/noEmptyBlockStatements: intentional no-op for Readable contract` comment. (c) Track the drain in this task's body: target is 199 → 0 (or ≤5 with documented exemptions per rule-#10 ratchet) within 60 days.
  - **Hypothesis**: the 199-warning corpus drains to ≤5 in 60 days (by 2026-07-22) under the existing tick-loop. If it doesn't, the rules were too noisy and at least some should be removed from `biome.json` (the alternative pivot).
  - **Success**: `pnpm exec biome ci . 2>&1 | grep -oE "Found [0-9]+ warnings\." | head -1` reports ≤5 warnings.
  - **Pivot**: if 60 days pass with the warning count >50, demote the noisiest rule family (`useAwait` if it's still dominating) to advisory-only by removing it from `biome.json`. A permanent-warn rule is worse than no rule — it trains the operator to ignore the lint output, which is rule #4's exact failure mode.
  - **Measurement**: `pnpm exec biome ci . 2>&1 | grep -E "^Found .* warnings\." | grep -oE "[0-9]+" | head -1` ≤ `5` after 60 days.
  - **Anchor**: rule #4 (visible — warning count is a tracked metric, not a noisy fixed cost); rule #10 (deterministic enforcement — every rule is a load-bearing gate, not advisory permanent warnings); operator directive 2026-05-23; vision.md row 96 (the substrate this task drains).

- [ ] `corpus-add-github-copilot-coding-agent` — add GitHub Copilot Coding Agent (Microsoft) to the M1.10 corpus with a Microsoft-primary or AIDev-paper citation
  - **ID**: corpus-add-github-copilot-coding-agent
  - **Tags**: p2, milestone-m1, m1-10, metrics, competitive, corpus-add, observed-2026-05-22
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Competitive-goal**: thickens the M1.10 scorecard's per-competitor cell density with one of the largest deployed autonomous-coding agents by user count (4.7M paid subscribers per skillpack.co secondary); without it the scorecard's competitor set systematically undersamples the GitHub-default enterprise path.
  - **Touches**: `novel/competitive-benchmark/src/competitors.ts` (add the entry), `competitors/github-copilot-coding-agent.md` (new research file), `competitors/openai-codex.md` (cross-reference if relevant).
  - **Details**: the 2026-05-22 competitor-research sweep found two strong candidates blocked on primary citation: GitHub Copilot Coding Agent (no SWE-bench Verified number cited in github.blog's launch post; multiple secondary sources put it at ~56%, including TIMEWELL Inc. benchmark column and paperclipped.de) and CodeMate.ai's `swe-benchmarking` repo (`github.com/CodeMate-AI/swe-benchmarking`) which ran Copilot GPT-4.1 at 72/100 = 0.72 on a 100-instance subset. Two acceptable resolution paths: (a) fetch the AIDev paper PDF (`arxiv.org/abs/2602.02345` — "A Task-Level Evaluation of AI Agents in Open-Source Projects") which evaluates Copilot's PR acceptance rate on the AIDev-pop dataset (same primary-citation shape as claude-code and cursor-agent already in the corpus); (b) wait for Microsoft to publish a SWE-bench Verified number in a github.blog research post. Then invoke `/competitor-research https://github.blog/news-insights/product-news/github-copilot-meet-the-new-coding-agent/` with the chosen primary citation.
  - **Hypothesis**: adding github-copilot-coding-agent with a primary-cited reading raises the corpus to ≥9 competitors and adds ≥1 reading on either `swe-bench-verified-resolve-rate` or `autonomous-merge-rate`. The Pinna et al. AIDev-pop arxiv 2602.08915 paper (already cited for claude-code and cursor-agent) likely reports Copilot's per-task acceptance rate in the same table that gave us 0.726 for claude-code — extracting it is the lowest-cost path.
  - **Success**: `bin/minsky competitive --json | jq '.competitors[] | select(.id == "github-copilot-coding-agent")'` returns a competitor record with `resultSource.kind == "published"`, a Microsoft / GitHub blog OR Pinna 2026 / Rahman 2026 arxiv citation, and ≥1 `values.<metric-id>` entry.
  - **Pivot**: if no primary citation is available within 60 days, document the blocker in `competitors/github-copilot-coding-agent.md` and close this task without adding the competitor — the validator's published-only rule (rule #4 visible, no fabricated readings) takes precedence over corpus growth (rule #6 stay alive applies to existing entries, not aspirational ones).
  - **Measurement**: `bin/minsky competitive --json | jq '.competitors | map(select(.id == "github-copilot-coding-agent")) | length'` ≥ `1`.
  - **Anchor**: rule #4 (visible — every reading carries a primary citation; no fabricated numbers); operator directive 2026-05-22 ("add a mechanism so that minsky keeps competitors list updated and competitors there too" — this task is one of the per-vendor adds the discovery sweep surfaced); Pinna, Gong, Williams, Sarro, "Comparing AI Coding Agents", arXiv 2602.08915 (the AIDev paper already cited for claude-code and cursor-agent — likely contains Copilot data in the same table).

- [ ] `corpus-add-goose-block` — add Goose (Block) to the M1.10 corpus once Block publishes an official SWE-bench Verified resolve rate
  - **ID**: corpus-add-goose-block
  - **Tags**: p2, milestone-m1, m1-10, metrics, competitive, corpus-add, blocked-on-vendor, observed-2026-05-22
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Competitive-goal**: covers the open-source / local-first autonomous-coding agent space (37,400+ GitHub stars per aiforautomation.io, the AAIF-governed alternative to Claude Code) which Minsky currently leaves uncovered in the corpus.
  - **Touches**: `novel/competitive-benchmark/src/competitors.ts` (add the entry), `competitors/goose-block.md` (new research file).
  - **Details**: 2026-05-22 sweep found Goose has substantial adoption (37.4k stars, MIT-license, Block-governed) but **Block has not published official SWE-bench results**. The ~45% figure cited by Morph (third-party comparison) and the public `block/goose` GitHub discussion #3133 (where the maintainer says they "are trying to regularly run the tbench benchmark") are not Block-primary. The competitor-research validator (rule #4 — visible, no fabricated readings) rejects readings without a primary citation. Two acceptable resolution paths: (a) Block publishes an official SWE-bench number on their Block / Goose blog → invoke `/competitor-research <url>`; (b) Block publishes a tbench result (their preferred internal benchmark) and we adopt tbench as a sibling metric to `swe-bench-verified-resolve-rate`. Pivot-resistant: do NOT add Goose with the third-party 45% number; that would erode the corpus's primary-citation invariant.
  - **Hypothesis**: Block publishes an official SWE-bench (or tbench) number within 6 months of this task being filed, given the public commitment in discussion #3133. If they don't, Goose stays out of the corpus until they do.
  - **Success**: `bin/minsky competitive --json | jq '.competitors[] | select(.id == "goose-block")'` returns a competitor record with `resultSource.kind == "published"` and a Block-primary citation (`block.xyz/blog` or `github.com/block/goose/...`).
  - **Pivot**: if 6 months pass with no Block-primary publication, this task is closed without adding Goose. The corpus's `swe-bench-verified-resolve-rate` cell for Goose remains empty (`undefined` per Helland 2007 visible-not-silent) — the corpus is honest about what is and is not measured.
  - **Measurement**: `bin/minsky competitive --json | jq '.competitors | map(select(.id == "goose-block")) | length'` ≥ `1`.
  - **Anchor**: rule #4 (visible — published primary citation only); rule #6 (stay alive — without periodic vendor-publication check, this task ages out); Helland, P., "Memories, Guesses, and Apologies", CIDR, 2007 (visible-not-silent — don't backfill with synthetic numbers when the vendor declines to publish); operator directive 2026-05-22.

- [ ] `corpus-add-factory-droid` — add Factory Code Droid to the M1.10 corpus once Factory publishes a SWE-bench Verified number (currently only Full + Lite are published)
  - **ID**: corpus-add-factory-droid
  - **Tags**: p2, milestone-m1, m1-10, metrics, competitive, corpus-add, blocked-on-vendor, observed-2026-05-22
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Competitive-goal**: covers the enterprise CI/CD-integrated autonomous-coding agent space (Factory.ai's Code Droid product); thickens the closed-commercial competitor set without padding via synthetic metrics.
  - **Touches**: `novel/competitive-benchmark/src/competitors.ts` (add the entry), `competitors/factory-droid.md` (new research file).
  - **Details**: 2026-05-22 sweep found `factory.ai/news/code-droid-technical-report` (2024-06) cites **SWE-bench Full: 19.27%** and **SWE-bench Lite: 31.67% pass@1 (37.67% pass@2)**, but does NOT cite a SWE-bench Verified resolve rate. The M1.10 corpus's primary metric is `swe-bench-verified-resolve-rate`; adding Factory with a Lite-as-Verified-proxy (the same compromise made for aider) would double the proxy debt. Acceptable resolution: wait for Factory to publish a SWE-bench Verified number, then invoke `/competitor-research https://factory.ai/news/code-droid-technical-report` with `--refresh` (or whichever URL carries the Verified-split publication).
  - **Hypothesis**: Factory publishes a SWE-bench Verified number within 9 months of this task being filed, given the 2024-06 technical report's "we will continue evaluating against SWE-bench" framing. If they don't, Factory stays out of the corpus.
  - **Success**: `bin/minsky competitive --json | jq '.competitors[] | select(.id == "factory-droid") | .resultSource.values."swe-bench-verified-resolve-rate"'` returns a number from a Factory-primary citation (not a third-party site like awesomeagents.ai).
  - **Pivot**: if 9 months pass with no Factory Verified-split publication, close this task without adding Factory. Do NOT use the SWE-bench Lite 0.3167 as a Verified-rate proxy — the aider precedent (rule #4 visible) is already the maximum-acceptable proxy debt in the corpus.
  - **Measurement**: same as Success.
  - **Anchor**: rule #4 (visible — no proxying Verified rates from non-Verified runs without an explicit aider-style precedent caveat); operator directive 2026-05-22; Factory AI, "Code Droid: A Technical Report", factory.ai/news/code-droid-technical-report, 2024-06 (current published readings; awaiting Verified-split update).

- [ ] `corpus-discover-quarterly` — quarterly: scan the autonomous-coding-agent landscape for NEW competitors that should be added to the M1.10 scorecard corpus, and surface them as `/competitor-research <url>` candidates
  - **ID**: corpus-discover-quarterly
  - **Tags**: p2, milestone-m1, m1-10, metrics, competitive, corpus-discovery, recurring
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Competitive-goal**: keeps the M1.10 corpus COMPETITOR LIST current (the freshness loop handles the per-vendor readings; this task handles whether the right set of vendors is in the corpus at all). A list frozen at 6 vendors loses signal as new autonomous-coding agents launch (e.g., GitHub Copilot coding agent, OpenAI Codex, MetaGPT v2, etc.).
  - **Touches**: `novel/competitive-benchmark/src/competitors.ts` (additions only), `competitors/<new-id>.md` (one per added vendor), and the `/competitor-research` skill (no edits — just invoked).
  - **Details**: every quarter the operator (or the tick-loop's `/next-task` picks this up after the 90-day mark) runs a discovery sweep: (a) `gh search repos "autonomous coding agent" --sort=updated --limit=20` and the equivalent for `WebSearch` against news + Hacker News; (b) filter out anything already in `COMPETITORS` (via `bin/minsky competitive --json | jq '.competitors[].id'`); (c) filter against `EXCLUDED_VENDOR_SUBSTRINGS` (no Groq/xAI/Elon-affiliated); (d) for each surviving candidate, invoke `/competitor-research <vendor-url>` to add it. Acceptance: ≥1 new competitor evaluated per quarter (added OR explicitly rejected with a note in the research file).
  - **Hypothesis**: today the corpus is 6 vendors. After 4 quarters of running this task, the corpus is ≥9 vendors (≥1 addition per quarter on average; rejections don't grow the corpus but DO produce a research file documenting the why).
  - **Success**: `bin/minsky competitive --json | jq '.competitors | length'` increases by ≥1 each quarter, OR a new `competitors/<id>.md` research file documents an explicit rejection (with vendor name + why-not).
  - **Pivot**: if the autonomous-coding landscape consolidates such that 2 consecutive quarters yield 0 net-new credible vendors, switch this task from quarterly to semi-annual cadence. Don't pad the corpus with marginal players just to hit the quarterly addition target — vanity adds dilute the comparison signal (rule #9 — vanity metrics forbidden).
  - **Measurement**: `bin/minsky competitive --json | jq '.competitors | length'` ≥ `7` after Q3 2026, ≥ `8` after Q4 2026, ≥ `9` after Q1 2027 (assumes ≥1 addition per quarter starting Q3 2026 when this task ships).
  - **Anchor**: rule #4 (visible — the corpus's coverage IS a measurable scoreboard metric); rule #6 (stay alive — without periodic discovery, the corpus snapshots the autonomous-coding market at a single point and stops being useful as the market evolves); operator directive 2026-05-22 ("add a mechanism so that minsky keeps competitors list updated and competitors there too" — this task is the LIST half; the `corpus-refresh-*` auto-filed tasks are the READINGS half); Doerr 2018 *Measure What Matters* (OKR — set the cadence target explicitly so progress is measurable, not aspirational).

- [ ] `tui-src-vs-test-api-drift-pivot-tracker` — track the orphan-tests detector's false-positive rate over the next 5 PRs; if >2 false positives accumulate, switch from regex-based detection to a tsc-based detector (per-package `tsconfig.test.json` + `tsc --noEmit` over `test/`).
  - **ID**: tui-src-vs-test-api-drift-pivot-tracker
  - **Tags**: p2, lint, rule-10, pivot-tracker, observed-2026-05-21
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-21 PM PR-drain — PR #713 shipped `scripts/check-orphan-tests.mjs`, a regex-based lint that catches the test/ vs src/ API drift class (the PR #639 → #705 regression). The lint declares a pivot threshold in its top-of-file comment: ">2 false positives over a 5-PR window → switch to tsc-based". This task is the tracker so the threshold is actually monitored.
  - **Hypothesis**: regex-based ESM import/export parsing is sufficient for the orphan-test class; the source-of-drift surface is small (named runtime imports from a parallel directory) and TypeScript's edge cases (default imports, namespace imports, dynamic imports) are out of scope for this lint.
  - **Success**: 5 consecutive PRs land with the `orphan-tests` CI job either green or red-for-a-real-orphan, no false positives.
  - **Pivot**: if any single PR triggers a false positive, file an immediate diagnostic task. If 3 false positives across <5 PRs, abandon the regex approach and ship the tsc-based detector (separate `tsconfig.test.json` per `novel/*` package that has a `test/` dir, run `tsc --noEmit` over the test files in `check-orphan-tests.mjs`).
  - **Measurement**: `gh run list --workflow=ci --json conclusion,databaseId --jq '[.[] | select(.conclusion == "FAILURE")] | length'` over the next 5 PRs touching `novel/**/test/`. Cross-reference any failure against the actual orphan-symbol report to classify true-vs-false positive.
  - **Anchor**: rule #9 (pre-registered pivot threshold — the lint declared its own pivot in its top-of-file Pivot comment); rule #10 (deterministic enforcement — every rule is mechanically checked); novel/tui scaffolding from PR #639 is the source of the original regression.
  - **Files**: `scripts/check-orphan-tests.mjs` (the lint to pivot from), `scripts/check-orphan-tests.test.mjs` (new test cases for the tsc-based detector), `novel/*/tsconfig.test.json` (new per-package configs if pivot fires), `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (rewire the orphan-tests job).

- [ ] `pre-pr-lint-biome-diff-scoped-vs-ci-whole-tree-divergence` — `pnpm pre-pr-lint` runs `biome ci --changed --since=origin/main` (diff-scoped), but the CI `biome` workflow job runs `biome ci .` (whole-tree). Formatting drift in unchanged files survives the local gate and explodes in CI 5 minutes later.
  - **ID**: pre-pr-lint-biome-diff-scoped-vs-ci-whole-tree-divergence
  - **Tags**: p2, lint, biome, pre-pr-lint, ci-divergence, observed-2026-05-21
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-21 PM CI-stabilization sweep — PR #704 had to ship three pre-existing biome violations (in `scripts/heal-mttr-report.mjs`, `.releaserc.json`, `novel/tui/src/index.ts`) that the local diff-scoped gate didn't see because the files weren't in the PR's diff. Each CI failure required a separate fix PR (#705, #706, #707).
  - **Hypothesis**: the diff-scoped local gate is fundamentally a different gate from the whole-tree CI gate; a single PR's locally-green lint stack can't guarantee CI green. Two paths to reconcile: (a) make the local gate whole-tree too (slower but consistent) or (b) keep diff-scoped local but add an explicit `biome ci .` step to the lint stack's `full` stage so the whole-tree pass runs locally before push.
  - **Success**: after the fix, a PR that's clean against `pnpm pre-pr-lint --stage=full` never fails the CI `biome` job for unrelated whole-tree violations. Measurement: 5 consecutive PRs with this invariant.
  - **Pivot**: if making the local gate whole-tree exceeds 60s wall-clock on M2-class hardware (rule-#11 perf budget), keep diff-scoped local + add a separate `pnpm biome:full` script that runs in lefthook pre-push (not pre-commit).
  - **Measurement**: `time pnpm pre-pr-lint --stage=full` p50/p95 over 10 runs after the fix; assert wall-clock budget ≤ 60s.
  - **Anchor**: rule #10 (deterministic enforcement — gates that disagree are non-deterministic); rule #11 (no flaky gates — a gate that says "ok" while the next gate says "fail" is flaky-by-construction); 2026-05-21 PR #704/#706/#707 chain.
  - **Files**: `scripts/run-pre-pr-lint-stack.mjs` (the biome step args), `lefthook.yml` (pre-push hook reuse), `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (the biome job that exposes the whole-tree gap).

- [ ] `lefthook-bot-commit-bypass-discipline` — the experiment workflow's bot-commit step ran the local `check-toolchain` hook inside a linux/x64 CI runner; the hook expected darwin-arm64 optional deps. PR #710 worked around by setting `git config core.hooksPath /dev/null` in the bot's commit step; the right answer is a documented bypass discipline so future bot commits don't recurringly trip this class.
  - **ID**: lefthook-bot-commit-bypass-discipline
  - **Tags**: p2, ci, lefthook, bot-commit, observed-2026-05-21
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-21 PM CI-stabilization — the experiment workflow's `experiment-store` bot commit ran on a fresh linux/x64 runner and tripped lefthook's pre-commit `check-toolchain` hook (which expected the macOS darwin-arm64 optionalDependency). PR #710 fixed by adding `git config core.hooksPath /dev/null` to the bot's commit step, but the fix is local to one workflow.
  - **Hypothesis**: every workflow that bots-commit-back-to-repo (currently: experiment-tracker, fresh-clone, lighthouse) needs the same hook-bypass; a documented pattern + a CI lint that catches workflows missing the bypass would prevent recurrence.
  - **Success**: a deterministic check (new `scripts/check-bot-commit-hook-bypass.mjs`) scans every `.github/workflows/*.yml` for steps that `git commit` AND don't have either `core.hooksPath=/dev/null` set OR `--no-verify` on the commit; the lint fails the CI if any workflow is missing the bypass.
  - **Pivot**: if the bypass discipline is itself error-prone (operator forgets it), move the bot-commit class to a single shared composite action in `.github/actions/bot-commit/action.yml` that always sets `core.hooksPath=/dev/null` + always runs from a darwin-arm64-clean env.
  - **Measurement**: `grep -rE "git commit" .github/workflows/ | grep -v "core.hooksPath=/dev/null\|--no-verify"` should produce 0 lines.
  - **Anchor**: rule #10 (deterministic enforcement); rule #11 (no flaky gates); 2026-05-21 PR #710.
  - **Files**: `scripts/check-bot-commit-hook-bypass.mjs` (new lint), `scripts/check-bot-commit-hook-bypass.test.mjs` (new), `.github/workflows/*.yml` (audit + add the bypass where missing).

- [ ] `glossary-allowlist-update-discipline` — adding a new skill primer in `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` requires a matching entry in `scripts/glossary-allowlist.txt` in the same PR (rule #5). The rule isn't enforced — a forgotten allowlist entry isn't caught until a later PR cites the skill in `vision.md`.
  - **ID**: glossary-allowlist-update-discipline
  - **Tags**: p2, lint, rule-5, glossary, observed-2026-05-21
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-21 — PR #696 added `.claude/skills/pr-merge-no-shortcuts/SKILL.md` and cited it in `vision.md` § 18. The `pr-merge-no-shortcuts` token wasn't added to the allowlist. A subsequent merge dropped the entry; #704 had to re-add it. The cycle of "skill primer ships → allowlist entry forgotten → later PR's rule-5 check fails → re-add" happens every time.
  - **Hypothesis**: a deterministic check that asserts every `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (or `.devin/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`) directory name appears in `scripts/glossary-allowlist.txt` would prevent the recurrence. The check runs in fast-stage in 50ms.
  - **Success**: a lint `scripts/check-skill-allowlist-coverage.mjs` exists; it's red until every skill directory under `.claude/skills/` (or `.devin/skills/`) has its name on the allowlist. After the fix, 0 skill primers can be added without the allowlist entry.
  - **Pivot**: if maintaining the allowlist by hand becomes its own toil, move skill names to a derived list — `node scripts/derive-skill-list.mjs` generates the allowlist subset, committed via lefthook post-commit.
  - **Measurement**: `find .claude/skills .devin/skills -maxdepth 2 -name SKILL.md -exec dirname {} \; | xargs -n1 basename | sort -u > /tmp/actual; grep -oP '^[a-z][a-z0-9-]+$' scripts/glossary-allowlist.txt | sort -u > /tmp/allowed; diff /tmp/actual /tmp/allowed` — should produce 0 lines (every skill directory has its name on the allowlist).
  - **Anchor**: rule #5 (theoretical grounding — every term has a CS anchor; skill names are terms); rule #10 (deterministic enforcement); 2026-05-21 PR #696/#704 cycle.
  - **Files**: `scripts/check-skill-allowlist-coverage.mjs` (new), `scripts/check-skill-allowlist-coverage.test.mjs` (new), `scripts/glossary-allowlist.txt` (the allowlist that the lint checks), `scripts/run-pre-pr-lint-stack.mjs` (wire-in).

- [ ] `release-pat-vs-github-releases-decision` — `@semantic-release/git` was removed from `.releaserc.json` plugins (PR #703) because branch protection blocks the bot's auto-commit to main. The current path uses GitHub Releases only (CHANGELOG.md becomes a frozen artifact). Operator needs to confirm this is the durable choice OR provision a PAT with `admin:write_repo` so the git-commit-back plugin can be re-enabled.
  - **ID**: release-pat-vs-github-releases-decision
  - **Tags**: p2, release, operator-decision, observed-2026-05-21
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-21 PM CI-stabilization — PR #703 removed `@semantic-release/git` from the release plugins because the built-in `GITHUB_TOKEN` couldn't bypass branch protection. The release workflow now ships GitHub Releases only; CHANGELOG.md hasn't been updated since.
  - **Hypothesis**: the operator's actual preference (`"set up github releases for this"`) is the durable path — CHANGELOG.md is a developer-facing artifact and GitHub Releases is the user-facing one. Confirming this lets us delete the unused changelog plugin too. The alternative (PAT) adds credential surface for marginal benefit.
  - **Success**: operator confirms the path; if "stay GitHub-Releases", delete the unused `@semantic-release/changelog` plugin from `.releaserc.json` and update the docs that mention CHANGELOG.md. If "use PAT", provision the secret + re-add the git plugin + update the workflow.
  - **Pivot**: not applicable — this is an operator decision, not a hypothesis.
  - **Measurement**: `cat .releaserc.json | jq '.plugins | length'` matches the decided plugin count; `gh release list --limit 5` shows recent releases; `grep -rl CHANGELOG.md docs/ README.md` returns 0 lines after the docs cleanup.
  - **Anchor**: rule #2 (deps through interfaces — semantic-release plugin choice is a versioning-policy adapter); 2026-05-21 PR #703; operator directive "set up github releases for this".
  - **Files**: `.releaserc.json` (the plugin list), `README.md` (any CHANGELOG.md references), `docs/release.md` (if it exists), `.github/workflows/release.yml` (the workflow if PAT path chosen).

- [ ] `squash-merge-file-list-verifier` — observed 2026-05-21: PR #704's source commit had `.releaserc.json` in its `--stat`, but the squash-merge commit on main did NOT include it. The file appeared identical to base after some intermediate state. Need a lint that catches when a squash-merge drops files from the PR's diff.
  - **ID**: squash-merge-file-list-verifier
  - **Tags**: p2, gh, merge-verification, observed-2026-05-21
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-21 PM PR-drain — PR #704 was supposed to land a fix to `.releaserc.json`'s biome-formatted shape. The PR's commit DID include the change, but the squash-merge commit on main didn't. Result: PR #706 had to re-ship the same `.releaserc.json` fix. The squash-merge dropped the file silently because GitHub's squash-merge re-merges parallel main churn into the squashed body.
  - **Hypothesis**: after every squash-merge, a verifier can compare the PR's source-commit file list (from `gh pr view <N> --json files`) against the resulting squash-merge commit's `git show <sha> --name-only`. Any file in the PR's diff that's NOT in the squash-commit is a drop. The verifier can post a comment on the PR (or open a follow-up PR) flagging the drop.
  - **Success**: the verifier runs on every push to main, checks the last 5 squash-merges, and posts a PR comment if any file was dropped. After landing, 0 silent file drops across 20 consecutive squash-merges.
  - **Pivot**: if GitHub's squash-merge behavior is the root cause and not fixable client-side, move to merge-commits-only with `--ff-only` rebase requirement, accepting the linear-history loss.
  - **Measurement**: `gh pr view <N> --json files --jq '.files[].path' | sort > /tmp/pr-files; git show <merge-sha> --name-only --format= | sort > /tmp/squash-files; diff /tmp/pr-files /tmp/squash-files` — files in PR but not in squash = drop.
  - **Anchor**: rule #17 (proactive healing — observation IS the fix); rule #10 (deterministic enforcement — every dropped file is mechanically detectable); 2026-05-21 PR #704 → #706 ghost-fix-class.
  - **Files**: `scripts/verify-squash-merge-completeness.mjs` (new), `scripts/verify-squash-merge-completeness.test.mjs` (new), `.github/workflows/squash-merge-verifier.yml` (new workflow that runs on every push to main), `vision.md` § 18 (cross-link: this is the deterministic gate that prevents the "merge dropped my file" subclass of rule-18).

<!-- 9-hour monitoring window 2026-05-06 22:00 → 2026-05-07 ~07:30 surfaced these P2 ergonomics findings — improvements but not on the critical path. -->

- [ ] `pre-pr-lint-stack-vitest-hangs-vs-standalone` — `pnpm pre-pr-lint --stage=full` hangs in its `vitest` subprocess (10+ minutes, no output) while `npx vitest run` standalone completes in 33s with 3507/3518 passing
  - **ID**: pre-pr-lint-stack-vitest-hangs-vs-standalone
  - **Tags**: p2, infra, pre-pr-lint, vitest, observed-2026-05-20
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-20 verify cycle for `agents-can-self-heal-minsky-m1-13` (PR opening from `plan/agents-self-heal-minsky`). Standalone `npx vitest run --testTimeout=15000 --bail=1 --reporter=verbose` completed in 33.56s with `3507 passed | 2 skipped | 9 todo (3518)`. The same vitest invoked by `scripts/run-pre-pr-lint-stack.mjs`'s `vitest` step (via `pnpm test`) hung for >10 min in two separate runs; killing the process and re-running standalone reproduced the green result. All other 45 gates in the lint stack ran <2.5s.
  - **Details**: Diagnose why `pnpm test` invoked by the lint stack hangs but the same vitest invoked directly doesn't.
