Suspects: (a) the lint stack runs vitest under `tee` to a file, which may block stdin pipe / buffering; (b) `vitest.config.ts` has `retry: 2` which can multiply duration on a flaky test, possibly compounding under the stack's runner; (c) coverage instrumentation kicks in only via `pnpm test` (not `npx vitest run`) and hangs on a specific worker; (d) lefthook pre-pr-lint pre-push gates re-invoke vitest recursively. Find the root cause; fix it OR add a `--reporter=basic --bail=1` to the lint stack's vitest invocation so it surfaces hangs as test failures within 60s.
  - **Files**: `scripts/run-pre-pr-lint-stack.mjs` (the lint stack runner), `vitest.config.ts` (retry config), `package.json` (`test` script), `lefthook.yml` (pre-push wiring).
  - **Hypothesis**: a specific test or worker in the full vitest suite blocks indefinitely when stdout is piped through `tee`, but not when output goes directly to the terminal. Falsifiable: `pnpm test 2>&1 | tee /tmp/x.log` reproduces the hang while `pnpm test` (no pipe) completes; OR running `npx vitest run` with the exact `pnpm test` args reproduces the hang.
  - **Success**: `pnpm pre-pr-lint --stage=full` completes (success or failure) within 5 minutes wall-clock on the M2-class fleet host. Falsifiable: run 10 times; ≥9 complete within 5 min OR a deterministic failure is reported.
  - **Pivot**: if the hang is in vitest itself (not the stack's runner), file upstream + work around by running `npx vitest run` instead of `pnpm test` in the stack.
  - **Measurement**: `time pnpm pre-pr-lint --stage=full` wall-clock; sample 10 runs; histogram p50/p95.
  - **Anchor**: vision.md row 73 (`scripts/run-pre-pr-lint-stack.mjs` — the lint stack runner pattern); AGENTS.md rule #10 (deterministic enforcement — a gate that can't terminate isn't a gate); operator directive 2026-05-20 (verify M1.13 PR before push).

<!-- Moved from P0 2026-05-18: M4 tasks deprioritized until M1+M2+M3 ship (see MILESTONES.md). -->
- [ ] `operator-machine-budget-autoscale` — minsky reads a single operator-defined machine-utilisation budget (default 70%, raised explicitly for swarm), auto-scales worker concurrency to *match* it via effective-throughput feedback, strips OS throttles that make the budget unreachable, and propagates host changes to dotfiles/agentbrew so they're durable (vision.md rule #15 operationalised)
  - **ID**: operator-machine-budget-autoscale
  - **Tags**: p0, operator-directive, resource-governance, autoscale, launchd, rule-9, rule-10, rule-15, cross-repo-sync
  - **Milestone**: M4
  - **Competitive-goal**: matching (not under- or over-shooting) the operator's machine budget maximises sustained parallel throughput per dollar of hardware — directly the autonomous-merge-rate and cost-per-merged-PR lines on the `self-metrics-competitive-benchmark` scorecard; a fleet that gridlocks at 20 or idles at 70%-unused both lose to one that auto-finds the saturation knee.
  - **Surfaced-by**: operator directive 2026-05-17 ("a user defines the % they're ok with, default 70%, minsky must match that; allow workers ~80% during swarm; adjust machine settings if needed; sync machine changes to dotfiles/agentbrew + enterprise-mirror tasks; update rules/skills"). Live evidence this is needed: on a 10-core box the worker plist shipped `ProcessType=Background` (macOS QoS throttles CPU/IO) AND a fixed `--spawn-additional-workers` count; empirically 4≈ok, 10 saturates usefully (load ~37, 10 active Opus subprocesses, PRs produced), 14 stalls the ramp, 20 gridlocks to zero work at load 61 — a hand-tuned constant cannot track this and the QoS flag made the budget physically unreachable. In-session stopgaps already applied (ProcessType→Standard, concurrency reverted to the proven knee); this task makes it durable + self-tuning.
  - **Details**: (a) **Budget input** — read `MINSKY_MACHINE_BUDGET_PCT` (or a config field) defaulting to `70`; a swarm window may set it higher (≤ the operator's stated max, e.g. 80) but only under the explicit weekly-gated swarm switch (compose [[native-agent-teams-with-tiered-adapter]] `MINSKY_SWARM_MODE` + the swarm-mode operational rule). (b) **Effective-throughput autoscaler** — a pure controller `computeWorkerTarget({budgetPct, cores, recentActiveSubprocs, recentPrRate, loadAvg, lastTargets})` that ratchets concurrency toward the budget while *effective* throughput (active model subprocesses + PR production) rises, and backs off on the gridlock signature (active-subprocs collapsing toward 0 with load runaway) — never trusting a nominal worker count. Pre-register the knee per box (rule #9). (c) **Throttle remover** — detect+correct OS throttles that contradict the budget: launchd `ProcessType=Background`→`Standard`, unintended `Nice`, low `ulimit -n`, stale `MINSKY_*` caps; verify empirically that the budget is now reachable. (d) **Cross-repo propagation** — every host-level change emits a task to the relevant enterprise mirror: `~/apps/dotfiles/TASKS.md` for launchd/shell/sysctl/ulimit, `~/apps/agentbrew/TASKS.md` for the agent rule/skill encoding of the budget; minsky then *pulls* the durable version instead of re-applying a one-off (rule #1). (e) **Deterministic check** (rule #10) — `scripts/check-machine-budget.mjs` in `verify`: asserts the budget is parsed, no contradicting throttle is present (`ProcessType=Background` with a non-trivial budget = hard fail), and the autoscaler controller has paired tests for ramp-up, knee-detection, and gridlock-backoff.
  - **Files**: `novel/tick-loop/src/machine-budget-autoscaler.ts` (new, pure controller) + `…test.ts` (ramp/knee/gridlock cases), `novel/tick-loop/src/os-throttle-detect.ts` (new) + tests, `novel/tick-loop/bin/tick-loop.mjs` (root reads budget, drives the autoscaler instead of a fixed `--spawn-additional-workers`), `scripts/check-machine-budget.mjs` (new, rule-#10) + test, `~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.minsky.opus-sonnet-run.plist` (ProcessType=Standard — operator-owned; mirrored as the dotfiles task), `docs/` (budget + propagation runbook), `package.json` verify wiring.
  - **Hypothesis**: a fixed worker count + `ProcessType=Background` either under-uses the box (idle budget) or gridlocks it (20→0 work); a budget-matched effective-throughput autoscaler with throttles removed holds utilisation at the operator's % and maximises PRs/hour, auto-finding the saturation knee per host without hand-tuning.
  - **Success**: with budget=80 the fleet sustains ≈80% utilisation with effective throughput at/above the hand-tuned 10-worker baseline (PRs produced/merged per hour ≥ baseline) and never gridlocks; with budget=70 (default) it sits at ≈70%; `check-machine-budget.mjs` hard-fails on a `Background` QoS + non-trivial budget; the dotfiles & agentbrew mirror tasks exist.
  - **Pivot**: if a closed-loop autoscaler oscillates (concurrency hunting), fall back to a per-host calibrated constant table (measured knee per core-count) selected by the budget — still no `Background` throttle, still cross-repo-propagated; never revert to a single hand-edited global constant.
  - **Measurement**: `node scripts/bench-throughput.mjs --budget=70 --budget=80` over fixed windows → {mean utilisation vs target, PRs merged/hr, gridlock incidents}; assert utilisation within ±10% of budget and PRs/hr ≥ the 10-worker baseline; controller unit tests assert ramp-up, knee-hold, gridlock-backoff; `check-machine-budget.mjs` red on injected `ProcessType=Background`+budget.
  - **Anchor**: operator directive 2026-05-17 (verbatim); vision.md **rule #15** (match the operator's machine-utilisation budget — this task operationalises it), rule #1 (propagate to dotfiles/agentbrew, don't hand-maintain), rule #9 (pre-registered HDD — each scaling change), rule #10 (deterministic budget/throttle check), rule #12 (scope discipline — match, don't sprawl); Little's Law / queueing theory (throughput bounded by capacity — past saturation concurrency only adds contention); Apple `launchd.plist(5)` (`ProcessType` QoS). Composes [[native-agent-teams-with-tiered-adapter]] (swarm tier sets the high budget; agent-view delegates worktree/session scheduling) and the swarm-mode operational rule (weekly, operator-gated, ≤80%).

<!-- Operator directive 2026-05-08: shift Minsky's product identity from "repo-rooted stack" to "global, context-aware CLI". The two blocks immediately below capture the remaining shift: (1) make `minsky` self-bootstrap on a fresh machine — auto-detect Claude exhaustion and install the local-LLM stack with one confirm prompt; (2) polish the `minsky` (no-args) UX so it reads context, suggests actions, and asks for confirmation. README's "Not a global CLI" disclaimer is retired in the same PR that lands (2). The original (0) — clean up the WIP merge backlog so the other-machine `git pull` produces a coherent main — shipped: PRs #324 / #331 / #339 / #341 / #344 / #353 / #365 / #366 merged, #361 closed (substrate shipped elsewhere). -->

<!-- Operator directive 2026-05-16: single-command run-anywhere multi-tenant minsky. `minsky` (zero args) in ANY project folder runs the orchestrator on that folder + subfolders, with: home-repo full push/PR; foreign-repo PR-only-into-TASKS.md; self-monitoring of minsky-on-itself; auto-restart with bounded backoff to a time limit; dynamic model-by-budget unless pinned; multi-tenant safe (dozens of concurrent processes, zero conflict); full local-model fallback. The 5 `runany-*` P0s below are that cluster — implement in order; each composes existing substrates rather than reinventing (rule #1). -->

- [ ] `runany-permission-scoped-writes` — home repo: push commits + open PRs for any task; foreign repos under the tree: ONLY open a PR adding tasks to their TASKS.md from findings (never push code); always self-monitor minsky-on-itself and file improvement tasks into minsky's TASKS.md
  - **ID**: runany-permission-scoped-writes
  - **Tags**: p0, operator-directive, runany, security, policy, rule-13
  - **Milestone**: M4
  - **Touches**: `novel/cross-repo-runner/**`, `scripts/orchestrate.mjs`, `scripts/local-gate-merge.mjs`
  - **Details**: a deterministic policy seam classifies each repo the run touches as **home** (the invoked folder's git repo / its origin) or **foreign** (any other git repo under the tree). Home → existing full flow (branch, push, PR, gate-merge). Foreign → the ONLY permitted write is `gh pr create` whose diff is limited to that repo's `TASKS.md` (append findings as tasks.md-spec task blocks); any non-TASKS.md change or push to a foreign repo is refused and logged. Independently, every run also vets minsky-on-itself and, on any friction/bug/regression, appends a task to minsky's own TASKS.md (scout-and-record across the fleet). Pure `classifyRepo()` + `assertWriteAllowed()` (rule #10, no model in the gate).
  - **Hypothesis**: without this, a run-anywhere conductor could push code to unrelated repos. After this, 0 code pushes to foreign repos occur; foreign-repo contributions are 100% TASKS.md-only PRs; minsky-self findings are filed every run that observes one.
  - **Success**: in a fixture with 1 home + 2 foreign repos, over a run: foreign repos receive only TASKS.md-only PRs (`gh pr diff` touches only TASKS.md), 0 foreign code pushes; ≥1 minsky-self improvement task filed when a seeded friction exists.
  - **Pivot**: if `gh pr diff`-shaped enforcement is bypassable, gate at the git layer (refuse `git push` whose remote ≠ home origin) as the hard backstop and keep the diff-shape check as defense-in-depth.
  - **Measurement**: `node scripts/runany-policy-audit.mjs --window=run --json` → `{foreign_code_pushes:0, foreign_prs_nontaskmd:0, minsky_self_tasks_filed:>=1}`.
  - **Anchor**: rule #13 (security/privacy — least-authority across repos); Saltzer & Schroeder 1975 (least privilege, fail-safe defaults); operator 2026-05-16 directive.
  - **Files**: `novel/cross-repo-runner/src/repo-policy.ts` (+ `.test.ts`), wiring in `scripts/orchestrate.mjs` + `scripts/local-gate-merge.mjs`, `scripts/runany-policy-audit.mjs` (+ test), `docs/run-anywhere.md`.
  - **Acceptance**: (1) `classifyRepo`/`assertWriteAllowed` shipped with paired tests (home vs foreign × push/PR/taskmd cells); (2) foreign push of non-TASKS.md refused + logged; (3) minsky-self scout files a task on seeded friction; (4) measurement thresholds met.

- [ ] `runany-multitenant-no-conflict` — dozens of concurrent `minsky` processes on one machine must never conflict (worktrees, locks, branches, launchd labels, scratch dirs, claims)
  - **ID**: runany-multitenant-no-conflict
  - **Tags**: p0, operator-directive, runany, concurrency, chaos-engineering
  - **Milestone**: M4
  - **Touches**: `novel/tick-loop/**`, `scripts/orchestrate.mjs`, `scripts/local-gate-merge.mjs`, `scripts/parallel-sweeper*`
  - **Details**: every per-run mutable namespace is keyed by a unique run-id (e.g. `<repo-hash>-<pid>-<rand>`): worktree dirs, `.minsky/locks/*`, branch names, scratch clones, the launchd label, the orchestrate ledger path, and any port. Cross-process task arbitration uses the existing `O_EXCL` flock claim extended to be repo+task scoped so two processes on the same repo can't grab the same task; the gate's `git clone --shared` scratch is already per-`mkdtemp` (safe) — verify + cover with a chaos test that spawns N=10 concurrent runs on the same repo and asserts zero collisions/corruption. Composes `daemon-parallel-worktree-launch` + `parallel-sweeper` + the touches-collision check; extends them cross-process/cross-repo.
  - **Hypothesis**: today concurrent runs on the same repo can collide (shared launchd label, branch, lock). After this, N=10 concurrent same-repo runs for 30 min produce 0 collisions (no double-claimed task, no branch clobber, no corrupt index/worktree, no launchd-label clash).
  - **Success**: chaos test `scripts/chaos-multitenant.mjs` (N=10, same repo, 30 min) → 0 collisions, 0 corrupt worktrees, every task claimed by ≤1 process, all processes independently supervised.
  - **Pivot**: if cross-process flock arbitration is unreliable on the FS, fall back to a single per-machine arbiter daemon that hands out task leases (still no central orchestrator — just a lock broker).
  - **Measurement**: `node scripts/chaos-multitenant.mjs --runs=10 --minutes=30 --json` → `{collisions:0, corruptWorktrees:0, doubleClaims:0}`.
  - **Anchor**: Basiri et al. 2016 (chaos steady-state hypothesis); Lamport (mutual exclusion); rule #7 (chaos engineering); operator 2026-05-16 directive.
  - **Files**: run-id namespacing in `novel/tick-loop/src/worker-config.ts` + `scripts/orchestrate.mjs` + `scripts/local-gate-merge.mjs`, `scripts/chaos-multitenant.mjs` (+ test), `docs/run-anywhere.md`.
  - **Acceptance**: (1) all per-run namespaces run-id-keyed; (2) repo+task-scoped claim prevents double-claim across processes; (3) `chaos-multitenant.mjs` ships + passes; (4) measurement = 0/0/0.

- [ ] `runany-self-restart-bounded-timelimit` — on any crash/error the run auto-restarts with sensible escalating backoff, repeatedly, until a configured wall-clock time limit, then stops cleanly
  - **ID**: runany-self-restart-bounded-timelimit
  - **Tags**: p0, operator-directive, runany, stay-alive, rule-6
  - **Milestone**: M4
  - **Touches**: `scripts/orchestrate.mjs`, `bin/minsky`, `distribution/launchd/**`
  - **Details**: a supervising wrapper (launchd KeepAlive + an inner deadline guard) restarts the conductor on crash/non-zero exit with escalating backoff (e.g. 5s→15s→60s→300s, capped) that RESETS after a sustained-healthy interval; a `MINSKY_RUN_TIME_LIMIT` (default e.g. 10h) is the hard wall-clock ceiling after which the wrapper stops restarting and exits 0 cleanly. Composes launchd `ThrottleInterval` + `tick-loop-backoff-schedule`; new = the deadline guard + backoff-reset-on-health + clean terminal stop.
  - **Hypothesis**: today launchd restarts indefinitely with a flat throttle and no time ceiling. After this, an injected crash-loop backs off on the documented schedule, recovers (backoff resets) when healthy, and the process stops cleanly at the time limit (no zombie, no infinite restart past the deadline).
  - **Success**: chaos: kill the conductor every 30s for 10 min → restart intervals follow the escalating schedule, then a 20-min healthy window resets backoff to base; with `MINSKY_RUN_TIME_LIMIT=600s` the wrapper exits 0 within 600±30s and no restart fires after.
  - **Pivot**: if backoff-reset-on-health oscillates, use a fixed escalating schedule with no reset (simpler, still bounded) and document the throughput tradeoff.
  - **Measurement**: `node scripts/chaos-restart-schedule.mjs --json` → `{schedule_followed:true, reset_on_health:true, stopped_at_limit:true, restarts_after_limit:0}`.
  - **Anchor**: Armstrong 2007 (supervisor restart); Beyer SRE 2016 (retry budget / backoff); rule #6 (stay alive, but bounded); operator 2026-05-16 directive.
  - **Files**: `scripts/orchestrate.mjs` (deadline + backoff), `bin/minsky` (wrapper), `distribution/launchd/com.minsky.runany.plist.tmpl`, `scripts/chaos-restart-schedule.mjs` (+ test), `docs/run-anywhere.md`.
  - **Acceptance**: (1) escalating backoff schedule implemented + tested; (2) backoff resets after sustained health; (3) hard time-limit clean stop, 0 restarts after; (4) chaos measurement all-true.

- [ ] `runany-dynamic-model-or-local-fallback` — model auto-selected by remaining tokens unless the operator explicitly pinned model(s); full automatic switch to local models when tokens are exhausted OR all remote backends are down/inaccessible
  - **ID**: runany-dynamic-model-or-local-fallback
  - **Tags**: p0, operator-directive, runany, model-router, local-llm
  - **Milestone**: M4
  - **Touches**: `novel/tick-loop/src/strategic-model-router.ts`, `novel/tick-loop/src/llm-provider-*.ts`, `novel/tick-loop/bin/minsky.mjs`, `scripts/orchestrate.mjs`
  - **Details**: end-to-end contract for the zero-arg run: (a) if the operator pinned model(s) (`MINSKY_STRATEGIC_PIN_MODEL` / explicit flag) → honor verbatim, never override; (b) else the strategic router picks the highest-quality model whose remaining budget fits (live-validated, not trusting a stale estimate — see #budget-guard-correctness); (c) when remote budget is exhausted OR every remote backend probe fails/inaccessible → switch fully + automatically to the local stack and keep running (bootstrap it if absent, per `minsky-cli-auto-bootstrap-local-llm`); (d) recover to remote when it returns. Composes `claude-usage-aware-strategic-model-router` (shipped) + `budget-guard-correctness` + the local-llm fallback wrapper; new = the unified "pin > dynamic > local" decision wired into the run-anywhere entrypoint with a liveness probe across ALL configured remote backends (not just claude).
  - **Hypothesis**: today provider selection requires env wrangling and silently mis-degrades on bad budget estimates. After this, with no pin: model tracks remaining budget; with all remote backends blocked (simulated): the run switches to local within one iteration and continues (0 wedged iterations); with a pin: the pinned model is used in 100% of iterations regardless of budget.
  - **Success**: 3 scenarios — (pin) 100% pinned-model dispatch; (dynamic) model tier correlates with remaining-budget bands; (all-remote-down fixture) ≤1 iteration to switch to local, then ≥95% local dispatch, 0 wedged/halted iterations.
  - **Pivot**: if multi-backend liveness probing is too costly per iteration, cache probe results (TTL ≥60s) and probe only on a provider error or budget-band crossing.
  - **Measurement**: `node scripts/runany-model-audit.mjs --scenario=<pin|dynamic|all-down> --json` → matches the success thresholds per scenario.
  - **Anchor**: rule #9 (pre-registered); Beyer SRE 2016 (graceful degradation, visible-not-silent); operator 2026-05-16 directive; composes #budget-guard-correctness (live-validate) + #claude-usage-aware-strategic-model-router (shipped).
  - **Files**: `novel/tick-loop/src/strategic-model-router.ts` + tests, `novel/tick-loop/src/llm-provider-selector.ts` + tests, `scripts/runany-model-audit.mjs` (+ test), `docs/run-anywhere.md`.
  - **Acceptance**: (1) pin overrides everything; (2) dynamic-by-remaining-budget when unpinned; (3) full auto local fallback when all remote down/exhausted, ≤1 iteration, 0 wedge; (4) recover to remote; (5) 3-scenario measurement passes.

- [ ] `runany-retro-tui-dashboard` — remove the web UI; replace with a retro-1995 CLI/TUI: a machine-wide dashboard of ALL running minsky processes + machine info; select one → its detail page with log list; starting `minsky` in a folder auto-opens that process's fullscreen page
  - **ID**: runany-retro-tui-dashboard
  - **Tags**: p0, operator-directive, runany, tui, ui-removal, rule-10-ratchet
  - **Milestone**: M4
  - **Touches**: `novel/dashboard-web/**`, `distribution/run-dashboard-web.sh`, `.github/workflows/lighthouse.yml`, `bin/minsky`, `novel/tui/**`
  - **Details**: **Ratchet (rule #10):** in the SAME PR that lands the TUI, remove the web UI surface — delete/retire `@minsky/dashboard-web`, `distribution/run-dashboard-web.sh`, the `lighthouse` CI workflow + its Mobile≥0.85 gate, and any `pnpm minsky:ui` wiring (never two competing operator surfaces). **New:** a zero-dependency retro TUI (ANSI/box-drawing, amber/green-on-black, 80×24 1995 aesthetic — no heavy TUI dep; raw ANSI or a tiny vendored renderer to stay $0 and dep-light per rule #1/$10-mo cap). Screens: (1) **machine dashboard** — every running minsky process on the host (scan: `pgrep -f 'scripts/orchestrate.mjs|tick-loop.mjs|local-gate-merge'`, each run-id, repo, role, uptime, last ledger tick, model, state) + machine info (host, load, cpu, mem, disk, time, total minsky procs); arrow/number-select a row. (2) **process detail** — selected run's repo, env (model/provider), launchd label, ledger summary, recent merges, AND a **log list** (its `.minsky/*.log` files, tail-able). `minsky` with zero args opens screen (1); when `minsky` STARTS a run in a folder it auto-opens that run's screen (2) fullscreen. Read-only surface (no model calls); composes the existing `GET /watch.json`-style data via direct file/process reads. Cluster sibling of #runany-zero-arg-entrypoint.
  - **Hypothesis**: today the operator surface is a web UI (Lighthouse-gated, needs a browser/port) — wrong for a $0 multi-tenant CLI tool. After this, `minsky` (no args) renders the machine dashboard listing 100% of running minsky processes; selecting one shows its detail+logs; a folder-start auto-opens that run's page; the web UI + lighthouse gate are gone (0 references remain).
  - **Success**: with 3 minsky runs active, the TUI dashboard lists all 3 with correct repo/uptime/model; selecting each opens its detail with its log files listed; a fresh `minsky` in a 4th folder auto-opens that run's fullscreen page; `grep -rn 'dashboard-web\|lighthouse\|dogfood:ui' --include=*.{ts,mjs,yml,json,sh,md}` (excluding this task's removal commit history) returns 0 live references.
  - **Pivot**: if a pure-ANSI renderer is too brittle across terminals, vendor ONE tiny MIT TUI primitive (still no runtime service, still $0) rather than reinstating the web UI — the web UI does not come back.
  - **Measurement**: `node scripts/runany-tui-audit.mjs --json` → `{procs_listed == procs_running, detail_has_logs:true, autoopen_on_start:true, web_ui_refs:0, lighthouse_workflow_present:false}`.
  - **Anchor**: rule #10 (deterministic ratchet — remove the superseded surface in the same PR); rule #1 (compose existing watch data, dep-light); $10/mo cap (no runtime web service); operator 2026-05-16 directive.
  - **Files**: new `novel/tui/src/*` (+ tests for the pure render/layout + process-scan functions), `bin/minsky` (no-arg → dashboard; start → auto-open detail), removals under `novel/dashboard-web/**` + `distribution/run-dashboard-web.sh` + `.github/workflows/lighthouse.yml`, `scripts/runany-tui-audit.mjs` (+ test), `docs/run-anywhere.md`, `vision.md` pattern-index row + ratchet note.
  - **Acceptance**: (1) retro TUI machine dashboard lists all running minsky procs + machine info; (2) per-process detail page with log list; (3) zero-arg → dashboard, folder-start → auto-open that run's fullscreen detail; (4) web UI + lighthouse gate fully removed in the same PR (audit shows 0 refs); (5) pure render/scan logic unit-tested (rule #10); (6) measurement all-pass.

- [ ] `security-privacy-priority-substrate` — codify security & privacy as Minsky's #2 priority after performance; ship the rule-#13 substrate (per-feature review gate + threat-model section + 8 minimum-bar items)
  - **ID**: security-privacy-priority-substrate
  - **Tags**: p0, security, privacy, substrate, pick-next
  - **Milestone**: M4
  - **Touches**: `vision.md`, `TASKS.md`, `user-stories/**`, `scripts/check-*.mjs`, `distribution/**`, `novel/dashboard-web/src/**`, `.github/workflows/**`, `novel/*/README.md`
  - **Pick-next**: yes — operator-promoted 2026-05-06: "make security and privacy one of main priorities for Minsky."
  - **Estimate**: 1w (substrate slice ships in initial PR — vision.md rule #13 + 6 user-story sections; subsequent slices ratchet)
  - **Hypothesis**: Operator directive 2026-05-06: *"performance comes first but [security/privacy] in a reasonable way comes second. Eg it's fine to follow industry standard solutions."* Today Minsky's security surface is ad-hoc — 6 security P0 tasks exist (`secret-scanning-precommit-and-ci`, `supervisor-sandbox-syscall-restriction`, `dashboard-localhost-only-by-default`, `otel-no-pii-in-spans-lint`, `supply-chain-hardening-lockfile-sbom-slsa`, `cloud-tier-external-security-audit-gate`) but none are pick-next AND there's no constitutional rule binding them together. Pre-registration: with rule #13 codified, every new feature PR's review explicitly checks the 8-item minimum bar; the existing 6 security P0s become the substrate the rule cites. Over the next 14 days, ≥1 of the 6 ships. Industry-standard primitives only — Minsky doesn't invent crypto / sandbox / threat-modelling.
  - **Details**: (a) **Substrate slice** (this PR): vision.md rule #13 "Security & privacy — second priority after performance" with 8-item minimum bar + performance-first carve-out clause + Saltzer/Schroeder/NIST/OWASP/SLSA citations; security-privacy section appended to all 6 user-story files (`001-006-*.md`); this task block. (b) **Slice 2**: ship `secret-scanning-precommit-and-ci` (gitleaks + trufflehog at CI). (c) **Slice 3**: ship `otel-no-pii-in-spans-lint`. (d) **Slice 4**: ship `supervisor-sandbox-syscall-restriction` (systemd ProtectSystem + macOS App Sandbox). (e) **Slice 5**: ship `dashboard-localhost-only-by-default`. (f) **Slice 6**: ship `supply-chain-hardening-lockfile-sbom-slsa` (CycloneDX SBOM + SLSA Build Level 3 + Sigstore). (g) **Slice 7**: write threat-model section into each `novel/*` package's README (STRIDE-shaped, ≥5 lines per package).
  - **Files**: `vision.md` (rule #13), `user-stories/001-006-*.md` (Security & privacy section per file), `TASKS.md` (this block + pick-next promotion of the 6 sibling security P0s), `scripts/check-rule-13-sibling-anchors.mjs` + `scripts/check-rule-13-sibling-anchors.test.mjs` (acceptance criterion #3 substrate-cohesion gate — pins each of the 6 sibling P0s to cite `rule #13`), `scripts/check-vision-rule-13-task-id-citations.mjs` + `scripts/check-vision-rule-13-task-id-citations.test.mjs` (inverse direction of the substrate-cohesion gate — pins vision.md § 13 minimum-bar items 1–6 to cite the canonical `SIBLING_P0_IDS` verbatim as backticked text), `scripts/check-vision-rule-13-non-task-anchors.mjs` + `scripts/check-vision-rule-13-non-task-anchors.test.mjs` (companion gate — pins vision.md § 13 minimum-bar items 7 (Privacy by default) and 8 (Threat model per novel/* package) to their named industry-standard anchors `GDPR Article 25` / `OWASP Privacy Top 10` / `STRIDE`, since those two items have no sibling P0 task ID to pin), `scripts/check-threat-model-section.mjs` + `scripts/check-threat-model-section.test.mjs` (acceptance criterion #5 drift gate — pins each of the 16 `novel/*/README.md` threat-model sections to STRIDE-shaped methodology + ≥5 non-empty content lines), `scripts/check-security-docs-cohesion.mjs` + `scripts/check-security-docs-cohesion.test.mjs` (substrate-cohesion gate over `docs/security/*.md` — pins each operator-readable security doc to cite `rule #13` AND carry a STRIDE-shaped `Threat model` heading; the fourth and last surface where rule #13's substrate cohesion can drift silently, complementing the task / spec / package gates above), `scripts/check-privacy-data-egress.mjs` + `scripts/check-privacy-data-egress.test.mjs` (rule #13.7 slice 2 drift gate — pins `docs/security/privacy-data-egress.md` to its six required H2 sections, five enumerated egress destinations, STRIDE engagement, and the GDPR Article 25 anchor), `scripts/check-dashboard-localhost-bind.mjs` + `scripts/check-dashboard-localhost-bind.test.mjs` (rule #13.4 drift gate — pins `novel/dashboard-web/src/{bind,start}.ts` substrate-cohesion: `BIND_DEFAULT="127.0.0.1"`, `resolveBindHostname` + `bindHostnameWarning` exports, start.ts importing + calling the resolver and threading `hostname` through `serve(...)`), follow-ups: `scripts/scan-secrets.mjs` (already shipped #239 substrate), `scripts/check-otel-no-pii.mjs`, `distribution/systemd/minsky-tick-loop.service` (ProtectSystem=strict + PrivateTmp + RestrictAddressFamilies), `distribution/launchd/com.minsky.tick-loop.plist` (App Sandbox), `novel/dashboard-web/src/start.ts` (default 127.0.0.1 bind), `.github/workflows/sbom.yml` (CycloneDX), `novel/*/README.md` (threat model per package).
  - **Verification**: `grep -c '## Security & privacy' user-stories/00*.md` returns 6. `grep -c 'Security & privacy — second priority after performance' vision.md` returns ≥1. The 6 sibling security P0s are surveyed quarterly: at least one of them ships within 14 days of substrate-merge.
  - **Measurement**: post-substrate, every feature PR's body or self-grade block contains either a "Security & privacy" line OR a `<!-- security: not-applicable — <reason> -->` opt-out. CI lint `pr-security-review` blocks merge otherwise (filed as follow-up). Concrete: `gh pr list --state merged --search "merged:>=YYYY-MM-DD" --json body --jq '[.[] | select(.body | contains("## Security") or contains("security: not-applicable") | not)] | length'` is 0 over rolling 7d.
  - **Pivot**: if false-positive rate on `pr-security-review` ≥10%/week (legit feature PRs flagged because they don't actually touch security surfaces), narrow the lint's required-mention set to "PRs that touch /scripts, /distribution, /novel/dashboard-web, or any auth/secret-related path". Don't retire the rule — performance-first carve-out is the relief valve, not retirement.
  - **Acceptance**: (1) vision.md rule #13 with 8-item minimum bar + performance-first carve-out; (2) Security & privacy section in all 6 user stories; (3) the 6 sibling security P0s linked to rule #13 in their Anchor lines; (4) at least one of the 6 sibling P0s ships within 14 days; (5) threat-model section in each `novel/*/README.md` (slice 7 follow-up).
  - **Anchor**: operator 2026-05-06 directive — "make security and privacy one of main priorities for Minsky"; vision.md rule #13 (this PR ships the rule); rule #1 (don't reinvent — adopt industry-standard); rule #6 (stay alive — security failures ground systems faster than bugs); rule #9 (pre-registered HDD — every security-affecting PR declares its threat model + mitigation hypothesis); Saltzer & Schroeder, *Proceedings of the IEEE* 63(9), 1975 (the 8 design principles); NIST SP 800-218 (SSDF, 2022); OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025 ed.); GDPR Article 25 (privacy by design); SLSA Specification 1.0 (slsa.dev/spec/v1.0/, 2025).
  - **Risk**: low. Pure substrate-discipline filing; the 6 sibling P0s already exist with full rule-#9 metadata. The risk is operator-overload on review (every PR now does two reviews — performance AND security) — mitigated by the performance-first carve-out clause: when they conflict, performance wins with a documented declared deviation. The carve-out is the relief valve.
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-06 operator directive — "Also add P0 to make security and privacy one of main priorities for Minsky. Add to vision, each user story; add P0 tasks. Note that performance comes first but that in a reasonable way comes second. Eg it's fine to follow industry standard solutions for that."

- [ ] `daemon-self-optimize-speed-tokens` — every iteration tries to improve daemon's own speed and decrease token consumption (one measurable optimization per loop)
  - **ID**: daemon-self-optimize-speed-tokens
  - **Tags**: p0, daemon, supervisor, throughput, optimization, self-improvement
  - **Milestone**: M4
  - **Touches**: `novel/tick-loop/**`, `scripts/iteration-cost-rolling-p95.mjs`
  - **Pick-next**: yes — operator-promoted 2026-05-05 ("Add p0 so that in every loop of minsky it tries to improve speed of itself and decrease tokens consumption").
  - **Estimate**: 1w (substrate slice ships in initial PR; subsequent slices ratchet)
  - **Hypothesis**: The daemon currently iterates without a per-iteration optimization gate — it ships task-progress code, but the daemon itself never consciously trims its own brief, eliminates redundant `gh` round-trips, or compresses log lines. Over hundreds of iterations this leaves measurable speed + token waste compounding silently. Pre-registration: with the optimization-discipline gate in `buildDaemonBrief` (this PR's substrate slice), every iteration emits either an optimization commit OR an `optimization: none-this-iteration` reason — visible-not-silent. Over a 14-day window, the rolling-7d daemon iteration p95 wall-time drops by ≥10% AND/OR rolling-7d input-token median drops by ≥10%. If neither moves, the gate is generating noise — pivot to a stricter eligibility filter (≥10-byte savings minimum) before retiring.
  - **Details**: (a) Substrate (this PR): extend `buildDaemonBrief` with an `## Optimization-discipline gate` section listing eligible optimization patterns (brief-shrinking, cached-prompt extension, skip-earlier gate, log-line dedup, round-trip elimination), an anti-vanity guard (must be measurable in tokens / wall-time / round-trip count), and a `none-this-iteration` carve-out. (b) Follow-up slice: per-iteration metrics — emit `tick-loop.iteration.duration_ms`, `tick-loop.iteration.tokens_in_delta`, `tick-loop.iteration.tokens_out_delta` as span attributes. (c) Follow-up slice: `daemon-iteration-cost-regression` self-diagnose invariant that fires when rolling p95 of either metric increases >20% week-over-week. (d) Follow-up slice: dashboard's "Throughput" section adds a "Cost" tile showing those rolling p95s. (e) Each shipped iteration explicitly notes the optimization (or its absence) in its iteration reason — this is the operator-readable feedback signal.
  - **Files**: `novel/tick-loop/src/daemon.ts` (extend `buildDaemonBrief`), `novel/tick-loop/src/daemon.test.ts` (paired tests for optimization gate), follow-ups: `novel/tick-loop/src/daemon.ts` (per-iteration cost spans), `scripts/self-diagnose.mjs` (regression invariant), `novel/dashboard-web/src/throughput.ts` (Cost tile).
  - **Verification**: rolling-7d daemon iteration p95 wall-time on `main` measured at fix-time + 14d via `node scripts/iteration-cost-rolling-p95.mjs --window=7d` (script in follow-up slice).
  - **Measurement**: `node scripts/iteration-cost-rolling-p95.mjs --window=7d --metric=duration_ms` returns ≥10% decrease vs. the baseline measured at substrate-merge time AND/OR `--metric=tokens_in_delta` returns ≥10% decrease. Concrete: baseline p95 wall-time ~`<measured-at-substrate-merge>` ms; target p95 ≤ baseline × 0.9 by day 14 OR documented why not. Daemon iteration log carries either a commit-bound optimization OR an explicit `optimization: none-this-iteration: <reason>` line per iteration — `grep -c 'optimization:' .minsky/tick-loop.out.log | awk '$1 == iteration_count'` (every iteration accounted for).
  - **Pivot**: if iterations ship 1-byte/whitespace "optimizations" to satisfy the gate, tighten the eligibility to ≥10-byte savings minimum or a measurable wall-time delta of ≥1ms before retiring. If the metric-regression invariant fires false-positively due to CI runner variance, switch from p95 to p50 trimmed mean. Don't retire the gate — silence-is-failure (Beyer SRE 2016) means an iteration without a stated optimization choice is itself a bug.
  - **Acceptance**: (1) `buildDaemonBrief` carries the optimization-discipline gate; (2) every daemon iteration's reason contains either an optimization commit reference OR `optimization: none-this-iteration: <reason>`; (3) span attributes for per-iteration duration_ms + tokens_in_delta + tokens_out_delta land on the existing `tick-loop.iteration` span; (4) `daemon-iteration-cost-regression` self-diagnose invariant ships; (5) dashboard's Throughput section gains a Cost tile.
  - **Anchor**: operator 2026-05-05 — "Add p0 so that in every loop of minsky it tries to improve speed of itself and decrease tokens consumption"; rule #9 (pre-registered HDD); Beyer SRE 2016 Ch. 6 (silence-is-failure); rule #2 (Strategy seam — pure builder fed by tasksMd content); composes with `daemon-self-detect-throughput-issues` (#184) — defensive (catch regressions) vs. offensive (compress proactively).
  - **Risk**: low. Substrate slice is a pure-builder extension covered by paired tests; follow-up slices add measurement infrastructure, none of which changes runtime behaviour beyond the existing tick loop.
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-05 operator directive — "Add p0 so that in every loop of minsky it tries to improve speed of itself and decrease tokens consumption. Then actually implement it, then continue minsky."

- [ ] `secret-scanning-precommit-and-ci` — gitleaks (or trufflehog) scan in pre-commit + CI; secrets detected = block merge
  - **ID**: secret-scanning-precommit-and-ci
  - **Tags**: security, p0, leak-prevention, rule-10
  - **Milestone**: M4
  - **Touches**: `.lefthook.yml`, `lefthook.yml`, `.github/workflows/ci.yml`, `.gitleaks.toml`, `docs/security/secret-scanning.md`
  - **Estimate**: 2d
  - **Hypothesis**: The supervisor edits arbitrary files in the repo per iteration (including config, env templates, plist files); without a deterministic gate, a single regression can write `OPENAI_API_KEY=...` / `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=...` / a private RSA key into a tracked file and push it. Industry baseline: Anthropic / GitHub / npm / etc. all rotate compromised tokens within 24h of detection, but the leak window before detection is real (median TTD for leaked tokens on public GitHub is hours; Truffle Security 2023 study). A `gitleaks` (or `trufflehog`) scan in lefthook pre-commit + GH Actions CI catches secrets before they ship. **Pre-registration**: 0 secrets in the repo at baseline (verified by initial scan); 0 secrets allowed to land in any future PR. Pivot if scan rate is too noisy (>2 false positives per month → tune the allow-list / gitleaks `.gitleaks.toml` config).
  - **Details**: (a) Add `gitleaks` to lefthook's pre-commit hook (already used for biome/typecheck/test); fail commit on any finding. (b) Add `.github/workflows/ci.yml` job `secret-scan` that runs `gitleaks detect --redact --no-banner --exit-code 1` on the PR diff; mark `needs:` in the `ci` aggregator. (c) `.gitleaks.toml` config tuned for the project — start with the default ruleset, allow-list any false positives encountered (each allow-list entry must reference a TASKS-id justifying the exclusion). (d) **One-time historical audit**: run `gitleaks detect --log-opts="--all"` against all reachable history; any historical leak goes into a P0 rotation task (separate; needs immediate human approval per the no-cross-repo / no-message-board rule, since rotating an API key is a human-visible action). (e) Operator escape hatch: `MINSKY_SKIP_SECRET_SCAN=1` for the local pre-commit hook only (NOT for CI — CI is non-bypassable).
  - **Files**: `.lefthook.yml` (new pre-commit step), `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (new job + needs entry), `.gitleaks.toml` (new — tuned config), `docs/security/secret-scanning.md` (operator guide explaining the override + how to add allow-list entries).
  - **Verification**: paired tests via fixture commits (a synthetic test with a fake AWS-style key in a temp branch — `gitleaks` rejects it; same fixture without the key — passes); CI job blocks merge on the seeded fixture; `gitleaks detect --log-opts="--all"` against current main returns 0 findings.
  - **Measurement**: `gitleaks detect --log-opts="--all" --no-banner --exit-code 1; echo $?` returns 0 against main (no historical leaks). After merge: `gh api repos/fyodoriv/minsky/actions/workflows --jq '.workflows[] | select(.name == "secret-scan") | .state'` returns "active". Per-PR: every PR's checks include a passing `secret-scan` job.
  - **Pivot**: if gitleaks's default ruleset produces ≥3 false positives in the first month, swap to trufflehog (more aggressive entropy detection but more tunable per-pattern). The lefthook + CI integration is reversible — only the scanner binary changes.
  - **Acceptance**: zero secrets in current main (verified by full-history scan); pre-commit hook blocks new secrets locally; CI blocks new secrets on every PR; the historical-audit task is filed as a separate task (since rotation is a human action).
  - **Anchor**: rule #13 (security & privacy as #2 priority after performance — this task implements minimum-bar item #1, "no secrets in the repo, ever"); rule #10 (deterministic enforcement — secret detection is a CI lint, not a hope); Truffle Security "The State of Secrets Sprawl 2023" (median TTD for public GitHub secrets is in hours; the cost of a leak is real); Beyer SRE 2016 Ch. 5 (defence in depth — pre-commit catches operator slips; CI catches everything else); rule #2 (the scanner is a Strategy seam — gitleaks → trufflehog pivot is one config change).
  - **Risk**: low-medium. False positives are the main operational risk; mitigation is the allow-list + pivot threshold.
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-04 user brief — "Add P0 tasks to make sure that minsky is extremely secure and doesn't leak anything."

- [ ] `supervisor-sandbox-syscall-restriction` — restrict the supervisor's filesystem + network reach to the minimum it actually needs
  - **ID**: supervisor-sandbox-syscall-restriction
  - **Tags**: security, p0, sandbox, supervisor, blast-radius
  - **Milestone**: M4
  - **Touches**: `distribution/launchd/**`, `distribution/systemd/**`, `scripts/check-supervisor-sandbox-hardening*.mjs`, `scripts/chaos/**`, `docs/security/supervisor-sandbox.md`, `README.md`
  - **Estimate**: 2w
  - **Hypothesis**: Today the launchd / systemd-user supervisor runs as the operator's full UID with no syscall restrictions — it can read every file in `$HOME`, write anywhere, open any network port. The actual surface it needs is much narrower: read `~/.claude/projects/` (token-monitor JSONLs), read+write `<repo>/` (git operations), execute `claude` / `node` / `git` / `pnpm` / `gh` (and nothing else), bind a localhost port for the dashboard. A regression that causes the supervisor to read `~/.ssh/` or `~/Documents/` should fail loudly, not silently exfiltrate. macOS sandbox-exec (deprecated but still functional) + Linux systemd `ProtectHome=`, `ProtectSystem=`, `ReadWritePaths=`, `RestrictAddressFamilies=`, `SystemCallFilter=` are the levers. **Pre-registration**: the sandboxed supervisor passes every existing dogfood iteration smoke (the same lints / tests / iterations work) AND `sandbox-exec` (or systemd) blocks reads to `~/.ssh/` (verified via a chaos test that intentionally reads it from inside the supervisor — should fail with EPERM). Pivot if the constraint set is too tight to allow normal operation (>3 iterations per week fail with EPERM on legitimate paths the operator uses) — relax that specific path with a TASKS-id-justified entry, don't disable the sandbox entirely.
  - **Details**: (a) macOS: write a sandbox profile in `distribution/launchd/com.minsky.tick-loop.sb` (Apple TrustedBSD MAC profile — `(version 1) (deny default) (allow ...)` syntax). The launchd plist's `ProgramArguments` becomes `/usr/bin/sandbox-exec -f <profile> <run-tick-loop.sh>`. (b) Linux: extend `distribution/systemd/minsky-tick-loop.service` with `ProtectHome=read-only` (with `ReadWritePaths=<repo>` for the writable subset), `ProtectSystem=strict`, `PrivateTmp=true`, `RestrictAddressFamilies=AF_UNIX AF_INET AF_INET6`, `SystemCallFilter=@system-service`, `NoNewPrivileges=true`. (c) Both: chaos test in `scripts/chaos/sandbox-attempts-disallowed-read.mjs` — spawned inside the supervisor via the dry-run path; tries to read `~/.ssh/known_hosts`; expects EPERM. (d) Operator escape hatch: `MINSKY_SANDBOX=off` for emergencies (rule #2 + Beyer SRE Ch. 17). (e) Documentation: `docs/security/supervisor-sandbox.md` explains the threat model + the allow-list + how to extend it.
  - **Files**: `distribution/launchd/com.minsky.tick-loop.sb` (new — pending), `distribution/systemd/minsky-tick-loop.service` (extended — stage 0 safe directives shipped this PR), `distribution/systemd/minsky-budget-guard.service` (extended — stage 0 safe directives shipped this PR), `distribution/systemd/minsky-watchdog.service` (extended — stage 0 safe directives shipped this PR), `scripts/check-supervisor-sandbox-hardening.mjs` (new — stage 0 lint pinning the safe directive set across all three units; paired test shipped this PR), `scripts/chaos/sandbox-attempts-disallowed-read.mjs` (new — pending; paired test), `docs/security/supervisor-sandbox.md` (new — shipped #342), README mentions of the sandbox + escape hatch.
  - **Verification**: `pnpm minsky:setup` works post-sandbox (regression suite); chaos test asserts EPERM on `~/.ssh/`; manual: `cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa` from inside the supervisor's spawn fails with EPERM.
  - **Measurement**: `sandbox-exec -f distribution/launchd/com.minsky.tick-loop.sb /bin/cat ~/.ssh/known_hosts; echo $?` returns non-zero (sandbox blocks). On Linux: `systemd-run --user --uid=$(id -u) --slice=minsky-test --property=ProtectHome=read-only /bin/cat /home/$(whoami)/.ssh/known_hosts` returns non-zero. After supervisor restart with sandbox on: existing dogfood iteration smoke passes — `tail .minsky/tick-loop.out.log | grep -c '"iteration.status":"completed"'` ≥ 1 within 30 minutes.
  - **Pivot**: if the macOS sandbox-exec API is removed by Apple (it has been deprecated since macOS 14), pivot to a wrapper based on `seatbelt` (the underlying mechanism, still exposed via private API) OR to running the supervisor inside a Docker container with `--security-opt=apparmor=...`. The Linux systemd config is unaffected.
  - **Acceptance**: sandbox profile shipped; chaos test pins the EPERM-on-disallowed-path behaviour; existing dogfood still works; the override env documented.
  - **Anchor**: rule #13 (security & privacy as #2 priority after performance — this task implements minimum-bar item #3, "supervisor sandbox"); McKusick & Watson's TrustedBSD MAC framework (the `sandbox-exec` substrate); systemd's hardening guide (`systemd.exec(5)` man page — every property cited above is a documented security primitive); Saltzer & Schroeder 1975 "The Protection of Information in Computer Systems" — principle of least privilege; rule #7 (graceful-degrade chaos table — every disallowed path attempt is itself a chaos test fixture).
  - **Risk**: high. Sandboxing introduces real friction with normal operation; the path-allow-list is the most likely source of false positives. Mitigation: ship in stages (pre-merge: dry-run the sandbox locally for 7 days; post-merge: ramp via `MINSKY_SANDBOX=warn-only` for 14 days, then `MINSKY_SANDBOX=enforce`). Each ramp step is a separate PR, not a single big-bang change.
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-04 user brief — "Add P0 tasks to make sure that minsky is extremely secure and doesn't leak anything."

- [ ] `otel-no-pii-in-spans-lint` — CI lint that blocks any `record({...})` call carrying PII-shaped attributes
  - **ID**: otel-no-pii-in-spans-lint
  - **Tags**: security, p0, otel, observability, leak-prevention, rule-10
  - **Milestone**: M4
  - **Touches**: `scripts/check-rule-otel-no-pii*.mjs`, `.github/workflows/ci.yml`
  - **Estimate**: 1w
  - **Hypothesis**: Every `record({...})` call in `novel/**/*.ts` becomes an OTEL span. Spans flow to OpenObserve (and, in the cloud tier, to a multi-tenant aggregator). A regression that puts a Claude API key in a span (`record({apiKey: env.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY})` is an easy mistake) leaks across the whole pipeline. The lint scans for: attribute names matching `(api[_-]?key|password|token|secret|credential|bearer)` or values matching `(sk-[a-zA-Z0-9]{20,}|ghp_[a-zA-Z0-9]{30,})` and rejects them. **Pre-registration**: 0 PII-shaped attributes across all current `record({...})` calls (verified via initial sweep); 0 allowed in any future PR. Pivot if the regex is too narrow (a leak-class slips through) or too noisy (≥2 false positives per month → refine rules).
  - **Details**: (a) `scripts/check-rule-otel-no-pii.mjs` — regex over `record\(\{[^}]+\}\)` call sites in `novel/**/*.ts`. (b) Pure decision function `classifySpanAttribute(name, value): {ok: boolean, reason?: string}` with paired tests. (c) Allow-list: explicit annotations like `// @otel-pii-allowed: <reason>` next to a flagged line, recording why this particular attribute is intentionally PII-shaped (e.g., a hash of an opaque ID where the regex false-positives on the `_token` substring). Each allow-list usage requires a TASKS-id justifying it.
  - **Files**: `scripts/check-rule-otel-no-pii.mjs` + paired tests, `.github/workflows/ci.yml` job + `needs:` entry.
  - **Verification**: ≥6 paired tests (token-named-attribute / sk-prefixed-value / password-named / safe-attribute-name / annotated-allowlist-honored / nested-object-traversed); CI job runs on every PR.
  - **Measurement**: `node scripts/check-rule-otel-no-pii.mjs --diff-base=origin/main` exits 0 against current main (no leaks today). Synthetic fixture: `test/fixtures/otel-pii/leaking-span.ts` containing `record({apiKey: env.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY})` makes the lint exit 1.
  - **Pivot**: if the regex misses a leak class (e.g., the operator names a field `userPassphrase` instead of `password`), extend the regex with a learned pattern; the allow-list grows when refinement causes a false positive. Don't drop the lint — the alternative is "trust the operator", which is exactly what fails.
  - **Acceptance**: 0 PII-shaped attributes in current spans; lint blocks new ones; doc in `docs/security/otel-no-pii.md` explains what counts.
  - **Anchor**: rule #13 (security & privacy as #2 priority after performance — this task implements minimum-bar item #2, "no PII in OTEL spans"); rule #4 (OTEL coverage; this is the security companion); rule #10 (deterministic enforcement); GDPR Art. 25 (data protection by design); CCPA equivalent in California; OWASP A04 "Insecure Design".
  - **Risk**: low. Static regex over source code; both false-positive and false-negative are tunable.
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-04 user brief — "Add P0 tasks to make sure that minsky is extremely secure and doesn't leak anything."

- [ ] `cloud-tier-external-security-audit-gate` — block the cloud tier ship until a third-party security audit clears
  - **ID**: cloud-tier-external-security-audit-gate
  - **Tags**: security, p0, audit, cloud-tier, blocked-until-cloud-readiness
  - **Milestone**: M4
  - **Touches**: `scripts/check-cloud-audit-gate*.mjs`, `.github/workflows/ci.yml`, `docs/security/audit-gate.md`
  - **Estimate**: external — 4-8w from when triggered
  - **Blocked**: needs-user-approval — engaging an external security audit firm is a human action (vendor selection, contract, payment) and out of scope for autonomous loops per `feedback_modify_only_minsky_repo.md`. Filed here so the gate exists in advance of the cloud tier shipping; user must approve in a separate session before this task starts.
  - **Hypothesis**: The local CLI's blast radius is the operator's own machine (already heavily mitigated by the other 5 P0 tasks here). The cloud tier's blast radius is **every customer's repo**, **every customer's API tokens**, and **every customer's data flowing through the multi-tenant OTEL aggregator**. Self-audit is insufficient — operators / customers correctly discount internal claims. **Pre-registration**: before any cloud tier feature ships to a non-operator user, an external firm (Trail of Bits / NCC Group / Doyensec / a comparable specialist) audits the threat model + sample implementation; their report is public (or at minimum the executive summary is); their critical findings are remediated and re-tested before the gate clears. Pivot if the audit cost exceeds the cloud tier's projected MRR for 12 months — postpone the cloud tier until the unit economics support a real audit; don't ship cloud-multi-tenant without one.
  - **Details**: (a) Block any PR that touches `novel/cloud-supervisor/`, `novel/cross-repo-benchmark/`, or `novel/shared-invariant-catalog/` (when those packages exist) until this task's `**Blocked**` line is removed. The block is enforced by a CI lint that scans for those package paths in the PR diff. (b) Scope of audit: cloud-supervisor's GH App auth flow, multi-tenant data isolation, OTEL ingestion pipeline, billing-once-paywall-lands. (c) Findings tracker: each finding is a P0 sub-task; gate clears only when all P0 sub-tasks ship.
  - **Files**: `scripts/check-cloud-audit-gate.mjs` + tests, `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (new lint), `docs/security/audit-gate.md` (operator-facing — explains the gate + the unblock criteria).
  - **Verification**: synthetic PR touching `novel/cloud-supervisor/` is rejected by the lint; same diff after this task's `**Blocked**` line is removed → accepted.
  - **Measurement**: `node scripts/check-cloud-audit-gate.mjs --diff=test/fixtures/cloud-tier-without-audit.diff` exits 1; same with audit-cleared fixture exits 0.
  - **Pivot**: if no external audit firm has bandwidth in a 4-8w window, the gate stays — the cloud tier just doesn't ship. Don't auto-clear the gate based on internal review alone (that's exactly the failure mode the audit prevents).
  - **Acceptance**: gate lint shipped + `needs:`-ed; cloud-tier sub-tasks (in the `paid-tier-features-skeleton` block) reference this gate as a prerequisite; doc explains what triggers the gate clear.
  - **Anchor**: rule #13 (security & privacy as #2 priority after performance — this task implements minimum-bar item #6, "external security audit gate before cloud tier"); CNCF Security TAG's recommendation that multi-tenant infra ships with third-party audit; the empirical reality that every cloud-SaaS breach since 2020 (Log4Shell-adjacent, MOVEit, etc.) was eventually caught by external researchers — internal review is insufficient; rule #1 (use the existing audit-firm market — don't reinvent).
  - **Risk**: low for the gate itself (small CI lint); high for the cloud tier as a whole (this gate is what makes that risk manageable).
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-04 user brief — "Add P0 tasks to make sure that minsky is extremely secure and doesn't leak anything." Pairs with `paid-tier-features-skeleton` (P3) — this gate is the security prerequisite for that task's cloud sub-tasks.

- [ ] `gate-host-load-shed` — shed concurrent host load during a merge-gate vet so vitest stops flaking under 2-3x oversubscription
  - **ID**: gate-host-load-shed
  - **Tags**: gate, reliability, performance, orchestrator
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Competitive-goal**: raises minsky's autonomous-merge-rate + lowers change-fail-rate (DORA) on the `self-metrics-competitive-benchmark` scorecard by removing the last spurious-skip cause.
  - **Details**: 2026-05-17 root-caused the residual merge-rate-0 tail: with #590/#592/#593 + the 60-min vet timeout, the orchestrator's scratch vet of the clean MERGEABLE #591 still SKIP'd `gate red: vitest`. Isolated reproduction of the EXACT scratch (origin/main + #591 + `pnpm install` + `vitest`) passed **3039/3040** — the gate failure was a **load flake**: the host runs the orchestrator + worker daemon + other tenants concurrently at load ~14-26 on 10 cores, and a timing-sensitive test flaked under contention. Mitigated in the same PR by `vitest retry: 2` (a flake fails-then-passes; a real bug fails every attempt — Fowler 2011). This companion adds the durable structural fix: during a gate vet, shed competing load — e.g. pause/`SIGSTOP` the worker daemon's active iteration for the vet's duration, or `taskpolicy`/nice the scratch vet onto reserved cores, or serialize gate-vet vs worker-tick so they never run vitest simultaneously. Retry masks the symptom; this removes the cause.
  - **Files**: `scripts/local-gate-merge.mjs` (load-shed hook around `defaultVet`), `scripts/orchestrate.mjs` (coordinate with worker daemon), paired tests
  - **Acceptance**: during a gate vet, host load attributable to the worker daemon drops measurably (or vet runs on reserved cores); a 10-run gate-vet sample of a known-green PR yields 0 spurious `vitest` skips (was: intermittent under load).
  - **Hypothesis**: with load shedding, spurious `vitest` gate-red on green PRs → ~0; orchestrator autonomous-merge-rate per productive tick rises (retry alone leaves vet wall-time high under load).
  - **Success**: 10 consecutive gate vets of a seeded green PR all reach `gate green` (no vitest flake); vet p95 wall-time drops vs. the unshed baseline.
  - **Pivot**: if cooperative pause of the worker daemon is too invasive, fall back to retry-only (this PR) + raising `retry` to 3 and accept slower-but-correct vets.
  - **Measurement**: `grep -c 'gate red: vitest' .minsky/orchestrate.out.log` over a fixed window before/after → trends to 0 for green PRs; vet p95 from `.minsky/orchestrate.jsonl` timing.
  - **Anchor**: Fowler 2011 *Eradicating Non-Determinism in Tests*; Nygard 2018 *Release It!* (resource contention / bulkhead); vision.md rule #6.

- [ ] `worker-watchdog-scale-by-pinned-model-latency` — the 900s per-iteration watchdog default guarantees 100% iteration failure for Opus-pinned workers under host oversubscription; scale it by the pinned model's latency class
  - **ID**: worker-watchdog-scale-by-pinned-model-latency
  - **Tags**: tick-loop, daemon, reliability, throughput, healing
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Competitive-goal**: directly raises minsky's autonomous-merge-rate (DORA deployment-frequency) on the `self-metrics-competitive-benchmark` scorecard — a worker that never finishes an iteration produces zero PRs, so the merge pipeline starves regardless of gate health.
  - **Details**: 2026-05-17, supervising the Opus-on-Opus run, found every worker iteration failing with `claude-print-timeout: ~900515ms (child SIGKILLed by per-iteration watchdog)` on real implementation tasks (e.g. `runany-self-restart-bounded-timelimit`). Root cause: `novel/tick-loop/bin/tick-loop.mjs:339` defaults `claudePrintTimeoutMs` to `900_000` (15 min). That default was calibrated (comment at :332-335) for Claude's typical-iteration latency, but when a worker is operator-pinned via `MINSKY_STRATEGIC_PIN_MODEL=claude-opus-4-7` AND the host is 2-3x oversubscribed (orchestrator + 4 Opus workers + gate vets), a single Opus iteration (plan + multi-file edit + full `MINSKY_PRE_PR_STAGE=full` ~40-check gate) routinely exceeds 15 min — so it is SIGKILLed mid-flight, produces nothing, and wastes a full Opus iteration of budget against the near-exhausted 5h window (`fivehour:0.996` observed). Mitigated live by setting `MINSKY_CLAUDE_PRINT_TIMEOUT_MS=3000000` (50 min) on the `com.minsky.opus-sonnet-run` plist — but that is an out-of-repo operator workaround; the default should adapt. The watchdog should derive its ceiling from the pinned-model latency class (Opus > Sonnet > Haiku > local), or emit a loud span + auto-extend when >50% of recent iterations for a given pinned model hit the watchdog with zero edits (a 100%-timeout worker is strictly worse than a slower-but-completing one — the original 1h56m-hang rationale still bounds the max).
  - **Files**: `novel/tick-loop/bin/tick-loop.mjs` (watchdog default → pinned-model-aware), `novel/tick-loop/src/daemon.ts` (timeout-streak detector + span), paired tests
  - **Acceptance**: with `MINSKY_STRATEGIC_PIN_MODEL=claude-opus-4-7` and no explicit `MINSKY_CLAUDE_PRINT_TIMEOUT_MS`, the effective watchdog is ≥ a configurable Opus-class default (not the 15-min Claude default); a paired test covers each latency class; a worker that hits N consecutive zero-edit watchdog kills emits a `worker.watchdog-starvation` span.
  - **Hypothesis**: today Opus-pinned workers under oversubscription have a ~0% iteration-completion rate (100% SIGKILLed at 900s); with a pinned-model-aware default, completion rate rises to the host-load-bound baseline and the worker→PR→merge pipeline stops starving.
  - **Success**: post-fix, ≥1 in 3 Opus-pinned iterations completes with an edit under representative 2-3x host load (was 0 in the entire observed window); `worker.watchdog-starvation` span fires before, not after, hours of wasted budget.
  - **Pivot**: if model-latency-class tuning proves too coarse (Opus task variance too high), fall back to an adaptive watchdog that doubles from a low floor on each zero-edit timeout up to the 1h56m-hang safety ceiling, resetting on any completed iteration.
  - **Measurement**: `grep -c 'child SIGKILLed by per-iteration watchdog' .minsky/opus-sonnet-run.out.log` over a fixed window before/after the fix → completion ratio from `tick-loop.iteration` spans with `iteration.status:"succeeded"` vs `"failed"+timeout reason`.
  - **Live-data 2026-05-17 (operator escalation ladder, measured)**: env-driven override `MINSKY_CLAUDE_PRINT_TIMEOUT_MS` confirmed working end-to-end (the SIGKILL fired at the configured value, not the 900000ms default). Empirical ladder on the `com.minsky.opus-sonnet-run` plist under live 2-3x host oversubscription: **900000ms (15min)** → 100% iteration SIGKILL, 0 edits; **3000000ms (50min)** → still SIGKILL (observed exactly `claude-print-timeout: 3003395` ≈ 50min03s), 0 edits — 50min falsified; **5400000ms (90min)** → applied, under observation. Each value reloads only the worker agent (orchestrator launchd unit `com.minsky.orchestrator` is separate — its uptime clock is unaffected). **Pivot signal for the implementer**: if 90min ALSO yields a zero-edit SIGKILL, the bottleneck is NOT the watchdog value — it is the per-iteration workload (Opus plan+edit + full `MINSKY_PRE_PR_STAGE` ~40-check gate incl. 3000+ vitest with `retry:2`) being too heavy for one Opus turn under this contention. In that case land the code-side fix (pinned-model-aware default *plus* a lighter worker pre-PR stage / brief-shrink — compose with `gate-host-load-shed` and the local-LLM brief-shrinker) rather than ratcheting the watchdog toward the 1h56m-hang ceiling.
  - **Live-data 2026-05-17 ~06:02Z (90min DECISIVELY falsified — pivot fired, pre-registered)**: `5400000ms (90min)` yielded the same outcome as 15min and 50min: `claude-print-timeout: 5400768` and `5401036` (≈90min), `iteration.status:"failed"`, **0 edits, 0 `succeeded` across the entire 15/50/90-min ladder**. The pre-registered pivot threshold from the Pivot signal above is therefore MET: the bottleneck is the per-iteration workload, NOT the watchdog value — do NOT ratchet the watchdog further (the next rung approaches the 1h56m-hang ceiling and the ladder proves it would not help). **Operator-side pivot action TAKEN (no orchestrator restart, 10h clock preserved)**: switched the `com.minsky.opus-sonnet-run` plist `MINSKY_PRE_PR_STAGE` from `full`→`fast` and reloaded ONLY that agent (orchestrator launchd unit `com.minsky.orchestrator` pid 6685 confirmed `never exited`, `runs=1`, ~04:44 uptime — its 10h clock is untouched). Safe-for-merge rationale: per `novel/tick-loop/bin/tick-loop.mjs:1033-1038`, `fast` is the default/lighter pre-flight stage and `full` the heavy ~40-check merge-authority stage — but the orchestrator's autonomous-merge authority (and the manual `scripts/local-gate-merge.mjs`) re-runs the full `--stage=full` vet in its OWN scratch clone before merging regardless of what the worker ran, so worker `fast` only lightens pre-flight and does NOT weaken the merge gate. **Pre-registered sub-hypothesis (fast pivot)**: `full→fast` cuts per-iteration wall-time below the 90-min watchdog → ≥1 Opus iteration completes with edits within ~2h (was 0 across the whole ladder). **Success**: ≥1 `iteration.status:"succeeded"` / worker PR branch within ~2h of the `fast` reload, no 90-min SIGKILL on that iteration. **Pivot (next, if fast still 90-min-SIGKILLs zero-edit)**: bottleneck is Opus plan+edit itself, not the gate — escalate to model/brief-level (smaller task slices / brief-shrink / model routing per `[[task-aware-model-router]]`); still do NOT raise the watchdog. **Measurement**: post-`fast`-reload `grep -c 'iteration.status":"succeeded' .minsky/opus-sonnet-run.out.log` + `grep -cE 'claude-print-timeout: (5[4-9]|[6-9][0-9])[0-9]{5}'` + worker PR branches via `git for-each-ref`.
  - **Anchor**: live Opus-on-Opus run 2026-05-17 (100% iteration SIGKILL at 900515ms → 3003395ms → 5400768/5401036ms, all zero-edit; `fivehour:0.996` budget waste; ladder proves workload-bound not watchdog-bound); `novel/tick-loop/bin/tick-loop.mjs:332-343` (model-blind watchdog default + its 1h56m-hang rationale) and `:1033-1038` (`fast` default/light vs `full` merge-authority stage; orchestrator re-vets `full` in scratch at merge time); Nygard 2018 *Release It!* (timeout tuning / fail-fast vs. complete-slow); vision.md rule #6 (let-it-crash at the *right* boundary — a 100%-timeout worker crashes at the wrong one); rule #9 (pre-registered HDD — pivot committed before observing the `fast` result).

- [ ] `worker-liveness-detect-by-label-not-argv` — `workerDaemonAlive()` greps `tick-loop.mjs --worker-id=0`; with `--spawn-additional-workers=N` the root argv has no `--worker-id=0`, so liveness detection is fragile/coincidental
  - **ID**: worker-liveness-detect-by-label-not-argv
  - **Tags**: orchestrator, healing, detection, reliability, rule-6
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Competitive-goal**: a correct worker-liveness signal prevents spurious daemon kickstarts (avoids interrupting in-flight Opus iterations) → higher effective worker throughput on the `self-metrics-competitive-benchmark` scorecard.
  - **Details**: 2026-05-17 scaled workers to 4 via plist `--spawn-additional-workers=3` (root = worker-0, children `--worker-id=1/2/3`). `scripts/orchestrate.mjs` `workerDaemonAlive()` (line ~53) and the external heartbeat both do `pgrep -f 'tick-loop.mjs --worker-id=0'` — the root proc's argv is now `--spawn-additional-workers=3` (no `--worker-id=0` substring) and no child is `--worker-id=0`, so the intended match is gone. It currently returns true only because a STRAY pre-reload worker proc (old `--worker-id=0 --workers-total=1` argv) happens to still be running; if that stray exits, `workerDaemonAlive()` flips false and the conductor kickstarts the (healthy) 4-worker daemon every tick — disruptive churn that SIGKILLs in-flight Opus iterations. Heartbeat already false-alarms `worker=DOWN`. Fix: detect daemon liveness by launchctl label (`launchctl print gui/$uid/com.minsky.opus-sonnet-run` state=running, runs/last-exit) — the daemon's actual supervisor truth — instead of an argv-substring grep that breaks on any arg-shape change. Update the heartbeat probe identically.
  - **Files**: `scripts/orchestrate.mjs` (`workerDaemonAlive` → launchctl-label probe), heartbeat/monitor script, paired test in a new `scripts/orchestrate.test.mjs` (or extend) with injected probe
  - **Acceptance**: `workerDaemonAlive()` returns true whenever `com.minsky.opus-sonnet-run` is launchd-running regardless of `--worker-id`/`--spawn-additional-workers` arg shape, and false only when the agent is genuinely down; paired test covers both via injected launchctl-state seam; no kickstart when the daemon is up under `--spawn-additional-workers`.
  - **Hypothesis**: spurious worker-daemon kickstarts → 0 (currently latent: would fire every tick if the stray proc exits); in-flight Opus iterations no longer SIGKILLed by false heals.
  - **Success**: with the 4-worker daemon up, `decideHeal` returns "ok" across 5 consecutive ticks even after the stray `--worker-id=0` proc is killed; heartbeat reports worker=up.
  - **Pivot**: if launchctl-label parsing is brittle across macOS versions, fall back to a sentinel file the worker root touches each tick + an mtime-freshness check.
  - **Measurement**: `grep -c 'healed.*true\|kickstarted com.minsky.opus-sonnet-run' .minsky/orchestrate.out.log` over a window with the daemon healthy → 0; ledger `healed:false` stays consistent after killing the stray proc.
  - **Anchor**: vision.md rule #6 (supervisor owns restart — detect via the supervisor, not a brittle process-argv heuristic); Nygard 2018 *Release It!* (health checks must reflect real liveness, not a proxy that drifts).

- [ ] `gate-merge-delete-branch-vs-worktree-pin` — `gh pr merge --delete-branch` errors when a daemon worktree pins the PR head branch; the server-side merge succeeds but the gate mis-records it as a failed merge
  - **ID**: gate-merge-delete-branch-vs-worktree-pin
  - **Tags**: gate, merge, worktree, reliability, false-negative
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Competitive-goal**: removes a false-negative in the merge path → cleaner autonomous-merge-rate signal + lower MTTR on the `self-metrics-competitive-benchmark` scorecard.
  - **Details**: 2026-05-17 gate sweep on #580 logged `#580: merge call failed: Command failed: gh pr merge 580 --squash --admin --delete-branch — failed to delete local branch worktree-daemon-0-minsky-cli-context-aware-ux`. But `gh pr view 580` shows `state=MERGED merged=2026-05-17T02:18:33Z` — the server-side squash merge SUCCEEDED; only the post-merge LOCAL `git branch -d` failed because a parallel worker daemon worktree had that branch checked out (worktree pins the ref). `gh pr merge` does server-merge first, then local cleanup; the cleanup error makes `execFileSync` throw, so `processOnePr` records `merge-failed` for an already-merged PR. Harm: (a) the conductor may re-vet/re-attempt an already-merged PR (wasted bounded sweep slot), (b) the `merged[]` ledger undercounts real merges (corrupts the competitive scorecard's autonomous-merge-rate). Fix: in `defaultMerge`, detect "already merged" (treat as success — re-query `gh pr view --json state` on merge-call failure; MERGED ⇒ success), and/or pass merge without `--delete-branch` when the head branch is held by a worktree (`git worktree list --porcelain` check), leaving branch cleanup to a separate best-effort step (rule #6).
  - **Files**: `scripts/local-gate-merge.mjs` (`defaultMerge` + `processOnePr` post-merge state re-check), paired test in `scripts/local-gate-merge.test.mjs`
  - **Acceptance**: a PR whose head branch is worktree-pinned still records `merged` (not `merge-failed`) when GitHub reports `state=MERGED`; a genuinely failed merge still records `merge-failed`; paired tests cover both via injected `mergeFn`/state-probe.
  - **Hypothesis**: ledger `merged[]` count matches GitHub's actual merged-PR count over a window (currently undercounts by the worktree-pinned cases); 0 re-attempts of already-merged PRs.
  - **Success**: in a fixture where `mergeFn` throws a delete-branch error but the PR is server-MERGED, `processOnePr` returns `merged`; scorecard autonomous-merge-rate stops undercounting.
  - **Pivot**: if post-merge state re-query is rate-limit-costly, drop `--delete-branch` entirely from the gate merge and reap merged branches in a periodic GC pass.
  - **Measurement**: diff `grep -c '"merged":\[[0-9]' .minsky/orchestrate.jsonl`-derived count vs `gh pr list --state merged --search 'merged:>=<window>'` count → equal after fix.
  - **Anchor**: vision.md rule #6 (cleanup best-effort, never gates the result) + rule #10; the merge is the result, branch deletion is incidental cleanup.

- [ ] `gate-scratch-dir-gc` — sweep-start garbage-collect of stale `minsky-gate-*` scratch dirs, companion to the best-effort teardown
  - **ID**: gate-scratch-dir-gc
  - **Tags**: gate, hygiene, rule-6, reliability
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Competitive-goal**: lowers minsky's change-fail-rate / MTTR (DORA) by removing a class of sweep-aborting infra error → improves the autonomous-merge-rate metric on the competitive scorecard (`self-metrics-competitive-benchmark`).
  - **Details**: 2026-05-17 the orchestrator tick logged `sweepError: ENOTEMPTY` from `defaultVet`'s `finally` `rmSync` racing a SIGKILL'd vet's draining children; the throw escaped the finally and aborted the whole sweep (0 merges). Fixed in the same PR by `bestEffortRmScratch` (one retry, then swallow — rule #6, tmpdir is OS-reaped). This companion adds the durable hygiene half: at sweep START, GC any `minsky-gate-*` dir under `tmpdir()` older than the vet timeout AND not owned by a live vet pid, so leaked scratch dirs (from crashes/SIGKILL/operator process-kills) cannot accumulate and cannot cause a future ENOTEMPTY against a half-gone sibling.
  - **Files**: `scripts/local-gate-merge.mjs` (sweep-start GC pass + paired test in `scripts/local-gate-merge.test.mjs`)
  - **Acceptance**: given seeded stale `minsky-gate-XXX` dirs (mtime > timeout, no live owner), a sweep start removes them; a fresh dir owned by a running vet is left untouched; paired unit test proves both.
  - **Hypothesis**: with start-GC + best-effort teardown, `sweepError`-aborted ticks from scratch-dir races drop to ~0; autonomous-merge throughput per 10h run rises.
  - **Success**: zero `ENOTEMPTY`/`sweepError` scratch entries in `.minsky/orchestrate.jsonl` across a 10h run after deploy (was: ≥1 in the first hour).
  - **Pivot**: if mtime+pid ownership detection is unreliable cross-platform, GC only dirs older than 2× vet-timeout (coarser but safe) and accept slower reclamation.
  - **Measurement**: `grep -c ENOTEMPTY .minsky/orchestrate.jsonl` before/after over equal windows → after = 0.
  - **Anchor**: vision.md rule #6 (cleanup best-effort, never gates the loop); Nygard 2018 *Release It!* (housekeeping / resource leak prevention).

<!-- Taskgrind-extraction cluster 2026-05-12: taskgrind (`<taskgrind-repo>`, 5656 LOC bash + 32 bats suites) is deprecated alongside bosun in favour of minsky. Before it's archived, this sweep extracts the 2 patterns where taskgrind has battle-tested behaviour minsky genuinely lacks. Anti-recs (DO NOT port) documented at /tmp/taskgrind-to-minsky-extraction-analysis.md: `caffeinate -ms` and `taskpolicy -B` (macOS-specific, supervisor's job per launchd `KeepAlive` / `ProcessType`); `lib/watchdog.sh` signal escalation (minsky's launchd/systemd supervisor handles restart per rule #6 — daemon has no internal watchdog by design); `self-copy protection` (TypeScript loaded once at module-instantiation, not byte-offset-lazy like bash); per-repo `slot_lock_file` (already covered by the more sophisticated `daemon-parallel-worktree-launch` P0, which uses per-TASK `flock` claims instead of per-REPO locks — see line 426). The 2 P2 tasks below are the residual high-confidence extractions where minsky has a clear gap and taskgrind's pattern is portable to TypeScript. -->

- [ ] `gate-vet-timeout-circuit-breaker` — a PR that vet-times-out should be skipped for a cooldown instead of re-consuming a bounded sweep slot every tick
  - **ID**: gate-vet-timeout-circuit-breaker
  - **Tags**: gate, orchestrator, reliability, candidate-starvation
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Details**: Companion to the mergeability pre-resolution fix (this PR makes `defaultSnapshot` resolve TRUE mergeability via `gh pr view` so `pickGateCandidates` actually drops CONFLICTING PRs before the `limit` slice — previously `gh pr list` returned `UNKNOWN`, conflicts slipped to the 25-min scratch vet, and with `limit=2` the conductor perpetually re-vetted #577/#580 and never reached a mergeable PR: the merge-rate-0 starvation). Conflicts are now cheap-skipped, but a PR that genuinely vet-*times-out* (e.g. #580, a slow/hung test) still burns one full `MINSKY_GATE_VET_TIMEOUT_MS` (~25 min) slot on EVERY tick forever. Add a circuit-breaker: record per-PR-head timeout strikes (e.g. `.minsky/gate-timeout-strikes.json`, keyed by PR number + head SHA so a new push clears it) and skip a PR with ≥N recent strikes for a cooldown window, so one pathological PR cannot indefinitely consume the bounded sweep.
  - **Files**: `scripts/local-gate-merge.mjs` (strike record in `vetErrorToResult`/`processOnePr`, skip in `prepareSweep`), `scripts/local-gate-merge.test.mjs` (paired: strike accrues on timeout; skipped within cooldown; cleared on new head SHA)
  - **Acceptance**: a PR that timed out ≥2 ticks is pre-skipped (logged `timeout-circuit-open`) and consumes 0 vet slots until cooldown elapses OR its head SHA changes; non-timeout skips never accrue strikes; paired tests cover accrue/skip/clear.
  - **Hypothesis**: with conflicts cheap-skipped (this PR) AND timeouts circuit-broken (this task), a bounded `limit=2` sweep reaches a mergeable PR within 1 tick instead of never; orchestrator merge-rate per productive tick goes 0 → ≥1.
  - **Success**: in a sweep where candidates are [conflict, timeout-striked, green], only the green PR is vetted and it merges.
  - **Pivot**: if head-SHA-keyed strike state proves fragile across force-pushes, fall back to a simple per-PR-number N-strikes-then-cooldown with a short TTL.
  - **Measurement**: `.minsky/orchestrate.jsonl` — first `merged:[…]` non-empty tick after deploy; vet-slots-spent-on-timeouts per tick before/after.
  - **Anchor**: vision.md rule #6 (bounded, supervised) + rule #10; Nygard 2018 *Release It!* — Circuit Breaker stability pattern.
- [ ] `gate-scratch-resolvable-ratchet` — add a deterministic check that the local-merge-gate's scratch clone yields a module-resolvable workspace, so the zero-merge regression cannot recur silently
  - **ID**: gate-scratch-resolvable-ratchet
  - **Tags**: gate, ratchet, rule-10, regression-guard
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Details**: On 2026-05-16 the Opus orchestrator merged 0 PRs across every tick. Root cause: `scripts/local-gate-merge.mjs` `prepareScratchClone` symlinked only the *root* `node_modules`, but pnpm scatters a per-package `node_modules` symlink-farm across the workspace. A `git clone` scratch therefore had no nested `node_modules`, so `tsc -b` and `vitest` failed `Cannot find module` / `Failed to load url` for EVERY PR → the conductor correctly-but-uselessly skipped all of them. Fixed in the same PR by replacing the root-only symlink with an isolated `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --prefer-offline --ignore-scripts` (warm store ⇒ ~3.6s). The bug was silent because an infra-broken vet was misattributed as a per-PR gate-red. This task adds the *rule* so the class cannot return: a check that fails CI if the gate's scratch-prep cannot resolve a known workspace import.
  - **Files**: `scripts/local-gate-merge.mjs`, `scripts/` (new `check-gate-scratch-resolvable` + paired test), `package.json` (wire into the verify gate)
  - **Acceptance**: a deterministic check (a) builds a throwaway scratch via the real `prepareScratchClone` path, (b) asserts resolution succeeds for a representative workspace pkg (`@minsky/adapter-types`) AND a registry dep (`@opentelemetry/api`), (c) fails loudly if either is unresolvable; wired into `npm run verify`; paired unit test proves it goes red when the install step is stubbed out.
