  - **Hypothesis**: with the ratchet present, a future edit that reverts `prepareScratchClone` to symlink-only (or otherwise breaks scratch resolution) fails the verify gate before it can merge, so the recurrence mode changes from "silent until a human notices 0 merges" to "caught at PR time".
  - **Success**: stubbing the install step makes the check exit non-zero; the unmodified path exits 0.
  - **Pivot**: if standing up a full scratch in the check is too slow for `verify` (>30s), assert the invariant structurally instead — grep that `prepareScratchClone` performs a real install and not a bare root-only symlink — accepting the weaker guarantee.
  - **Measurement**: `npm run verify` exit code with vs. without the install step stubbed; CI duration delta < 30s.
  - **Anchor**: vision.md rule #10 (deterministic enforcement / ratchet); global rule "every bug becomes a rule — prevent the category, not the instance".
- [ ] `daemon-network-resilience-detector` — minsky daemon detects network loss, pauses the iteration timer, and extends the deadline on recovery so long-running daemons survive Wi-Fi drops without wasting iterations
  - **ID**: daemon-network-resilience-detector
  - **Tags**: p2, daemon, supervisor, stability, network, taskgrind-extraction
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Touches**: `novel/tick-loop/src/network-resilience.ts` (new), `novel/tick-loop/src/network-resilience.test.ts` (new), `novel/tick-loop/src/daemon.ts` (wire into iteration loop), `experiments/network-resilience-detector-2026-05-12.yaml` (new — rule-#9 substrate)
  - **Hypothesis**: today the minsky daemon has only *passive* network-error handling — `claude-health-probe.ts` classifies "ECONNREFUSED" / "transient ENETUNREACH" as `error` rather than `exhausted` and the next iteration retries the same broken path. There's no ACTIVE network-loss detection that pauses the iteration timer or extends a deadline. On a 1-hour Wi-Fi drop, the daemon burns iterations spawning claude (which fails fast), the watchdog fires, the iteration loop keeps grinding — wasting tokens AND consuming the budget envelope that should be defended across the outage. Taskgrind's `bin/taskgrind:1167-1209` ships `_check_network_once` + `check_network` retry wrapper (3 retries × 2s delay before declaring down; configurable via `TG_NET_RETRIES` / `TG_NET_RETRY_DELAY`); paired with `bin/taskgrind:4566-4595` `wait_for_network` loop that pauses the deadline and extends it by the wait duration on recovery. Pre-registration: post-fix, **(a)** `NetworkResilienceDetector` ships behind an interface (`isNetworkUp(): Promise<boolean>`, `waitForNetwork(maxWaitMs): Promise<{ recovered, waitedMs }>`) at `novel/tick-loop/src/network-resilience.ts`; **(b)** the daemon's main loop calls `isNetworkUp` after every iteration that ended with `error`-classified output AND the iteration wall-clock was < 30s (fast-failure signal — a healthy iteration with no work-to-do takes longer); **(c)** on detected loss, the loop calls `waitForNetwork(maxWaitMs = MINSKY_NET_MAX_WAIT_MS || 3600_000)` which polls every 30s (configurable via `MINSKY_NET_POLL_MS`); **(d)** on recovery, the iteration deadline is extended by the wait duration (rather than the iteration counter incrementing); **(e)** a new `tick-loop.network.outage` span attribute records each detected outage with `{ start_ts, end_ts, recovered: bool, waited_ms }`; **(f)** simulated 1-hour outage test: iteration count stays unchanged, deadline shifts by ≥3600s, `network.outage` span emitted with `recovered=true`. If (f) over-fires on transient DNS hiccups (≥2 false-positive outages per 24h sustained), tighten the retry logic — require 3 consecutive fail bursts (not just 3 retries in one check) before declaring down. If (f) doesn't fire at all in 30d (no real outages observed in operator's environment), the value isn't iteration-budget-defence (the operator's network is fine) — retire the deadline-extension and keep only the body-text `error`-classification path that already exists.
  - **Details**: (a) New file `novel/tick-loop/src/network-resilience.ts` — pure `decideNetworkState(opts): { up: boolean; reason: string }` (consumes injected fetch seam + retry config) + I/O-edge `runNetworkCheck(opts): Promise<NetworkCheckResult>` (wraps `fetch` with timeout). The check URL is `https://connectivitycheck.gstatic.com/generate_204` by default (matches taskgrind's pattern + zero-byte response = minimal bandwidth); operator override via `MINSKY_NET_CHECK_URL`. (b) `runNetworkCheck` uses the existing `fetch` global (Node 20 native); 5s timeout per attempt; 3 retries × 2s delay (same as taskgrind's defaults). (c) New file `novel/tick-loop/src/network-resilience-loop.ts` — wraps the existing iteration loop with `waitForNetwork` calls; pure logic (deadline extension) + I/O-edge (sleep). (d) Daemon integration in `daemon.ts`: after each iteration, if `iteration.verdict === 'error'` AND iteration `wall_ms < 30_000`, call `decideNetworkState`; on down, enter the wait loop; on recovery, log the extension and continue. (e) Configurable thresholds via env: `MINSKY_NET_CHECK_URL`, `MINSKY_NET_MAX_WAIT_MS` (default `3600000`), `MINSKY_NET_POLL_MS` (default `30000`), `MINSKY_NET_RETRIES` (default `3`), `MINSKY_NET_RETRY_DELAY_MS` (default `2000`). (f) Telemetry: new `tick-loop.network.outage` span emitted on detected outages, attributes per Hypothesis (e); new `tick-loop.network.up`/`tick-loop.network.down` counters for the dashboard's Watch surface (story 005's 10-metric budget allows this — replace one of the existing slots if at cap).
  - **Files**: `novel/tick-loop/src/network-resilience.ts` (new — pure + I/O-edge split), `novel/tick-loop/src/network-resilience.test.ts` (new — paired tests covering the 5 chaos rows: network-up, network-down-recovers, network-down-times-out, DNS-hiccup-recovers-within-retries, malformed-response), `novel/tick-loop/src/network-resilience-loop.ts` (new — deadline-extension logic; pure), `novel/tick-loop/src/daemon.ts` (wire into iteration lifecycle), `novel/adapters/observability/src/otel.ts` (register new span + counters; if it conflicts with the otel.ts surface limit, accept the increment under threat-model gate), `novel/tick-loop/README.md` (document the env vars + behaviour), `experiments/network-resilience-detector-2026-05-12.yaml` (new).
  - **Verification**: `pnpm vitest run novel/tick-loop/src/network-resilience.test.ts` exits 0 with ≥ 7 paired tests passing (5 chaos rows + 2 retry-wrapper rows). Integration test: inject a fake `fetch` that returns 500/ECONNREFUSED for 3 ticks then 204; assert `waitForNetwork` returns `{ recovered: true, waitedMs ≥ 90000 }`. `MINSKY_NET_MAX_WAIT_MS=10000 MINSKY_NET_POLL_MS=1000 node novel/tick-loop/dist/network-resilience.js --self-test` exits 0 after ≤ 12s.
  - **Measurement**: `pnpm vitest run novel/tick-loop/src/network-resilience.test.ts --reporter=json 2>/dev/null | jq -e '.numPassedTests >= 7 and .numFailedTests == 0' && grep -cE 'MINSKY_NET_CHECK_URL|MINSKY_NET_MAX_WAIT_MS|MINSKY_NET_POLL_MS' novel/tick-loop/src/network-resilience.ts | grep -qE '^[3-9]$|^[1-9][0-9]+$' && grep -q 'tick-loop.network.outage' novel/tick-loop/src/network-resilience-loop.ts`
  - **Pivot**: see Hypothesis (f). Two falsifiable thresholds: (i) ≥ 2 false-positive outages per 24h sustained 7d → tighten retry logic OR retire deadline-extension; (ii) 0 detected outages in 30d → retire the new path entirely and keep only the existing body-text `error` classification (the network on the operator's machine is stable enough that this substrate doesn't pay for itself).
  - **Acceptance**: (1) `NetworkResilienceDetector` interface + impl ship with paired tests covering the 5 chaos rows; (2) `decideNetworkState` is a pure function (rule #2 — Strategy seam); (3) daemon integration is opt-in via `MINSKY_NET_RESILIENCE=1` env (default OFF until 7d of dogfood validates the false-positive rate); (4) all 5 env vars wired with documented defaults; (5) new OTEL span + counters ship; (6) experiment substrate passes the 3 substrate lints + ci-experiment-runner gate; (7) README documents the env vars + the chaos table.
  - **Anchor**: `<taskgrind-repo>/bin/taskgrind:1167-1212` (the `_check_network_once` + `check_network` retry wrapper); `<taskgrind-repo>/bin/taskgrind:4566-4595` (the `wait_for_network` loop with deadline extension; verified by direct read of the function body); `<taskgrind-repo>/tests/network.bats` (the regression-test pattern this task adopts); Nygard, M., *Release It! Design and Deploy Production-Ready Software*, Pragmatic Bookshelf, 2007, ISBN 978-1680502398, Ch. 4 (Network resilience — circuit breaker probe + half-open recovery is what this substrate IS); Beyer, B., Jones, C., Petoff, J., Murphy, N., *Site Reliability Engineering*, O'Reilly, 2016, ISBN 978-1491929124, Ch. 3 (Error budget — network outages consume the budget; deadline-extension recovers it); rule #1 (don't reinvent — taskgrind's pattern is 3500+ commits of production observation); rule #2 (Strategy seam — `decideNetworkState` is the pure decision, `runNetworkCheck` is the I/O edge); rule #6 (stay alive — the daemon doesn't crash on outage, it waits gracefully); rule #7 (chaos coverage — the 5 chaos rows pin the contract); rule #9 (HDD substrate above).
  - **Risk**: low-medium. Risk is false-positive outages causing unnecessary deadline extensions (a transient DNS hiccup → 30s pause → operator confusion). Mitigation: 3-retry wrapper before declaring down; opt-in via `MINSKY_NET_RESILIENCE=1` so 7d dogfood validates before becoming default; chaos row "malformed-response → graceful-degrade to up (the network IS up, just the check URL is misbehaving)".
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-12 taskgrind-extraction analysis. Verified file:line by directly reading `bin/taskgrind:1167-1212` and `4566-4595` (functions confirmed present, retry-wrapper config confirmed via `DVB_DEFAULT_NET_RETRIES` constant in `lib/constants.sh`).

- [ ] `daemon-stall-detection-per-task` — minsky daemon tracks per-task retry attempts in `.minsky/state.json` and bails out on a task that has been retried too many times, preventing infinite loops on broken tasks
  - **ID**: daemon-stall-detection-per-task
  - **Tags**: p2, daemon, supervisor, stability, stall-detection, resume-state, taskgrind-extraction
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Touches**: `novel/tick-loop/src/stall-detector.ts` (new), `novel/tick-loop/src/stall-detector.test.ts` (new), `novel/tick-loop/src/claude-exhaustion-state.ts` (extend `.minsky/state.json` schema), `novel/tick-loop/src/daemon.ts` (wire into iteration lifecycle), `experiments/stall-detection-per-task-2026-05-12.yaml` (new — rule-#9 substrate)
  - **Hypothesis**: today minsky's watchdog substrate covers per-iteration timing (`daemon-claude-print-hang-watchdog` — claude `--print` hang detection), per-PR aging (`daemon-pr-age-watchdog`, `daemon-stuck-pr-rebase-watchdog`), and duplicate-PR detection (`daemon-duplicate-work-detection`). NONE of them track per-task retry attempts. A broken task — e.g. one where the worker keeps writing a fix that ships a PR, the PR fails CI, the daemon picks the same task again, writes a similar fix, ships another PR — can ship 5-10 broken PRs against the same task ID before any watchdog notices. Taskgrind's `bin/taskgrind:690,693,3000+` ships `max_zero_ship` (consecutive-zero-ship streak detector — bail at 6) AND a per-task-ID retry counter persisted in `.taskgrind-state` (resume file). Pre-registration: post-fix, **(a)** `.minsky/state.json` schema gains a `task_attempts: { [taskId]: { count: number; lastAttemptAt: epoch; lastVerdict: string } }` map; **(b)** `StallDetector` interface ships at `novel/tick-loop/src/stall-detector.ts` with `recordAttempt(taskId, verdict)` + `getTaskAttempts(taskId): number` + `shouldSkipTask(taskId): { skip: bool; reason: string }`; **(c)** the daemon's `pickTask` consults `shouldSkipTask` before claiming — if the task's attempts exceed `MINSKY_MAX_TASK_ATTEMPTS` (default 3), the task is skipped with a `task-attempts-exhausted` verdict written to TASKS.md as a `**Status**: blocked` field; **(d)** a NEW second axis: `consecutive_zero_ship: number` in `.minsky/state.json` tracks how many iterations in a row produced zero merged PRs (capped at `MINSKY_MAX_ZERO_SHIP`, default 6); **(e)** on a consecutive-zero-ship streak ≥ `MINSKY_SELF_INVESTIGATE_ZERO_SHIP`, default 3, the daemon files a self-diagnose task (`daemon-self-detect-throughput-issues` overlap — composes); on the cap (default 6), the daemon writes a `Blocked: needs-operator-investigation` row to TASKS.md and exits 1; **(f)** the rolling-30d count of "same task ID gets ≥ 4 PR attempts" → 0 (currently rare, but observed during the 2026-05-05 watchdog-window monitoring window). If (f) is already 0 (no real broken tasks observed in 30d), the per-task cap is over-engineered for the symptom — pivot to consecutive-zero-ship-only detection (drop the per-task map) and keep the resume-state foundation for other future state.
  - **Details**: (a) Extend `.minsky/state.json` schema (managed via `claude-exhaustion-state.ts`'s existing read/write helpers — rule #2, single resume-state surface). New fields: `task_attempts: Record<string, { count, lastAttemptAt, lastVerdict }>` and `consecutive_zero_ship: number` and `last_zero_ship_at: number | null`. (b) New pure module `novel/tick-loop/src/stall-detector.ts` with `decideStallVerdict(state, opts): { verdict: 'continue' | 'self-investigate' | 'task-skip' | 'bail'; reason: string; nextState: StallState }`. Pure function — caller injects the current state, gets back the verdict + next state to persist. (c) Daemon integration: after every iteration, read `.minsky/state.json`, call `decideStallVerdict`, write the next state back. `pickTask` queries `getTaskAttempts` and the per-task verdict to filter the queue. (d) When the daemon writes a `task-attempts-exhausted` skip to TASKS.md, it includes a `**Pushback**: task hit MINSKY_MAX_TASK_ATTEMPTS (3); see <PR1>, <PR2>, <PR3>` field so the operator can pick up the trail. (e) Resume across daemon restart: the next daemon process reads `.minsky/state.json` and resumes the per-task counters — no double-counting, no reset. (f) Operator escape hatch: `rm .minsky/state.json::task_attempts` (or `jq 'del(.task_attempts.<id>)' .minsky/state.json | sponge .minsky/state.json`) resets a specific task; documented in `novel/tick-loop/README.md`.
  - **Files**: `novel/tick-loop/src/stall-detector.ts` (new — pure decideStallVerdict), `novel/tick-loop/src/stall-detector.test.ts` (new — paired tests covering 8 verdict cells: continue × at-cap × self-investigate × bail × task-skip × across-restart × multi-task × operator-override), `novel/tick-loop/src/claude-exhaustion-state.ts` (extend schema to include task_attempts + consecutive_zero_ship), `novel/tick-loop/src/claude-exhaustion-state.test.ts` (extend tests for the new fields), `novel/tick-loop/src/daemon.ts` (wire into iteration lifecycle + pickTask), `novel/tick-loop/README.md` (document the env vars + the operator escape hatch), `experiments/stall-detection-per-task-2026-05-12.yaml` (new).
  - **Verification**: `pnpm vitest run novel/tick-loop/src/stall-detector.test.ts` exits 0 with ≥ 8 paired tests. Integration test: simulate 3 consecutive failed iterations against task-id-X via injected verdicts; assert `pickTask` skips task-id-X on the 4th iteration AND `**Status**: blocked` + `**Pushback**:` written to TASKS.md. `pnpm vitest run novel/tick-loop/src/claude-exhaustion-state.test.ts` still passes (extended schema, no regression on existing tests).
  - **Measurement**: `pnpm vitest run novel/tick-loop/src/stall-detector.test.ts --reporter=json 2>/dev/null | jq -e '.numPassedTests >= 8 and .numFailedTests == 0' && grep -qE 'task_attempts|consecutive_zero_ship' novel/tick-loop/src/claude-exhaustion-state.ts && grep -qE 'MINSKY_MAX_TASK_ATTEMPTS|MINSKY_MAX_ZERO_SHIP' novel/tick-loop/src/stall-detector.ts`
  - **Pivot**: see Hypothesis (f). If 30d of dogfood shows zero "broken task with ≥4 attempts" events, retire the per-task cap (keep the consecutive-zero-ship cap — that's still a useful liveness check). If the consecutive-zero-ship cap fires false-positively on a healthy idle daemon (queue empty + no new tasks for hours), raise the threshold to 12 OR add a "queue-emptiness" exemption.
  - **Acceptance**: (1) `.minsky/state.json` schema extended with `task_attempts` + `consecutive_zero_ship`; (2) `stall-detector.ts` ships with ≥ 8 paired tests covering the 4 verdict cells × 2 axes; (3) `pickTask` consults the stall detector; (4) on task-attempts-exhausted, TASKS.md gets `**Status**: blocked` + `**Pushback**:` fields; (5) on consecutive-zero-ship cap, daemon writes a self-diagnose task and exits 1; (6) operator escape hatch (`jq -i 'del(.task_attempts.<id>)' .minsky/state.json`) documented; (7) experiment substrate passes the 3 substrate lints + ci-experiment-runner gate.
  - **Anchor**: `<taskgrind-repo>/bin/taskgrind:690,693` (the `max_zero_ship` / `self_investigate_zero_ship_streak` constants — verified present); `<taskgrind-repo>/bin/taskgrind:3000+` (the main-loop zero-ship-count tracking — verified by grep for `zero_ship_count` + `task_attempts`); `<taskgrind-repo>/docs/resume-state.md` (the resume-file lifecycle — minsky inherits the same lifecycle but in JSON instead of YAML); Lamport, L., "The Implementation of Reliable Distributed Multiprocess Systems", *Computer Networks*, 2 (2), 1978 (fault-stop semantics — the per-task cap is a fail-stop mechanism); Beyer SRE 2016 Ch. 3 (error-budget — every retry burns budget; the cap bounds the burn); rule #1 (taskgrind's stall detection is battle-tested); rule #2 (`stall-detector.ts` is the pure decision, `claude-exhaustion-state.ts` is the I/O edge — single resume-state surface); rule #6 (stay alive across daemon restart — the resume state is the durable substrate); rule #9 (HDD substrate above — falsifiable per (f)); rule #12 (scope discipline — this is stability work on existing watchdog substrate, not new scope).
  - **Risk**: medium. Risk is a slow-but-healthy task getting marked as stalled after 3 attempts (false positive). Mitigation: the per-task cap is configurable via `MINSKY_MAX_TASK_ATTEMPTS=N`; the `**Pushback**:` field gives the operator full audit trail (which 3 PRs were the failed attempts) before unblocking; the operator escape hatch (`jq -i 'del(.task_attempts.<id>)'`) is one-line. Secondary risk: TASKS.md mutation during the daemon's own iteration (the daemon writes `**Status**: blocked` to a task it just claimed). Mitigation: serialize via the existing `daemon-tasks-md-auto-lint-fix` substrate so the lint failures don't deadlock the pre-PR gate.
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-12 taskgrind-extraction analysis. Verified file:line by directly reading `bin/taskgrind:680-700` (the constants block) and grep for `zero_ship_count` + `task_attempts` in `bin/taskgrind` (confirmed present in the main loop). `docs/resume-state.md` confirmed the resume-file lifecycle pattern this task inherits.

- [ ] `taskgrind-extraction-sweep-followups` — meta-task tracking the 2 taskgrind subsystems anti-rec'd in the 2026-05-12 sweep + 1 candidate deferred for evidence collection
  - **ID**: taskgrind-extraction-sweep-followups
  - **Tags**: p3, meta, taskgrind-extraction, audit, deferred
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Touches**: `TASKS.md`, `/tmp/taskgrind-to-minsky-extraction-analysis.md`
  - **Hypothesis**: the 2026-05-12 taskgrind-extraction sweep filed 2 high-confidence P2 tasks (`daemon-network-resilience-detector`, `daemon-stall-detection-per-task`) and explicitly anti-rec'd 4 subsystems (caffeinate, taskpolicy, self-copy protection, watchdog signal escalation — all properly handled by minsky's supervisor units or not applicable to TypeScript). It also identified the slot-locker as REDUNDANT with the existing P0 `daemon-parallel-worktree-launch` (which uses per-TASK `flock` claims, more sophisticated than taskgrind's per-REPO locks). Pre-registration: by 2026-09-01, run a one-shot revisit pass against the anti-rec'd subsystems with grounded data from the daemon's own observability. For each, the verdict is either (a) confirm the anti-rec stands (the supervisor unit's behaviour is observably equivalent), or (b) file a follow-up task because the anti-rec proved wrong in practice. If by 2026-09-01 fewer than 2 of the 4 anti-recs can be either confirmed or overturned with grounded data, retire the meta-task — the verdicts are stable enough that no further audit is warranted. Taskgrind is archived after the meta-task resolves.
  - **Details**: anti-rec'd subsystems to confirm or overturn by 2026-09-01:
    - **`taskgrind-caffeinate-anti-rec`** — confirmed-stands evidence: launchd plist's `KeepAlive = true` + `ProcessType = Background` directives keep the daemon alive across system sleep on macOS without invoking `caffeinate -ms`. Confirm by checking `pmset -g log` for 30d post-rollout: zero "system slept while minsky daemon was active" events. If found, OVERTURN: file `daemon-caffeinate-wrapper-port` to wrap the daemon launch in `caffeinate -ms` via the launchd plist.
    - **`taskgrind-taskpolicy-anti-rec`** — confirmed-stands evidence: launchd `ProcessType = Background` (or `Adaptive`) sets the scheduling tier such that minsky's claude-print invocations don't experience > 100ms scheduler latency on macOS. Confirm by checking iteration p95 latency over 30d: stays within ±10 % of the pre-supervisor-unit baseline. If found, OVERTURN: file `daemon-taskpolicy-wrapper-port`.
    - **`taskgrind-watchdog-anti-rec`** — confirmed-stands evidence: the launchd/systemd supervisor's `Restart=on-failure` + `RestartSec=5` restart cycle handles all daemon-crash scenarios without an internal watchdog (rule #6 — let it crash). Confirm by checking 30d of `.minsky/tick-loop.out.log` for any daemon-hang scenarios that survived for >5min before restart. If found, OVERTURN: file `daemon-internal-watchdog-port` with the SIGINT→SIGTERM→SIGKILL escalation from `lib/watchdog.sh:61-161`.
    - **`taskgrind-slot-locker-redundancy-verified`** — already verified-redundant against the P0 `daemon-parallel-worktree-launch` (per-TASK granularity vs. per-REPO). Confirm by checking that the parallel-worktree-launch task shipped with no slot-collision regressions vs. taskgrind's per-repo model. If parallel-worktree-launch is shipped + zero slot-collisions in 30d, the redundancy verdict stands.
  - **Files**: `TASKS.md` (this meta-task block updated as anti-recs are confirmed/overturned).
  - **Verification**: by 2026-09-01, ≥ 2 of the 4 anti-recs have been resolved (confirmed-stands OR overturned-with-follow-up-task). The meta-task block is updated to mark each anti-rec's resolution date + outcome.
  - **Measurement**: `node -e "const t = require('fs').readFileSync('TASKS.md','utf8'); const m = t.match(/taskgrind-extraction-sweep-followups[\s\S]*?(?=^- \[ \])/m); if (!m) process.exit(1); const resolved = (m[0].match(/Verdict (confirmed-stands|overturned-with-follow-up)/g) || []).length; process.exit(resolved >= 2 ? 0 : 1)"` exits 0 by 2026-09-01.
  - **Pivot**: if by 2026-09-01 fewer than 2 anti-recs can be resolved (the supervisor units' behaviour is too rarely-exercised to surface confirmation data), retire the meta-task — the verdicts are stable. Bosun + taskgrind get archived (read-only) and any unported pattern stays unported.
  - **Acceptance**: by 2026-09-01, each of the 4 anti-recs has either (a) verdict `confirmed-stands` with the data that confirmed it, or (b) verdict `overturned-with-follow-up: <task-id>` filing a concrete extraction with rule-#9 substrate.
  - **Anchor**: full analysis at `/tmp/taskgrind-to-minsky-extraction-analysis.md` (see § 4 for the 4 anti-recs); rule #1 (don't reinvent — but also don't port things minsky's supervisor unit already does); rule #12 (scope discipline — every potential port is gated on observability evidence); Ries, E., *The Lean Startup*, Crown Business, 2011, ISBN 978-0307887894 (validated learning — port-decisions for anti-recs are now gated on data, not speculation).
  - **Risk**: low. Meta-task is documentation + a 2026-09-01 deadline. Failure mode is the meta-task gets abandoned and the anti-recs silently rot. Mitigation: when taskgrind is finally archived (separate task), trigger a forced-close of this meta-task as part of the archive PR.
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-12 taskgrind-extraction analysis.
<!-- Bosun-extraction cluster 2026-05-12: bosun is deprecated. These tasks extract battle-tested patterns from `<bosun-repo>/orchestrator/server/src/` where minsky has a clear gap and bosun's implementation is portable. Anti-recs (DO NOT port) are documented in /tmp/bosun-to-minsky-extraction-analysis.md: bosun's pipeline engine (minsky uses OMC per rule #1), bosun's persona skills (live in OMC), bosun's API routes + frontend (minsky is CLI + daemon, not a web service per rule #12), bosun's `eventBus.ts` (just 10-line wrapper over Node EventEmitter — not worth a port), and bosun's full learning bridge chain (minsky's iteration model is different). The 4 P2 tasks below are the high-confidence extractions; further smaller patterns are tracked under `bosun-extraction-sweep-followups` in P3. -->

- [ ] `daemon-multi-provider-rate-limit-detector` — minsky gains a fixture-tested rate-limit detection pattern set covering Claude + aider/mlx + opencode/LM Studio + Devin so the daemon stops retry-looping on exhausted backends
  - **ID**: daemon-multi-provider-rate-limit-detector
  - **Tags**: p2, daemon, supervisor, stability, rate-limit, bosun-extraction
  - **Milestone**: M4
  - **Touches**: `novel/tick-loop/src/llm-provider-selector.ts`, `novel/tick-loop/src/claude-health-probe.ts`, `novel/tick-loop/test/fixtures/rate-limit-bodies.json` (new), `experiments/multi-provider-rate-limit-detector-2026-05-12.yaml` (new — rule-#9 substrate)
  - **Hypothesis**: minsky's existing rate-limit detection is Claude-only — `HARD_LIMIT_PATTERNS` in `novel/tick-loop/src/llm-provider-selector.ts` (`usage limit`, `rate limit`, `429`, `quota exceeded`, `limit reached`, `limit will reset`) and the `classifyClaudeProbeOutput` classifier in `novel/tick-loop/src/claude-health-probe.ts` only fire on Anthropic's CLI shape. When the LOCAL stack hits an exhaustion edge (mlx_lm.server OOM, aider rate-limit on a remote OpenRouter-backed config, opencode misroute to a non-existent endpoint), the daemon does not classify it as `exhausted` and retries the same broken path until the watchdog fires. Bosun's `<bosun-repo>/orchestrator/server/src/service/backendHealthService.ts:11-30` carries a 10-pattern shared set across Anthropic, Devin, generic-throttle, and Claude-SDK shapes (`'hit your limit'`, `'limit · resets'`, `'overloaded'`, `'too many requests'`, `'out of usage'`, plus `429` / `quota exceeded` / `throttl` / `usage limit` / `rate limit`). The bosun shape carries an inline comment (line 19) explaining which observed pipeline IDs surfaced each pattern — the patterns are anchored in real session traces, not generated. Pre-registration: post-fix, **(a)** `RATE_LIMIT_PATTERNS` (renamed from `HARD_LIMIT_PATTERNS`) is the single shared substrate consumed by both `decideProvider` and `classifyClaudeProbeOutput`; **(b)** the pattern set carries ≥ 4 non-Claude-shaped entries (the `hit your limit`, `limit · resets`, `overloaded`, `out of usage` bosun additions), with the inline comment naming the observed body that surfaced each; **(c)** `novel/tick-loop/test/fixtures/rate-limit-bodies.json` ships ≥ 12 redacted real-world bodies (5 Claude / 3 aider+mlx / 2 opencode+LM-Studio / 2 other), each paired with the pattern it should match; **(d)** a new paired test iterates every fixture body through `detectRateLimit` and asserts the expected pattern hit; **(e)** post-fix the daemon's `tick-loop.iteration.classified-as-exhausted` span attribute fires on non-Claude exhaustion events too (verified by injecting a redacted aider+mlx OOM body via the existing `claudeProbeFn` seam). If (e) doesn't move the needle (rate-limit-classified iterations stay ≈ 0 over the next 7d), the gap isn't pattern coverage — it's that the local stack doesn't surface its exhaustion in a textual body. Pivot in that case: add a structured probe (`huggingface-cli`/`mlx-lm`/`opencode --health` exit codes) to `decideProvider`'s input and retire the body-text approach for the local providers.
  - **Details**: (a) Refactor `HARD_LIMIT_PATTERNS` → `RATE_LIMIT_PATTERNS` in `novel/tick-loop/src/llm-provider-selector.ts`; export it so `claude-health-probe.ts` shares the same set (rule #2 — single source of truth). Bosun's substrate at `orchestrator/server/src/service/backendHealthService.ts:11-30` is the seed list — copy the patterns, keep the inline comments that name the surfacing pipeline IDs (replace with minsky-side surfacing observations as new patterns get added). (b) Add `novel/tick-loop/test/fixtures/rate-limit-bodies.json` modeled on bosun's `orchestrator/server/src/__tests__/fixtures/rate-limit-bodies.json` — same shape (`{ name, body, expectedMatch, source: "claude-cli" | "aider-mlx" | "opencode-lm-studio" | "other" }`). Seed with the 5+ existing Claude bodies from minsky's own iteration logs and 4 new redacted samples from the local stack. (c) New paired test `llm-provider-selector.fixture.test.ts` iterates every fixture and asserts `detectRateLimit(body) === fixture.expectedMatch` AND that the matched pattern is the expected one — fail-loud if a body slips through. (d) `decideProvider` and `classifyClaudeProbeOutput` both consume the shared list — change one place, both detectors update. (e) Document the fixture workflow in `novel/tick-loop/README.md` § "Rate-limit detector fixture workflow" mirroring bosun's AGENTS.md instruction at `<bosun-repo>/AGENTS.md:138-153`.
  - **Files**: `novel/tick-loop/src/llm-provider-selector.ts` (refactor + extend patterns), `novel/tick-loop/src/claude-health-probe.ts` (consume shared `RATE_LIMIT_PATTERNS`), `novel/tick-loop/test/fixtures/rate-limit-bodies.json` (new), `novel/tick-loop/src/llm-provider-selector.fixture.test.ts` (new), `novel/tick-loop/README.md` (document fixture workflow), `experiments/multi-provider-rate-limit-detector-2026-05-12.yaml` (new — rule-#9 substrate).
  - **Verification**: `pnpm vitest run novel/tick-loop/src/llm-provider-selector.fixture.test.ts` exits 0 with ≥ 12 passing fixture cases. `pnpm vitest run novel/tick-loop` overall test count rises by the new fixture suite (`(was N)` + 12 minimum) with zero regressions. `grep -c '^  "name":' novel/tick-loop/test/fixtures/rate-limit-bodies.json` returns ≥ 12.
  - **Measurement**: `pnpm vitest run novel/tick-loop/src/llm-provider-selector.fixture.test.ts --reporter=json 2>/dev/null | jq -e '.numPassedTests >= 12 and .numFailedTests == 0' && grep -c '^  "name":' novel/tick-loop/test/fixtures/rate-limit-bodies.json | grep -qE '^[1-9][0-9]+$' && node scripts/check-rate-limit-fixture-shape.mjs`
  - **Pivot**: see Hypothesis (e). If the local-stack exhaustion doesn't manifest as a textual body within the iteration's captured stderr, retire the body-text approach for local providers and switch to structured probes (binary exit codes, `/health` endpoints). The Claude side of the detector stays body-text-based because Anthropic's CLI exit code is `0` even on hard-limit.
  - **Acceptance**: (1) `RATE_LIMIT_PATTERNS` is the single shared substrate; (2) ≥ 4 new non-Claude-shaped patterns added with inline source comments; (3) ≥ 12 fixture bodies cover the 4 source classes (Claude / aider+mlx / opencode+LM-Studio / other); (4) fixture-driven paired test ships and passes; (5) README § "Rate-limit detector fixture workflow" documents the contract; (6) the substrate experiment passes all 3 substrate lints + ci-experiment-runner gate.
  - **Anchor**: `<bosun-repo>/orchestrator/server/src/service/backendHealthService.ts:11-30` (the 10-pattern set with surfacing-pipeline comments); `<bosun-repo>/orchestrator/server/src/__tests__/fixtures/rate-limit-bodies.json` (the fixture-driven regression-test pattern); `<bosun-repo>/AGENTS.md:138-153` (the operator-facing fixture-workflow doc); Nygard, M., *Release It! Design and Deploy Production-Ready Software*, Pragmatic Bookshelf, 2007, ISBN 978-1680502398, Ch. 17 (Stability Antipatterns — Cascading Failure is the pattern this gate guards against); rule #1 (don't reinvent — bosun's pattern set is battle-tested across 3500+ commits and named pipeline traces); rule #2 (Strategy seam — `RATE_LIMIT_PATTERNS` is the shared substrate, classifiers are the strategies); rule #7 (chaos-coverage — every new pattern carries its surfacing-body fixture so a regression that loses pattern coverage trips the gate); rule #9 (HDD — measurement command above tests fixture pass count + shape lint).
  - **Risk**: low. Pattern additions are additive (existing detection stays true); fixture-driven test pins the contract so a future "tighten the pattern set" change can't silently regress.
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-12 bosun-extraction analysis (see `/tmp/bosun-to-minsky-extraction-analysis.md` § Extraction #1 — verified by spot-check of `backendHealthService.ts:11-30` and the `RATE_LIMIT_PATTERNS` const).

- [ ] `cross-repo-runner-per-repo-project-memory` — cross-repo-runner gains a per-host-repo SQLite learnings store so the runner stops repeating the same mistake against the same host across iterations
  - **ID**: cross-repo-runner-per-repo-project-memory
  - **Tags**: p2, cross-repo-runner, learning, bosun-extraction, memory
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Touches**: `novel/cross-repo-runner/src/project-memory.ts` (new), `novel/cross-repo-runner/src/project-memory.test.ts` (new), `novel/cross-repo-runner/src/host-loop.ts` (wire memory into brief assembly), `novel/cross-repo-runner/README.md`, `experiments/cross-repo-per-repo-memory-2026-05-12.yaml` (new — rule-#9 substrate)
  - **Hypothesis**: cross-repo-runner today is stateless across iterations — when it picks the same task block twice in a row (because the first iteration didn't fully ship), or when it picks two semantically-similar tasks against the same host, it cannot consult what it already learned. Bosun's `<bosun-repo>/orchestrator/server/src/service/projectMemory.ts` (1085 LOC) ships a per-repo SQLite store keyed on `{ repoSlug, scope }` with learning entries that carry `{ id, scope, lesson, createdAt, source }`. Pre-registration: post-fix, **(a)** `cross-repo-runner` opens `.minsky/project-memory.db` (per-host, gitignored, SQLite via `better-sqlite3` — the same dep bosun uses; rule #1) at the start of every `runLive` invocation; **(b)** when an iteration ships a PR, the runner extracts up to 3 lesson-shaped lines from the PR-body Hypothesis-self-grade `Lesson:` field and writes them to the host's memory keyed by `{ host: <host-slug>, task: <task-id> }`; **(c)** when the runner picks a task next time, `assembleBrief` queries the memory for lessons matching the current task ID (exact match) plus the current host's recent (≤30d) lessons and prepends them to the brief; **(d)** the integration test (`novel/cross-repo-runner/test/integration/host-memory.test.ts`) seeds a fake `.minsky/project-memory.db` with 2 lessons, runs `assembleBrief`, asserts the rendered brief contains both lesson texts; **(e)** the rolling-7d count of "duplicate PR opened against the same host for the same TASKS.md task-id" → 0 (currently rare, but non-zero; observed during the 4-PR cross-repo-runner v0/v1 ship of cross-repo-runner-v1-live-spawn). If (e) is already 0 today (i.e. duplicate-prevention is not the value), the memory's value is the *quality* improvement on the FIRST iteration of a related task — pivot in that case to a `lesson-utility` rubric where a sample of 5 lessons gets rated by the operator after a month; if ≤ 2/5 are rated "useful", retire the memory and switch to a simpler per-host CLAUDE.md append.
  - **Details**: (a) New file `novel/cross-repo-runner/src/project-memory.ts` exporting `ProjectMemory { open(dbPath: string): Promise<ProjectMemory>; remember(args: { host, task, lesson, source }): Promise<void>; recall(args: { host, task?, sinceMs? }): Promise<ProjectMemoryEntry[]>; close(): Promise<void> }`. Schema: `CREATE TABLE memory (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, host TEXT NOT NULL, task TEXT, lesson TEXT NOT NULL, source TEXT NOT NULL, created_at INTEGER NOT NULL); CREATE INDEX idx_memory_host_task ON memory(host, task); CREATE INDEX idx_memory_created ON memory(created_at DESC);`. (b) Lesson extraction — pure helper `extractLessonsFromPrBody(body: string): string[]` greps the `## Hypothesis self-grade` block for the `- **Lesson**:` line; returns at most 3 lessons after splitting on `";"` or `"."` (semicolon-space or period-space) and dropping short fragments (< 20 chars). (c) `host-loop.ts::runLive` opens the memory at start of iteration; after a PR is created, calls `memory.remember({ host, task, lesson, source: 'pr-body' })` for each extracted lesson; before assembling the next brief, calls `memory.recall({ host, task })` and prepends each lesson under a `## Lessons from prior iterations` brief section. (d) The DB lives at `.minsky/project-memory.db` (per-host, gitignored); rule-#13 trust boundary — lessons are operator-readable but not sent to the agent verbatim if they contain a redacted-by-default token (`extractLessonsFromPrBody` runs them through `scrubSecrets` from the existing scan-secrets substrate).
  - **Files**: `novel/cross-repo-runner/src/project-memory.ts` (new), `novel/cross-repo-runner/src/project-memory.test.ts` (new, paired tests), `novel/cross-repo-runner/src/lesson-extractor.ts` (new — pure helper), `novel/cross-repo-runner/src/lesson-extractor.test.ts` (new), `novel/cross-repo-runner/src/host-loop.ts` (wire memory into iteration lifecycle), `novel/cross-repo-runner/test/integration/host-memory.test.ts` (new — full integration), `novel/cross-repo-runner/README.md` (document the memory location + schema + privacy contract), `experiments/cross-repo-per-repo-memory-2026-05-12.yaml`.
  - **Verification**: `pnpm vitest run novel/cross-repo-runner` passes (≥ 4 new paired tests). Integration test seeds a fake DB with 2 lessons, runs `runLive` against a fixture host, asserts the rendered brief contains both lesson texts AND that a third lesson is written back to the DB after the iteration ships. `node -e "const db = require('better-sqlite3')('.minsky/project-memory.db'); console.log(db.prepare('SELECT COUNT(*) AS n FROM memory').get())"` returns `{ n: <n>0 }` after one live `cross-repo-runner --loop` iteration against a host.
  - **Measurement**: `pnpm vitest run novel/cross-repo-runner --reporter=json 2>/dev/null | jq -e '.numPassedTests >= 4' && pnpm vitest run novel/cross-repo-runner/test/integration/host-memory.test.ts --reporter=json 2>/dev/null | jq -e '.numPassedTests >= 1' && grep -q 'project-memory.db' .gitignore && [ -f .minsky/project-memory.db ] && node -e 'const db=require("better-sqlite3")(".minsky/project-memory.db"); const n=db.prepare("SELECT COUNT(*) AS n FROM memory").get().n; process.exit(n>0?0:1)'`
  - **Pivot**: see Hypothesis (e). If lessons don't improve iteration quality after 5 operator-rated lessons in 30 days, retire the SQLite store and switch to a simpler per-host `CLAUDE.md` append (the lesson goes into the host's CLAUDE.md, which the next iteration's brief reads via the existing additional-dirs context loader — no DB layer). Decision boundary: ≤ 2/5 operator-rated lessons judged "useful" within 30 days.
  - **Acceptance**: (1) `ProjectMemory` adapter ships with paired tests (open/remember/recall/close + schema migration + chaos row "DB corrupt → graceful-degrade to empty recall"); (2) `extractLessonsFromPrBody` ships with paired tests covering the canonical Hypothesis-self-grade block + 3 edge cases (missing block, multi-line lesson, secrets-scrubbed); (3) `host-loop.ts` wires memory into the iteration lifecycle without changing existing tests; (4) integration test ships and passes; (5) `.minsky/project-memory.db` is in `.gitignore`; (6) README documents the schema + privacy contract; (7) experiment substrate passes all 3 substrate lints + ci-experiment-runner gate.
  - **Anchor**: `<bosun-repo>/orchestrator/server/src/service/projectMemory.ts:1-1085` (the SQLite-backed per-repo memory; minsky takes the schema + access pattern, not the full bosun shape since bosun's memory is keyed by `{ projectId, scope }` which is bosun-specific); `<bosun-repo>/orchestrator/server/src/service/prFeedbackService.ts:32-50` (the `filterSubstantiveComments` + `MIN_COMMENT_LENGTH = 20` pattern for filtering noise from lesson-shaped text); Sutton, R.S. & Barto, A.G., *Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction*, 2nd edition, MIT Press, 2018, ISBN 978-0262039246, Ch. 1 (memory as the value function — lessons are the agent's value-function-of-prior-experience); rule #1 (don't reinvent — bosun's schema and access pattern have a year of production observation); rule #2 (`ProjectMemory` interface + `memory.sqlite.ts` impl); rule #13 (privacy — scrubSecrets gates lesson writes); rule #9 (HDD substrate above).
  - **Risk**: medium. Lesson extraction is the variable part — if `extractLessonsFromPrBody` is too greedy (extracts marketing prose instead of actionable lessons), the brief gets noisier. Mitigation: paired tests cover the canonical shapes; `MIN_COMMENT_LENGTH = 20` filters short fragments; operator can manually expire lessons by editing the DB or deleting `.minsky/project-memory.db` (lossless).
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-12 bosun-extraction analysis (`/tmp/bosun-to-minsky-extraction-analysis.md` § Extraction #4 + #12) — bosun has a 1085-LOC `projectMemory.ts` + 116-LOC `prFeedbackService.ts` covering this gap; minsky's cross-repo-runner has no per-host learning today.

- [ ] `daemon-cross-vendor-reviewer-bias-prevention` — CTO-audit reviewer (and any future review-shaped persona) runs against a DIFFERENT model/provider than the worker that produced the output, preventing self-validation bias
  - **ID**: daemon-cross-vendor-reviewer-bias-prevention
  - **Tags**: p2, daemon, cto-audit, model-routing, bias, bosun-extraction
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Touches**: `novel/tick-loop/src/post-task-cto-audit.ts`, `novel/tick-loop/src/llm-provider-selector.ts`, `novel/tick-loop/src/strategic-model-router.ts`, `experiments/cross-vendor-reviewer-bias-2026-05-12.yaml` (new — rule-#9 substrate)
  - **Hypothesis**: today the `runPostTaskCtoAudit` flow in `novel/tick-loop/src/post-task-cto-audit.ts` runs against whatever model `decideProvider` returns — which, in the budget-paused branch, is the same model (local-Qwen via aider) that produced the original iteration. A reviewer agent that uses the SAME underlying weights as the developer agent will systematically miss the developer's own blind spots (e.g. Qwen consistently writes the same off-by-one bug AND fails to flag it in review). Bosun's `<bosun-repo>/orchestrator/server/src/service/modelRouter.ts:148-204` documents this exact concern (the `getBackendTypeForPersona` function returns the configured `alternativeBackendType` for the reviewer persona to prevent self-validation bias — quote from the source: *"For the reviewer persona, returns the configured alternative backend type so it runs through a different LLM than the one used by Developer — preventing self-validation bias."*). Pre-registration: post-fix, **(a)** `runPostTaskCtoAudit` takes a `reviewer-model-override` option that, when set, forces the audit invocation to run against a model NOT used by the iteration being audited; **(b)** `decideProvider` gains an optional `excludeProvider` arg and returns the highest-quality alternative that excludes the named provider; **(c)** the default config sets `cto-audit.reviewer = "claude-opus"` when the iteration ran on `local-qwen`, and `cto-audit.reviewer = "local-qwen"` when the iteration ran on `claude-opus` — bidirectional alternative; **(d)** the integration test injects a fake iteration that ran on `local-qwen`, runs the audit, asserts the audit invocation went through the `claude-print` path (or `MINSKY_AUDIT_PROVIDER` override); **(e)** rolling-30d CTO-audit findings should diverge from worker-output language (cosine similarity ≤ 0.6 on bag-of-tokens) — if the audit and the iteration produce textually-similar output, the reviewer is parroting the worker, not auditing. If (e) doesn't separate (similarity stays > 0.6), the bias-prevention claim is wrong for our specific Opus/Qwen pair — pivot to a different alternative pairing (Opus reviewer with Sonnet worker, or two distinct local models).
  - **Details**: (a) Extend `runPostTaskCtoAudit(opts)` with `opts.reviewerProvider?: ProviderId`; when set, override `decideProvider` for the audit call only. (b) `decideProvider` gets a new optional `excludeProvider?: ProviderId` — when set, returns the *next-best* provider that isn't the excluded one (e.g. if budget-paused excludes claude, return `local-qwen`; if `local-qwen` is excluded and claude is budget-healthy, return `claude-print`). (c) New config block `cto-audit.reviewer-pairing: { worker-provider → reviewer-provider }` in `Config` shape; default `{ "local-qwen": "claude-opus", "claude-opus": "local-qwen", "claude-sonnet": "claude-opus" }`. (d) The fast-path: when `MINSKY_AUDIT_PROVIDER` env var is set, it short-circuits all the pairing logic and forces that provider. (e) Telemetry: add `tick-loop.cto-audit.reviewer-provider` + `tick-loop.cto-audit.worker-provider` span attributes so the cosine-similarity script can read them.
  - **Files**: `novel/tick-loop/src/post-task-cto-audit.ts` (extend opts + wire reviewer pairing), `novel/tick-loop/src/llm-provider-selector.ts` (add `excludeProvider` arg + `pickAlternativeProvider` pure helper), `novel/tick-loop/src/strategic-model-router.ts` (config-driven pairing lookup), `novel/tick-loop/src/post-task-cto-audit.test.ts` (new paired tests for pairing logic), `scripts/cto-audit-bias-similarity.mjs` (new — compute cosine similarity over a sample), `docs/cto-audit.md` (document the bias-prevention rationale), `experiments/cross-vendor-reviewer-bias-2026-05-12.yaml`.
  - **Verification**: `pnpm vitest run novel/tick-loop/src/post-task-cto-audit.test.ts` passes (≥ 5 new pairing tests: same-provider-not-allowed, alternative-found, alternative-not-found-fallback-to-default, env-var-override, telemetry-attribute-emitted). `node scripts/cto-audit-bias-similarity.mjs --window=7d` exits 0 and reports `cosine_similarity ≤ 0.6` for the post-fix sample (pre-fix baseline recorded in the experiment substrate for comparison).
  - **Measurement**: `pnpm vitest run novel/tick-loop/src/post-task-cto-audit.test.ts --reporter=json 2>/dev/null | jq -e '.numPassedTests >= 5 and .numFailedTests == 0' && grep -qE '(excludeProvider|reviewer-pairing|MINSKY_AUDIT_PROVIDER)' novel/tick-loop/src/llm-provider-selector.ts && node scripts/cto-audit-bias-similarity.mjs --window=7d --json | jq -e '.cosine_similarity <= 0.6'`
  - **Pivot**: see Hypothesis (e). If Opus-reviewer-of-Qwen-worker doesn't separate (cosine > 0.6), Opus and Qwen are too aligned in our specific use case — try Opus reviewer of GPT-5 worker (different vendor + different family). If no vendor pairing separates, the bias-prevention thesis is wrong for code review and we retire the feature.
  - **Acceptance**: (1) `runPostTaskCtoAudit` accepts `reviewerProvider` override + reads `MINSKY_AUDIT_PROVIDER` env; (2) `decideProvider` accepts `excludeProvider` and returns alternative or falls back; (3) Config carries `cto-audit.reviewer-pairing` with the default 3 pairings; (4) ≥ 5 new paired tests cover the pairing logic + env override; (5) `cto-audit-bias-similarity.mjs` ships and exits 0 with ≤ 0.6 cosine; (6) `docs/cto-audit.md` documents the rationale + the literature anchor; (7) telemetry attributes ship and are queryable.
  - **Anchor**: `<bosun-repo>/orchestrator/server/src/service/modelRouter.ts:118-180` (the cross-vendor pairing logic and the JSDoc rationale verbatim); Sunstein, C.R., *#Republic: Divided Democracy in the Age of Social Media*, Princeton, 2017, ISBN 978-0691175515, Ch. 3 (group polarisation — same-vendor reviewers polarise the same biases as the worker); Surowiecki, J., *The Wisdom of Crowds*, Anchor, 2005, ISBN 978-0385721707, Ch. 2 (diversity-of-opinion as the load-bearing pre-condition for crowd accuracy); rule #1 (don't reinvent — bosun has the concept named and shaped); rule #2 (`pickAlternativeProvider` is a Strategy seam); rule #7 (chaos table — `MINSKY_AUDIT_PROVIDER=invalid-provider` → graceful-degrade to default pairing, not crash); rule #9 (HDD measurement above — cosine similarity is the falsifiable metric).
  - **Risk**: medium. Cosine similarity is a crude metric for "different conclusions" — a sophisticated reviewer could legitimately reach a similar conclusion via different reasoning. Mitigation: operator manually rates a sample of 5 audits post-rollout; if the rating disagrees with the cosine-similarity verdict, replace the metric with a rubric-based audit comparison.
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-12 bosun-extraction analysis — verified by reading `modelRouter.ts:148-204` which carries the cross-vendor logic + the explicit JSDoc rationale.

- [ ] `daemon-pr-feedback-extraction` — daemon extracts substantive PR review comments from merged daemon PRs and feeds them into the next iteration's brief, learning from human feedback
  - **ID**: daemon-pr-feedback-extraction
  - **Tags**: p2, daemon, learning, pr-feedback, bosun-extraction
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Touches**: `novel/tick-loop/src/pr-feedback-extractor.ts` (new), `novel/tick-loop/src/pr-feedback-extractor.test.ts` (new), `novel/tick-loop/src/daemon.ts` (wire post-merge extraction), `experiments/pr-feedback-extraction-2026-05-12.yaml` (new — rule-#9 substrate)
  - **Hypothesis**: today the daemon does not consult human review comments on its own merged PRs. When an operator leaves a review comment like "this would be cleaner with a switch statement" on PR #N, the next iteration on a similar task makes the same mistake. Bosun's `<bosun-repo>/orchestrator/server/src/service/prFeedbackService.ts` (116 LOC total) ships `filterSubstantiveComments` (filters noise via 8 regex patterns: `^lgtm$`, `^looks good`, `^\+1$`, `:thumbsup:`, `^approved$`, `^nice work`, `^ship it`, `^nit:`) plus `MIN_COMMENT_LENGTH = 20` and `extractLearningsFromComments` (truncates at 300 chars, normalises whitespace, prefixes with `[<path>] PR feedback (<user>):`). Pre-registration: post-fix, **(a)** after a daemon PR merges, the daemon calls `gh pr view <n> --json reviews,comments,reviewThreads --jq ...` to fetch all review comments; **(b)** `filterSubstantiveComments` drops the 8 noise patterns + comments < 20 chars; **(c)** the remaining substantive lessons go into the next iteration's brief under a `## Lessons from recent reviews on this repo` section (capped at 5 most-recent, 300 chars each); **(d)** the integration test ships 4 review comments (2 noise + 2 substantive), asserts only the substantive 2 reach the brief; **(e)** rolling-30d count of repeated review comments (operator opens a comment that semantically matches one from a prior PR on the same daemon iteration target) drops by ≥ 50 % compared to the 30d pre-fix baseline. If (e) doesn't move (count stays flat), the issue is not that the lessons aren't reaching the brief — it's that the daemon's persona doesn't act on lessons in the brief. Pivot in that case: retire the brief-prepend approach and switch to a stricter pre-PR substrate lint that auto-rejects diffs matching a flagged anti-pattern from prior reviews.
  - **Details**: (a) New file `novel/tick-loop/src/pr-feedback-extractor.ts` — pure `extractFeedback(reviewBodies: PrReviewComment[]): string[]` using bosun's noise-pattern set + minsky's existing `scrubSecrets` for trust-boundary safety. (b) New file `novel/tick-loop/src/pr-feedback-fetcher.ts` — I/O-edge `fetchPrReviewComments(prNumber, gh = realGh): Promise<PrReviewComment[]>` that wraps `gh pr view --json` with a Strategy seam for testing. (c) Wire into `daemon.ts` post-merge — when an iteration detects a `MERGED` state on a daemon-authored PR, fetch + extract + store into the project-memory DB (composes with `cross-repo-runner-per-repo-project-memory` above — same DB, schema-compatible). (d) Brief assembly reads the last ≤ 5 substantive lessons from project-memory and prepends them under `## Lessons from recent reviews on this repo`. (e) The 8 noise regex patterns are copied byte-for-byte from bosun's `prFeedbackService.ts:19-28` (rule #1 — battle-tested set).
  - **Files**: `novel/tick-loop/src/pr-feedback-extractor.ts` (new — pure helpers; copy bosun's noise patterns), `novel/tick-loop/src/pr-feedback-extractor.test.ts` (new — paired tests covering 8 noise rows + 4 substantive rows + the truncate-and-prefix shape), `novel/tick-loop/src/pr-feedback-fetcher.ts` (new — I/O edge with `ghFn` seam), `novel/tick-loop/src/daemon.ts` (post-merge hook), `experiments/pr-feedback-extraction-2026-05-12.yaml` (new).
  - **Verification**: `pnpm vitest run novel/tick-loop/src/pr-feedback-extractor.test.ts` passes (≥ 12 new paired tests). Integration test injects 4 review comments via the `ghFn` seam, asserts only 2 substantive comments reach the rendered brief.
  - **Measurement**: `pnpm vitest run novel/tick-loop/src/pr-feedback-extractor.test.ts --reporter=json 2>/dev/null | jq -e '.numPassedTests >= 12 and .numFailedTests == 0' && grep -cE 'lgtm|looks good|\\\\+1|thumbsup|approved|nice work|ship it|^nit:' novel/tick-loop/src/pr-feedback-extractor.ts | grep -qE '^[8-9]$|^[1-9][0-9]+$'`
  - **Pivot**: see Hypothesis (e). If lessons don't reduce repeat review comments, retire brief-prepend and switch to an anti-pattern lint (catch the offending pattern at the diff stage, not the prompt stage).
  - **Acceptance**: (1) `extractFeedback` ships with the 8 bosun noise patterns copied verbatim + MIN_COMMENT_LENGTH = 20; (2) paired tests cover every noise pattern + ≥ 4 substantive rows; (3) `fetchPrReviewComments` ships with the `ghFn` injectable seam; (4) daemon's post-merge hook wires extraction into project-memory; (5) brief assembly prepends ≤ 5 recent lessons; (6) experiment substrate passes the 3 substrate lints + ci-experiment-runner gate.
  - **Anchor**: `<bosun-repo>/orchestrator/server/src/service/prFeedbackService.ts:19-28` (the 8 noise patterns); `<bosun-repo>/orchestrator/server/src/service/prFeedbackService.ts:30` (`MIN_COMMENT_LENGTH = 20`); `<bosun-repo>/orchestrator/server/src/service/prFeedbackService.ts:32-49` (filter-and-format pattern); Argyris, C., *Teaching Smart People How to Learn*, Harvard Business Review, May–June 1991 (the gap between espoused theory and theory-in-use — agents espouse "I will learn from feedback" but their iteration loop has no feedback ingress without this substrate); rule #1 (don't reinvent — bosun's noise set is battle-tested); rule #2 (`extractFeedback` is a pure function; `fetchPrReviewComments` is the I/O edge with a `ghFn` seam — both behind a single `PrFeedbackAdapter` interface).
  - **Risk**: low-medium. Risk is in the brief becoming noisy if 5 lessons of 300 chars each gets prepended every iteration. Mitigation: cap at 5 most-recent (last 30d); operator can lower the cap via `MINSKY_PR_FEEDBACK_CAP=2`.
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-12 bosun-extraction analysis. Verified file:line by reading `prFeedbackService.ts:1-60` directly (8 noise patterns + MIN_COMMENT_LENGTH constant confirmed).

- [ ] `dependabot-bumps-dep-regression-triage` — 8+ open dependabot PRs (#300–#307) failing CI on dep-upgrade regressions; need triage policy
  - **ID**: dependabot-bumps-dep-regression-triage
  - **Tags**: p2, dependencies, ergonomics, dependabot
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Estimate**: 1d
  - **Hypothesis**: 9h window 2026-05-07: 8 dependabot PRs (`actions/setup-python` 5→6, `actions/upload-artifact` 4→7, `markdownlint-cli2` 0.15→0.22, `hono` 4.12.16→4.12.18, OTEL 0.57.2→0.217.0 ×3, etc.) all failing CI. Most are major bumps with breaking-change semantics; some are patch bumps that should pass. Pre-registration: post-fix, dependabot PRs split by semver: patch+minor auto-merge if CI green; major bumps file a `dep-upgrade-<package>` task with the breaking-change diff and require operator approval. Rolling-7d failing-dependabot-PR count → ≤2.
  - **Details**: `.github/dependabot.yml` configures `versioning-strategy: increase` for patch + minor; major bumps go to a separate group with `labels: [needs-operator]`. Existing 8 PRs: triage manually — close the major OTEL bumps as out-of-scope, retry the patch bumps after main updates.
  - **Files**: `.github/dependabot.yml`, `docs/dependency-policy.md`.
  - **Verification**: paired test: synthetic patch-bump PR auto-merges; major-bump PR opens with `needs-operator` label.
  - **Measurement**: count of `closed-as-not-planned` dependabot PRs over 30d ≤ 1/month average.
  - **Pivot**: if patch bumps still cause regressions (transitive deps), tighten to lockfile-only updates.
  - **Anchor**: 9h window 2026-05-07; Dependabot best practices.
  - **Risk**: low.
  - **Surfaced-by**: 9h monitoring window — 8 stuck dependabot PRs.

- [ ] `spawn-n-workers-with-watchdog-monitoring` — substrate for safely running N (4-10) parallel workers with a monitor that auto-unblocks stuck children + files incident tasks
  - **ID**: spawn-n-workers-with-watchdog-monitoring
  - **Tags**: p2, daemon, supervisor, parallel, watchdog, follow-up
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Estimate**: 1w
  - **Hypothesis**: Operator question 2026-05-07: "should and can we spawn 10 workers, all monitored by this window that unblocks them & files tasks so that next time minsky unblocks itself". The 9h window demonstrated I was that monitor manually — killed the worker-1 hang, closed duplicate PRs, fixed body-fail PRs, rebased stuck PRs. Pre-registration: post-fix, a monitor process spawned by `pnpm minsky --spawn-additional-workers=N --watchdog` performs all 4 of those interventions automatically and emits incident spans + TASKS.md filings for each. Rolling-7d operator-manual-intervention count → 0 across N=4 workers; → ≤1/day across N=10.
  - **Details**: (a) New flag `--watchdog` on the root `pnpm minsky` invocation. (b) Watchdog process (separate from worker daemons): runs every 60s, queries `pgrep --parent <worker-pid>` for stuck children (CPU<5% for >15min) → SIGKILLs them. (c) Queries `gh pr list --author "@minsky-bot"` for: PRs CONFLICTING >2h → invokes the rebase watchdog (#daemon-stuck-pr-rebase-watchdog); PRs failing only on body-only checks → patches body. (d) For each intervention, files a TASKS.md entry under `## P2` with `**Auto-filed-by**: spawn-n-workers-with-watchdog-monitoring` so the daemon learns the unblock pattern over time. (e) Composes with `daemon-claude-print-hang-watchdog` (P0), `daemon-stuck-pr-rebase-watchdog` (P0), `daemon-task-rotation-on-completion` (P0), `daemon-duplicate-work-detection` (P0) — those four shipped is the precondition for safe N≥4.
  - **Files**: `novel/tick-loop/bin/watchdog.mjs` (new bin), `novel/tick-loop/src/watchdog.ts` (pure decisions for kill / rebase / body-patch), `novel/tick-loop/src/watchdog.test.ts` (paired tests for each intervention), `package.json` `bin: { minsky-watchdog: ... }`.
  - **Verification**: paired tests for each intervention class. Integration test: spawn 2 fake workers, simulate a hang, watchdog kills + files task within 60s.
  - **Measurement**: `count(operator-manual-interventions)` per worker per day → see hypothesis thresholds. Concrete log probe: `grep -c 'minsky: operator manually killed' .minsky/workers/*.log` over 7d → 0 (ideal); 1-3 is acceptable while substrate matures.
  - **Pivot**: if the watchdog itself crashes (silence-is-failure on the watchdog), the operator's manual intervention rate stays high — pivot to `launchd KeepAlive` + a separate `minsky-watchdog` plist so the watchdog has its own respawn.
  - **Anchor**: operator 2026-05-07 — "monitor + unblock + file tasks so next time minsky unblocks itself"; `daemon-parallel-worktree-launch` parent task; rule #2 (Strategy seam — pure decisions); rule #6 (let-it-crash — watchdog kills hung children); rule #9 (pre-registered HDD).
  - **Risk**: medium. The watchdog has destructive power (SIGKILL, force-push, body-patch). Mitigations: cap-3 retries before escalating to `Blocked: needs-operator`; all interventions logged + incident-task-filed; opt-out via `MINSKY_WATCHDOG_DRY_RUN=1` for first deploy.
  - **Surfaced-by**: operator 2026-05-07 directive ("file tasks for all findings and bugs… should we spawn 10 workers"); 9h monitoring window 2026-05-06/07 demonstrated all 4 intervention classes manually.
  - **Composes-into**: parent task `claude-orchestrator-local-worker-fanout` (P0, line 446) — this block provides the watchdog substrate (kill stuck child, rebase stuck PR, body-patch). The parent adds the **cross-layer** chaos slice (orchestrator-death-recovery and worker-hang-detection-from-orchestrator-side via `chaos-orchestrator-kill.mjs` and `chaos-worker-hang.mjs`).

- [ ] `brief-trim-coordinator` — workers compete on the same brief sections; need a coordinator so one PR/iter trims and others abstain
  - **ID**: brief-trim-coordinator
  - **Tags**: p2, daemon, optimization, parallel-launch
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Estimate**: 0.5d
  - **Hypothesis**: 9h window 2026-05-07 with 2 workers: both shipped brief-trim commits on overlapping sections → multiple rebases needed and ~1/3 of optimization commits got conflicts. With N=10 workers this becomes severe. Pre-registration: post-fix, `acquireTaskClaim`-style lock on `buildDaemonBrief` itself — only the worker holding the brief-trim claim may touch `daemon.ts` brief constants per iteration; others fall back to `optimization: none-this-iteration`.
  - **Details**: extend `acquireTaskClaim` with a per-resource lock (`brief-trim` resource) with TTL 10 min. The optimization-discipline gate in `buildDaemonBrief` reads this and either greenlights or steers to alternative optimization classes (round-trip elimination, log-line dedup) when locked.
  - **Files**: `novel/tick-loop/src/worker-claim.ts` (extend with resource locks), `novel/tick-loop/src/daemon.ts` (brief gate reads the lock).
  - **Verification**: paired tests for resource lock; brief contains coordinator hint when locked.
  - **Measurement**: post-deploy, brief-trim-commit conflicts in 7d → 0 across active workers.
  - **Pivot**: if resource lock causes optimization-gate noops too often, drop the lock and accept the conflict cost.
  - **Anchor**: 9h window 2026-05-07 — observed brief-trim conflicts; `daemon-parallel-worktree-launch` parent task.
  - **Risk**: low.
  - **Surfaced-by**: 9h monitoring window 2026-05-07.

See `vision.md` § "Pattern conformance index" rows 11 and 35. -->

The bidirectional / claim-propagation half is deferred to v1+ as `omc-tasksmd-bridge-v1-watcher` (P3 below) pending a CRDT story for OMC's optimistic-concurrency `version` field. -->

- [ ] `first-integration-test` — First user-story integration test passes (001) — tracker
  - **ID**: first-integration-test
  - **Tags**: testing, validation, tracker
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Estimate**: 6h (decomposed across 3 sub-tasks)
  - **Blocked by**: first-integration-test-nightly-self-hosted
  - **Hypothesis**: A 60-minute compressed simulation reproduces the failure modes that matter for an 8h overnight run with ≥80 % coverage of the failure-mode rows declared in the user-story file, while keeping CI runtime under 10 minutes. Per the documented Pivot below (reframe as 10-min smoke + nightly self-hosted), the work is decomposed into three sub-tasks; this entry is the tracker that closes when all three ship.
  - **Details**: Decomposed on 2026-05-04 per the parent task's documented Pivot. Sub-task 1 (`first-integration-test-coverage-manifest`) ships the coverage manifest test. Sub-task 2 (`first-integration-test-mock-tick-loop`) builds a mock daemon for the in-process 10-min smoke. Sub-task 3 (`first-integration-test-nightly-self-hosted`) wires the nightly self-hosted-runner workflow for the 7 OS-level chaos rows. This block remains as the coordination contract — Hypothesis / Success / Pivot / Measurement / Anchor are the parent-level invariants; the sub-tasks each carry their own rule-#9 fields scoped to their slice.
  - **Verification**: `npm test user-stories/001-loop-runs-overnight.test.ts` passes locally and on CI; OTEL collector receives ≥1 span per task type; CI workflow shows green. (Each sub-task's own Verification cell is the load-bearing one; this tracker closes when all three pass.)
  - **Measurement**: `pnpm vitest run user-stories/001-coverage-manifest.test.ts` exits 0 (sub-task 1) AND `pnpm vitest run novel/tick-loop` exits 0 (sub-task 2) AND `gh run list --workflow nightly-overnight-sim.yml --status success --limit 1 --json conclusion --jq '.[0].conclusion'` returns `success` (sub-task 3).
  - **Pivot**: if the 60-min sim's CI runtime exceeds 10 min OR misses >2 of the user-story's failure modes → reframe as a pair (10-min smoke in CI + nightly self-hosted run that does the full 60-min). **Pivot fired 2026-05-04** — decomposition into 3 sub-tasks landed; this tracker now coordinates the sub-tasks. If even the sub-task path fails (sub-task 2's smoke can't fit in 10 min OR sub-task 3's self-hosted runner is unreachable), the story's overnight assumption is wrong and the story needs splitting.
  - **Acceptance**: All three sub-tasks closed; coverage manifest's ≥80 % ratio holds; nightly self-hosted run lands at least once `success`.
  - **Anchor**: Basiri et al., "Principles of Chaos Engineering", *IEEE Software* 2016 (steady-state hypothesis); Beck, *Extreme Programming Explained*, 1999 (CI keeps the build fast).
  - **Risk**: 60min compressed sim may miss real overnight failure modes (memory leaks, log rotation, OS sleep). Documented gap; sub-task 3's nightly self-hosted run is the mitigation.

- [ ] `first-integration-test-nightly-self-hosted` — Nightly overnight-sim workflow on a self-hosted runner
  - **ID**: first-integration-test-nightly-self-hosted
  - **Parent**: first-integration-test
  - **Tags**: testing, infra, ci, dormant-until-self-hosted-runner
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Estimate**: 4h (when self-hosted runner is available)
  - **Hypothesis**: A nightly workflow (`.github/workflows/nightly-overnight-sim.yml`) running the full 60-min sim on a self-hosted runner — using the mock daemon from sub-task 2 — covers the 7 OS-level chaos rows (2, 5, 6, 7, 8, 11, 12) of user-story 001's failure-mode table that GH-hosted runners cannot exercise (libfaketime, iptables, tc qdisc, dd, pmset), without burning CI minutes on the hot per-PR path.
  - **Details**: Triggers when sub-task 2's mock-tick-loop ships AND a self-hosted runner is available (mirrors the precedent of `supervisor-integration-self-hosted-runner` and `lighthouse-self-hosted-runner-pivot`). Workflow uses `runs-on: [self-hosted, linux]`, runs nightly at low-stakes UTC hours, and exercises one randomly-chosen OS-level chaos row per night per `user-stories/001-loop-runs-overnight.md`'s weekly-fault-injection prose. Failures escalate to a Watch-level notification per the user-story's chaos-verification section.
  - **Files**: `.github/workflows/nightly-overnight-sim.yml`, `docs/self-hosted-runner.md` (shared with `supervisor-integration-self-hosted-runner` if it has fired)
  - **Verification**: at least one nightly run lands `success`; the run touches at least one of rows 2, 5, 6, 7, 8, 11, 12 (the OS-fault rows in the manifest).
  - **Measurement**: `gh run list --workflow nightly-overnight-sim.yml --branch main --status success --limit 5 --json conclusion --jq '[.[] | select(.conclusion=="success")] | length >= 1'` exits 0 with `true`.
  - **Pivot**: if self-hosted-runner maintenance burden exceeds the empirical signal value (e.g., the runner needs >1 manual intervention per quarter) OR if no self-hosted runner becomes available within 90 days of sub-task 2 shipping, retire this dormant task and document the OS-level chaos rows as a permanent declared deviation in `user-stories/001-loop-runs-overnight.md`.
  - **Acceptance**: this task fires only after sub-task 2 ships AND a self-hosted runner is available; otherwise it remains a dormant scout entry per the parent `first-integration-test` task's documented Pivot.
  - **Anchor**: Basiri et al., "Principles of Chaos Engineering", *IEEE Software* 2016 (the documented Pivot from `first-integration-test`'s rule-#9 block — coverage of OS-level rows belongs in a self-hosted runner with real OS primitives); Forsgren, Humble, Kim, *Accelerate*, IT Revolution Press, 2018 (DORA test reliability — a CI gate that doesn't run reliably teaches the team to ignore failure; the nightly cadence is the reliability bound).
  - **Risk**: self-hosted runners introduce supply-chain risk (a compromised runner can leak secrets). Mitigation: scope the runner to public-repo / non-secret jobs only; share infrastructure with `supervisor-integration-self-hosted-runner` if both fire (cost amortisation); standard GH guidance.

<!-- Replace-or-relocate research backfill (operator directive 2026-05-05) — vision.md rule #1 now requires this for every feature. Each block below evaluates one shipped novel/* module against (a) does an existing tool replace it? and (b) should it be relocated to agentbrew, dotfiles, or tasks.md? Output: <module>/REPLACE_OR_RELOCATE.md with a single Decision: line. Tasks intentionally tight — these are research, not engineering. -->

- [ ] `research-replace-or-relocate-tick-loop` — replace-or-relocate research for `novel/tick-loop`
  - **ID**: research-replace-or-relocate-tick-loop
  - **Tags**: p2, research, replace-or-relocate
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Estimate**: 0.5d
  - **Hypothesis**: Per vision.md rule #1's per-feature mandate (2026-05-05), the supervisor daemon (`tick-loop`: pickTask + runCtoAudit + runChangelog + runSnapshot + runMetricsRender + budget-guard wiring) should be evaluated for: (a) replacement by an existing tool — candidates: temporal.io, n8n, Camunda for the workflow engine; bullmq, agenda for the periodic-task scheduler; (b) relocation to `agentbrew` (most likely host since it's about Claude Code automation). The supervisor concept is highly Minsky-specific but the periodic-task scheduling + adapter pattern around `claude --print` could plausibly live elsewhere. Pre-registration: research lands a Decision: line within 14 days; if "replace", a swap PR is filed.
  - **Files**: `novel/tick-loop/REPLACE_OR_RELOCATE.md` (research note + Decision: line).
  - **Verification**: `[ -f novel/tick-loop/REPLACE_OR_RELOCATE.md ]` exits 0 AND `grep -c '^Decision: ' novel/tick-loop/REPLACE_OR_RELOCATE.md` returns 1.
  - **Measurement**: same as Verification.
  - **Pivot**: if no clean replacement exists, write the keep-as-is justification (1 paragraph max) and move on. Don't engineer an artificial extraction.
  - **Acceptance**: (1) `REPLACE_OR_RELOCATE.md` with explicit `Decision: keep | replace-by-<x> | relocate-to-<x>`; (2) follow-up PR filed within 14 days if Decision != keep.
  - **Anchor**: vision.md rule #1 per-feature mandate (2026-05-05); operator 2026-05-05 — "for every feature now".
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-05 operator backfill — vision update + research tasks for every feature.

  - **Anchor**: vision.md rule #1 per-feature mandate (2026-05-05).
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-05 operator backfill.

- [ ] `brief-instructs-exit-after-pr-open` — brief should tell devin to exit cleanly (`process.exit(0)`) immediately after `gh pr create` succeeds, instead of running to 15-min timeout

  - **ID**: brief-instructs-exit-after-pr-open
  - **Tags**: cross-repo-runner, spawn-plan, brief, cost, p2, surfaced-by-example-service-api-monitoring
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Detected-by**: devin-session-2026-05-16-monitoring
  - **Detected-at**: 2026-05-16T22:30Z
  - **Hypothesis**: The devin worker spawned by `ProcessSpawnStrategy` doesn't reliably exit when its iteration's work is complete (PR opened, tests passed). Observed: `lma-middleware-canonical-header-overhaul` spawn at 16:15Z opened PR #614 at 16:29Z (14 min into the iteration) then ran to the 15-min `MINSKY_LIVE_SPAWN_TIMEOUT_MS` SIGKILL — wasted 1 min of compute. `example-model-use-merge-for-partial-updates` opened PR #616 then continued for ~3 more minutes before SIGKILL — wasted 3 min. Adding a "Exit cleanly with exit code 0 immediately after `gh pr create` succeeds — do NOT wait for CI feedback (the next iteration handles CI-fix loops)" clause to the brief in `novel/cross-repo-runner/src/spawn-plan.ts` cuts the wall-clock cost of each iteration by ~10-30%.
  - **Success**: After updating the brief, sample 5 successive validated iterations. Wall-clock duration distribution shifts: today's median ≈ 12-15 min; target post-fix median ≤ 8 min (PR-open cadence). Verified by `cat .minsky/experiment-store/cross-repo/*.jsonl | jq '.notes' | grep -oE '[0-9]+ms' | sort -n | head -10 | awk '{sum+=$1} END {print sum/NR}'`.
  - **Pivot**: If devin exits before PR CI feedback is available (preventing immediate-react-to-red-CI), restore the previous "wait until something happens" behaviour AND reduce `MINSKY_LIVE_SPAWN_TIMEOUT_MS` default to 8 min — accepting that some long iterations get killed but cheap ones don't waste budget. (Trades worst-case for typical-case.)
  - **Measurement**: After brief update, `for f in .minsky/experiment-store/cross-repo/*.jsonl; do tail -1 $f | jq -r '.notes' | grep -oE '[0-9]+ms' | head -1; done | sort -n | awk '{print $1/1000}'` — median should drop from ~720s to ≤ 480s.
  - **Pivot escape hatch**: `MINSKY_BRIEF_WAIT_AFTER_PR_OPEN=true` env reverts to the legacy "run to timeout" behavior in case operators discover CI-fix loops need the wait.
  - **Anchor**: Ousterhout, *A Philosophy of Software Design*, 2nd ed., Ch. 12 §"Pull Complexity Downwards" — the cheap-iteration case shouldn't pay for the worst-case ceiling. Adapt the brief so exit-when-done is explicit; let timeout be the last-resort kill switch, not the typical exit path.
  - **Why**: At ~$1-2/min for headless agent compute (rough), an extra 1-3 min per iteration × 10-20 iterations / 8h = 10-60 wasted minutes per window. The hypothesis is testable from existing iter records (already have wall-clock numbers).
  - **Files**: `novel/cross-repo-runner/src/spawn-plan.ts` (the `renderSystemPromptOverlay` brief composer), `novel/cross-repo-runner/src/spawn-plan.test.ts` (assert the new clause + env-escape behaviour).

- [ ] `docs-operator-troubleshooting-prompt-too-long` — add a troubleshooting doc covering the `claude --print` prompt-too-long failure on heavy `~/.claude/` setups

  - **ID**: docs-operator-troubleshooting-prompt-too-long
  - **Tags**: documentation, operator-experience, claude-code, p2, surfaced-by-example-service-api-monitoring
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Detected-by**: devin-session-2026-05-16-monitoring
  - **Detected-at**: 2026-05-16T22:30Z
  - **Hypothesis**: On machines with a heavy `~/.claude/` ecosystem (many skills + plugins + agents + rules accumulated over months — e.g. 92MB plugin marketplaces, 1GB session history, 74KB+ CLAUDE.md), `claude --print` exits 1 with "Prompt is too long" before processing any user input — the auto-loaded system prompt alone exceeds the model context. The documented escape hatches (`--bare`, `--setting-sources project,local`, `--disable-slash-commands`) have caveats: `--bare` disables OAuth/keychain auth (requires `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`), and `--setting-sources` doesn't prune plugin/skill auto-loading. Today's MINSKY workaround is `MINSKY_WORKER=devin` (PR #4bb10f9 on `feat/devin-as-headless-worker`). Adding `docs/operator-troubleshooting.md` with the symptom → root cause → workarounds → verification path saves the next operator the ~30 min I spent diagnosing.
  - **Success**: `docs/operator-troubleshooting.md` exists with a section "`claude --print` fails with 'Prompt is too long'" containing: (a) symptom screenshot/text, (b) root cause explanation (auto-loaded context overflow), (c) workarounds (devin worker recommended path; `--bare` + API key alternative), (d) verification (`echo 'reply OK' | claude --print` should exit 0 with response "OK"). README.md "Troubleshooting" section links to this doc. `pnpm lint:md` (markdownlint) exits 0.
  - **Pivot**: If the doc adds confusion (operators try `--bare` without API key + report new "Not logged in" failures, OR they expect the doc to fix their setup automatically), trim the workarounds list to just the devin-worker recommendation and link upstream to anthropic-claude-code's docs for the auth detail. Doc is reference, not a tool.
  - **Measurement**: `test -f docs/operator-troubleshooting.md && grep -c "Prompt is too long" docs/operator-troubleshooting.md` ≥ 1 AND `grep -c "operator-troubleshooting" README.md` ≥ 1.
  - **Anchor**: Wickham, *R Packages*, 2nd ed., Ch. 10 §"Other markdown files" — README + troubleshooting are siblings; troubleshooting captures the "I hit this error, what now?" path that the README's happy-path doesn't cover. Diátaxis framework (<https://diataxis.fr/>) §"How-to guides" agrees.
  - **Why**: 2026-05-16 12:51-13:30Z (example-service-api session) — operator hit "Prompt is too long" on first minsky run. Investigation took ~30 min before discovering `--bare` won't work without `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`. A doc would have shortened this to 5 min.
  - **Files**: `docs/operator-troubleshooting.md` (new), `README.md` (one-line link in "Troubleshooting" or equivalent section).

- [ ] `host-loop-operator-branch-pin-with-auto-stash` — minsky-run should checkout MINSKY_OPERATOR_BRANCH (with auto-stash) before each picker invocation so the operator's curated task queue is always visible

  - **ID**: host-loop-operator-branch-pin-with-auto-stash
  - **Tags**: cross-repo-runner, host-loop, branch-strategy, p2, surfaced-by-example-service-api-monitoring
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Detected-by**: devin-session-2026-05-16-monitoring
  - **Detected-at**: 2026-05-16T22:30Z
  - **Hypothesis**: Devin's per-task spawn checks out `feat/<task-id>` (branched from `default_branch`/master) and leaves HEAD there at end of iteration. The picker (next tick) reads TASKS.md from THAT branch — which lacks any Rule #9-substrate reformats the operator committed only on their working branch. Result: picker finds 0 compliant tasks → seed-on-empty fires → CTO audit composes new tasks → minsky exits empty-queue → external watchdog respawns. Adding a `MINSKY_OPERATOR_BRANCH=feat/<branch>` env (or `--operator-branch <name>` CLI flag) that `git switch <name>` (with `git stash push -m "minsky auto-stash"` first to handle dirty WIP) BEFORE each picker call eliminates the orphaned-reformat-branch problem. Today an external watchdog at `/tmp/minsky-example-service-api-devin/watchdog.sh` does this — but it's a workaround layer that should be built in.
  - **Success**: After landing the flag, a synthetic test sets `MINSKY_OPERATOR_BRANCH=feat/test-operator`, spawns minsky-run in a fixture repo where `master` has 0 Hypothesis tasks but `feat/test-operator` has 3, runs 1 iteration: the picker selects from the 3 (not the 0). When the iteration completes and HEAD ends on `feat/<task-id>`, the NEXT picker tick `git switch feat/test-operator` succeeds (auto-stashing any worker WIP) and the picker again sees the 3 tasks. `pnpm test` exits 0.
