  - **Pivot**: If auto-stash captures content that confuses the next devin worker (the new HEAD has a stash-branch hint and devin tries to "fix" the conflict), switch to using a dedicated worktree at `.minsky/worktrees/operator-branch/` for picker-only reads — the worker still operates in the main checkout, but the picker reads TASKS.md from the pinned worktree. More complex but isolates picker from worker.
  - **Measurement**: `grep -c "MINSKY_OPERATOR_BRANCH" novel/cross-repo-runner/bin/minsky-run.mjs` ≥ 1 (env wiring landed) AND `pnpm exec vitest run novel/cross-repo-runner/src/host-loop.test.ts -t "operator branch" --reporter=verbose 2>&1 | grep -i "pass" | head -3` (≥ 1 passing test case).
  - **Anchor**: Cohn, *Pro Git*, 2nd ed. (2014), Ch. 7 §"Stashing and Cleaning" — auto-stash on branch switch is the canonical multi-task workspace pattern (`git switch --autostash` since 2.20). Convention is to pin the curated branch + auto-stash before transitions, not to fight branch state with an external supervisor.
  - **Why**: Observed on example-service-api host 2026-05-16 20:17Z onward: my Rule #9 reformat branch (`feat/minsky-task-rule9-fields-PROJ-720` with 8-12 compliant tasks) was inaccessible to the picker every time devin's per-task spawn left HEAD on `feat/lma-middleware-...` / `feat/example-model-use-merge-...` (with 0 reformats). External watchdog's HEAD-restore step (added 21:45Z) hit `git switch` failures 2x because of uncommitted WIP. Built-in pin + auto-stash collapses this to one config var.
  - **Files**: `novel/cross-repo-runner/bin/minsky-run.mjs` (CLI + env), `novel/cross-repo-runner/src/host-loop.ts` (pre-pick hook), `novel/cross-repo-runner/src/host-loop.test.ts` (tests), `docs/operator-branch-pin.md` (new operator-facing doc).

- [ ] `runner-scope-leak-soft-mode` — relax scope-leak verdict from "kills iteration" to "warns + continues" because devin's natural fix scope is broader than the task's **Files**: declaration

  - **ID**: runner-scope-leak-soft-mode
  - **Tags**: cross-repo-runner, runner, verdict-tuning, p2, surfaced-by-example-service-api-monitoring
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Detected-by**: devin-session-2026-05-16-monitoring
  - **Detected-at**: 2026-05-16T22:30Z
  - **Hypothesis**: `runLive` in `novel/cross-repo-runner/src/runner.ts:166-169` returns `verdict: scope-leak` when the worker writes files outside the task's `**Files**:` / `**Touches**:` declaration, which `runOneIteration` treats as a fatal verdict that halts the loop. Devin's natural fix-it style spans related files (helpers, callers, integration tests, docs) that the original task author didn't anticipate. On the `lma-middleware-canonical-header-overhaul` task (2026-05-16 21:32Z + 21:35Z), devin scope-leaked TWICE in a row — each time discarded — because the task's `**Files**:` listed only 2 files but the coherent fix touched 12 (matching what PR #614 had already shipped). Adding `MINSKY_SCOPE_LEAK_MODE=warn|hard` (default `warn`) — emit a structured WARN log with the offending paths but DON'T kill the iteration, just record the verdict as `validated-scope-warn` — lets the operator review post-hoc without losing the work.
  - **Success**: After landing the mode flag, a synthetic test with `MINSKY_SCOPE_LEAK_MODE=warn` and a worker that writes 1 in-scope + 2 out-of-scope files: iteration verdict is `validated-scope-warn`, the iteration record's `scopeLeakPaths` field contains the 2 out-of-scope paths, AND the runner emits a WARN log line that an operator dashboard could pick up. With `MINSKY_SCOPE_LEAK_MODE=hard` (legacy default override), behavior matches today's scope-leak → halt.
  - **Pivot**: If `warn` mode lets devin "wander" into unrelated files (sample 20 iterations post-change; if ≥3 show file modifications uncorrelated with the task's stated intent — operator's subjective judgment), revert to `hard` default and instead invest in a "scope autocomplete" linter that pre-expands `**Files**:` to include adjacent test/doc files when the task author writes only the implementation file.
  - **Measurement**: `cd $MINSKY_REPO && pnpm exec vitest run novel/cross-repo-runner/src/runner.test.ts -t "scope-leak-mode" --reporter=verbose 2>&1 | tail -20` (≥ 2 new passing test cases — warn-mode happy path + hard-mode legacy).
  - **Anchor**: Cockburn, *Crystal Clear*, 2004, Ch. 3 §"Vertical Slices" — vertical-slice features inherently span multiple files (impl + test + doc + caller updates). A `**Files**:` field that lists only the impl file forces a horizontal-slice fiction onto a vertical-slice reality. The published spec-kit pattern (which minsky inherits via Rule #9) explicitly assumes a vertical slice; the runner's scope-leak verdict should align.
  - **Why**: 4 iterations across 2 distinct tasks (lma-middleware-canonical + example-model-use-merge) hit scope-leak in 2h on example-service-api host. Each one wasted 3-15 min of devin compute and forced a watchdog respawn. The scope-leak verdict was fighting devin's instinct instead of informing it.
  - **Files**: `novel/cross-repo-runner/src/runner.ts` (scope-leak detection at line 166-169), `novel/cross-repo-runner/src/runner.test.ts`, `novel/cross-repo-runner/src/host-loop.ts` (line 282 — the `if (outcome.verdict === "scope-leak") return "scope-leak"` early-exit), `novel/cross-repo-runner/bin/minsky-run.mjs` (env wiring).

- [ ] `parse-files-field-multi-line-nested` — `parseFilesField` should accept the multi-line nested-bullet form, not just inline `**Files**: <globs>`

  - **ID**: parse-files-field-multi-line-nested
  - **Tags**: p2, parser, scope-leak, observed-in-fleet
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Touches**: `novel/cross-repo-runner/src/runner.ts`, `novel/cross-repo-runner/src/runner.test.ts`
  - **Hypothesis**: `parseFilesField` (`runner.ts:251-258`) regex `^\s*-\s*\*\*Files\*\*:\s*(.+)$/m` only matches inline content on the same line as `**Files**:`. Many real-world TASKS.md files (including the `example-service-plugin` repo's, which we ran against on 2026-05-16) use a nested-bullet form: `**Files**:` on its own line, then a blank line, then indented `-`-prefixed `path/x.ts` items. For these blocks the regex misses, `allowedPaths` returns empty, `detectScopeLeak` short-circuits (`runner.ts:94 — if (allowedPaths.length === 0) return []`), and the scope-leak gate is silently bypassed. Pre-registration: post-fix, when `parseFilesField` finds `**Files**:` with no inline content but a nested-bullet list follows, it walks forward collecting indented `-`-prefixed items (stripping leading backticks + trailing `(new)` / `(modified)` annotations) and returns them as `allowedPaths`. Across the 7-task example-service-plugin TASKS.md, `extractAllowedPathsFromTaskBlock` returns ≥1 path for every P0/P1 task instead of 0 for all of them — closes the silently-fail-open hole that companion `MINSKY_SCOPE_LEAK_MODE=warn|hard` only papered over.
  - **Success**: against an unmodified `~/apps/example-service/example-service-plugin/TASKS.md`, `extractAllowedPathsFromTaskBlock` returns ≥1 path for every P0/P1 task (today: 0 for every task in that file) AND existing 4 inline-form paired tests still pass unchanged.
  - **Details**: (a) Extend `parseFilesField` to detect the multi-line form: when the inline regex misses but a `**Files**:` line exists, scan forward up to 20 lines, collecting any line that matches `^\s+-\s+(.+)$` until indent drops below `**Files**` indent + 2 OR a blank line is followed by a non-list line. (b) Per-path: strip leading/trailing backticks, strip a trailing space-prefixed `(new)` / `(modified)` / `(no change)` annotation, trim. (c) Three new paired tests in `runner.test.ts`: (i) multi-line-3-paths-no-annotations; (ii) multi-line-with-annotations; (iii) mixed-inline-AND-multi-line (defensive — prefer the inline form if both present). (d) Symmetric support in `parseTouchesField`. (e) Top-of-function JSDoc documents both supported shapes with examples.
  - **Verification**: paired tests above. Live re-run against `~/apps/example-service/example-service-plugin/TASKS.md` (pre-flatten — see companion task in that repo's PR #291): `node --input-type=module -e 'import {parseTasksMd, extractAllowedPathsFromTaskBlock} from "./novel/cross-repo-runner/dist/index.js"; ...'` returns ≥1 path for every P0/P1 task. Existing 4 inline-form paired tests at `runner.test.ts:239-280` still pass unchanged.
  - **Measurement**: against `~/apps/example-service/example-service-plugin/TASKS.md` (pre-flatten), `extractAllowedPathsFromTaskBlock` per-task path-count median ≥ 2 (today: 0 for every task in that file). Companion measurement after both fixes ship: rolling 7d of iterations marked `scope-leak` AND with `pr_url != null` drops to 0 (today's number for the example-service-plugin host alone: 4).
  - **Pivot**: if multi-line parsing introduces false-positive globs (picks up file names from `**Details**:` continuation prose) more than 1 in 30 task blocks, narrow the multi-line scanner to ONLY accept lines that look like file paths (start with a known prefix `src/`, `tests/`, `scripts/`, `docs/`, OR end with a known extension `.ts | .tsx | .js | .mjs | .json | .md`). If THAT still produces false positives, retire the multi-line support and document the inline-only constraint with a tasks-md-lint rule that warns at author time (see agentbrew `tasks-md-files-field-inline-format-lint` companion task).
  - **Acceptance**: (1) `parseFilesField` accepts the multi-line nested-bullet form (3 new paired tests); (2) `parseTouchesField` accepts the same form (1 new paired test); (3) JSDoc on both functions documents the two supported shapes; (4) live re-run against unmodified `~/apps/example-service/example-service-plugin/TASKS.md` returns non-empty allowedPaths for every P0/P1 task; (5) existing 4 inline-form paired tests still pass unchanged.
  - **Anchor**: 2026-05-16 example-service-plugin run — scope-leak detection silently fail-open on every task because the multi-line form was the convention there. The tasks.md spec at <https://github.com/tasksmd/tasks.md/blob/main/spec.md> doesn't prescribe a single form for `**Files**:`, so the parser must accept both. Companion task in example-service-plugin (`flatten-files-field-for-minsky-scope-leak`, #291) flattens that repo's format as a workaround; this task fixes the parser so future hosts following the spec's multi-line convention don't need workarounds.
  - **Risk**: low. The change is additive (existing inline parsing unchanged), bounded (max 20 lines of forward-scan), and gated by paired tests. Failure mode is the Pivot's false-positive case, mitigated by the prefix/extension filter.
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-16 example-service-plugin autonomous run — iteration 1 of `parameterize-auth-posture-probe` marked `scope-leak` with no actual scope leak. Iteration record at `~/apps/example-service/example-service-plugin/.minsky/experiment-store/cross-repo/parameterize-auth-posture-probe.jsonl`. Sibling task `MINSKY_SCOPE_LEAK_MODE=warn|hard` (the P2 above) covers the verdict-recovery path; this task covers the parser-correctness path.

- [ ] `minsky-stop-host-filter` — `minsky stop` should accept `--host <dir>` to limit SIGTERM to a single host instead of every minsky-run on the machine

  - **ID**: minsky-stop-host-filter
  - **Tags**: p2, cli, multi-tenant-safety, observed-in-fleet
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Touches**: `bin/minsky` (the bash shim), `bin/minsky.test.mjs` (new — first paired test for the shim)
  - **Hypothesis**: Today `minsky stop` (defined in `bin/minsky` ~line 70-78) runs `pkill -TERM -f "cross-repo-runner/bin/minsky-run"` — it SIGTERMs every `minsky-run` on the host regardless of which host repo each is driving. When two operators (or one operator running two minsky-runs in parallel — e.g. plugin + api at the same machine) co-exist, calling `minsky stop` to clean up one of them sends SIGTERM to BOTH. Pre-registration: post-fix, `minsky stop` accepts an optional `--host <dir>` filter. `minsky stop --host /Users/.../example-service-plugin` SIGTERMs only the minsky-run whose argv contains the matching `--host`. `minsky stop` with no flag stays the existing global behavior but prints a pre-kill banner listing every host that's about to be stopped + a 3s grace period to abort with Ctrl-C.
  - **Success**: `pgrep -c "cross-repo-runner/bin/minsky-run"` after `minsky stop --host /tmp/host-a` equals pre-stop count minus 1 (only the matching host killed), AND `minsky stop` without flag prints the host-list banner and sleeps 3s before killing.
  - **Details**: (a) Extend the `bin/minsky` bash shim's `case` block for `stop` to parse `--host <dir>` via shift+positional. (b) When `--host` is set: `pgrep -f "cross-repo-runner/bin/minsky-run.*--host $RESOLVED_DIR"` (use `realpath` so relative paths still match absolute-path argv). Loop: kill each matched pid; report `sent SIGTERM to pid $P (host=$RESOLVED_DIR)`. (c) When `--host` not set: pre-list every match's argv via `ps`, sleep 3s (unless `MINSKY_NON_INTERACTIVE=1`) before sending the kill so the operator can Ctrl-C to abort. (d) `minsky stop --help` documents both shapes. (e) Two paired tests in a new `bin/minsky.test.mjs`: --host filter case (mock pgrep returns two processes, verify only the matching one is killed) and no-filter case (verify both are killed + the warning banner is printed). Note: the existing minsky shim is bash and isn't unit-tested today; this is the first paired test for it.
  - **Verification**: paired tests above. Live integration: in two terminals, run `minsky --host /tmp/host-a` and `minsky --host /tmp/host-b` (both bootstrapped). In a third terminal: `minsky stop --host /tmp/host-a` — terminal A exits cleanly, terminal B keeps running.
  - **Measurement**: `pgrep -c "cross-repo-runner/bin/minsky-run"` after `minsky stop --host /tmp/host-a` equals pre-stop count minus 1. Without the filter (today's behavior): equals 0.
  - **Pivot**: if the `--host` matcher produces false matches (kills a minsky-run whose host path is a SUBSTRING of the argument) more than 1 in 50 invocations, switch to exact-match via `realpath` + explicit `--host="$DIR"` argv pattern instead of substring `pgrep -f`. If false matches persist after that, retire the filter and revert to today's global behavior with the warning banner as the only safety.
  - **Acceptance**: (1) `minsky stop --host <dir>` SIGTERMs only the matching minsky-run; (2) `minsky stop` (no flag) prints the host-list banner and sleeps 3s before killing; (3) `MINSKY_NON_INTERACTIVE=1 minsky stop` skips the banner; (4) two paired tests pass; (5) `minsky stop --help` documents both shapes.
  - **Anchor**: 2026-05-16 example-service-plugin run — operator (this session) had two minsky-runs concurrent (plugin + api on the other session), needed to restart only the plugin one without killing the api one. Today's `minsky stop` would have killed both. Workaround used: direct PID kill via `kill -TERM <pid>`. Rule #6 (let-it-crash AT the right boundary — `minsky stop --host` is the operator's per-host kill switch; today's global stop crosses the wrong boundary). Hyrum's Law: the host-filter capability is exactly what multi-operator machines need.
  - **Risk**: low. Bash-shim-only change, additive (no flag preserves today's behavior + adds the safety banner), gated by the first paired test for the shim.
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-16 example-service-plugin autonomous run — supervisor log at `/tmp/minsky-example-service-plugin/supervisor.log` documents that the operator had to be careful about `minsky stop` interaction with the other session's API minsky-run. Workaround was direct PID kill; this task documents the right CLI shape.

- [ ] `competitor-deep-research-tier-s-2026-05` — deep-research the 4 Tier-S competitor stubs in `competitors/` (Agentless, Claude Agent SDK, Goose, Cline) under the sharpened identity *"OpenHands that improves itself + follows hard rules and principles"*; each must resolve to a concrete Dependency / Competitor / Reference verdict with cited sources
  - **ID**: competitor-deep-research-tier-s-2026-05
  - **Tags**: p2, competitive-research, dependency-decision, rule-1, rule-9, observed-2026-05-22, vision-fulfillment
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Surfaced-by**: operator 2026-05-22 conversation about competitor research priorities. Produced 5 STUB files in `competitors/`; the operator then sharpened the surviving-minsky identity (no task-level persona pipeline; two axes only: self-improvement loop + hard rules and principles), which demoted LangGraph from Tier-S (no concrete use case post-OpenHands-adoption); the active backlog is the 4 remaining stubs. OpenHands flipped from Competitor → Dependency same day as the precedent.
  - **Hypothesis**: deep-researching these 4 stubs under the sharpened identity surfaces at least one additional concrete change (adoption, benchmark addition, or interface alignment) beyond the OpenHands adoption already approved. Strongest prior: Agentless is a thesis falsifier we MUST include in the benchmark corpus regardless of adoption verdict, because the surviving-minsky thesis (autonomic loop + hard rules on top of OpenHands) is exactly what Agentless's 3-step pipeline implicitly tests.
  - **Success**: all 4 active stubs (`agentless.md`, `claude-agent-sdk.md`, `goose.md`, `cline.md`) have the `<!-- STUB -->` HTML comment removed AND the "OPEN: research questions" section replaced by cited answers AND the `Relationship` line classified as one of `Dependency` / `Competitor` / `Reference` per the README decision rule; at least 1 of the 4 results in a new row in either `novel/competitive-benchmark/src/competitors.ts` (benchmark corpus) or `ARCHITECTURE.md` § dependency table (runtime adoption); Agentless row must exist in `novel/competitive-benchmark/src/competitors.ts` regardless of its adoption verdict.
  - **Pivot**: if 0 of 4 produce a concrete change AND Agentless's benchmark reading is within the noise band of OpenHands, the current dependency landscape is settled for M1; close the Tier-S backlog, document in `validated-learnings.md` why prior expectations were wrong, revisit at M2.
  - **Measurement**: `grep -l "<!-- STUB" competitors/agentless.md competitors/claude-agent-sdk.md competitors/goose.md competitors/cline.md 2>/dev/null | wc -l` returns `0`; `git log --since=2026-05-22 --diff-filter=AM -- competitors/ novel/competitive-benchmark/src/competitors.ts ARCHITECTURE.md` shows the closing commit(s); `grep -c "Agentless" novel/competitive-benchmark/src/competitors.ts` returns at least 1.
  - **Anchor**: Porter, *Competitive Strategy*, 1980 — Ch. 2 (Five Forces analysis structures each entry's threat / dependency / substitute profile); reinforces vision.md rule #1 (don't reinvent the wheel — adopt where the OSS is best-in-class).
  - **Details**: For each of the 4 active stubs invoke the `competitor-research` skill to: (1) verify existence + license + version + maintainer health from primary sources; (2) answer every line under "OPEN: research questions" with a cited reading; (3) re-classify Relationship under the README decision rule (fit-a-layer → best-in-class → no-lock-in → preserves-thesis); (4) for adoption candidates draft the `novel/adapters/<name>.ts` interface stub in the same PR (rule #2); (5) for Agentless specifically, also add it to `novel/competitive-benchmark/src/competitors.ts` with a `local-harness` ResultSource because it is a load-bearing thesis falsifier regardless of adoption verdict. Order by priority: Agentless (thesis-falsifier — directly tests whether the surviving minsky layer adds value over a vanilla agent loop, highest impact) → Claude Agent SDK (affects how the OpenHands adapter could optionally use Anthropic-native primitives) → Goose (second runtime backend candidate alongside OpenHands) → Cline (reference / IDE niche — learn from rather than adopt). LangGraph's stub is on file (`competitors/langgraph.md`) with a re-promote criterion; do NOT research unless one of the 3 named concrete use cases surfaces.
  - **Files**: `competitors/agentless.md`, `competitors/claude-agent-sdk.md`, `competitors/goose.md`, `competitors/cline.md`, `competitors/README.md` (priority backlog table), `novel/competitive-benchmark/src/competitors.ts` (Agentless row, required), possibly `ARCHITECTURE.md` § dependency table (Goose adoption as second backend), possibly `vision.md` § Pattern conformance index (any new adapter row).
  - **Acceptance**: (1) measurement command returns `0`; (2) `competitors/README.md` Deep-research backlog table is updated to show each entry as moved-to-adopted, moved-to-competitor, or marked-not-applicable with reason; (3) Agentless row exists in `novel/competitive-benchmark/src/competitors.ts`; (4) a fresh reviewer reading each of the 4 final entries cold reaches the same Relationship verdict; (5) all literature citations resolve to real, primary sources (rule #5 anchor-primary-source lint passes).

## P3

- [ ] `cli-script-consolidation-pass` — the current `package.json` has 28+ scripts in 7 categories (build/lint/test/m1/changelog/metrics/diagnostic/minsky). Several are one-shot diagnostics (`chaos:budget-exhaust`, `runany:audit`, `cto-audit:metrics`, `llm-provider:throughput`, `daemon-pr-lint:metrics`, `m1:metrics`, `m1:observability`, `m1:coverage`, `test:m1-tdd`) that are used 0-1 times per quarter — they pollute the `pnpm run` list. Goal: collapse the rarely-run ones into `pnpm <category>` with a positional subverb, OR move them to docs/runbook with a `bash:` prefix so they're discoverable but not script-listed
  - **ID**: cli-script-consolidation-pass
  - **Tags**: p3, dx, cli-ux, observed-2026-05-26, operator-directive
  - **Milestone**: M2
  - **Competitive-goal**: reduce-pnpm-run-noise — competitor agents (Aider, Cline, Devin SDK) ship with ≤10 npm scripts. Minsky's 28+ is the high outlier in the OSS coding-agent set; consolidation closes the "feels-too-complex-to-onboard" perception gap surfaced in fresh-install user studies.
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-26 operator directive ("let's reduce number of cli commands?") — paired with the `dogfood:* → minsky:*` rename in the same session. The rename retired the worst noise (7 dogfood scripts → 6 minsky:* scripts), but the diagnostic/m1/audit families remain.
  - **Hypothesis**: 8 of the 28 scripts are used <1x/quarter (chaos, runany audit, cto-audit metrics, llm-provider throughput, daemon-pr-lint metrics, m1 metrics, m1 observability, m1 coverage). Moving them out of `package.json` into `docs/runbook.md` with explicit `bash:` invocations leaves the discoverable surface at the high-frequency 20. Operators who need the diagnostics still have them; new operators don't see the noise.
  - **Success**: `pnpm run` lists ≤20 scripts; the 8 diagnostic scripts move to `docs/runbook.md` with `node scripts/<name>.mjs` shown explicitly; no breaking change to CI (CI workflows updated in lockstep); each retired script's old `pnpm <name>` invocation prints a one-line deprecation hint pointing at the runbook entry for 30 days.
  - **Pivot**: if any of the 8 candidates is actually run weekly by CI or an operator (verified by `git log --grep='pnpm chaos:\|pnpm runany:audit\|pnpm cto-audit:metrics'`), keep it as a script. Threshold: ≥1 invocation/week in the 30 days before this task lands.
  - **Measurement**: `jq '.scripts | keys | length' package.json | awk '$1 <= 20 {print "PASS"} $1 > 20 {print "FAIL"}'`
  - **Anchor**: rule #1 (npm/brew convention compliance — `npm scripts` should be the operator's daily-use surface, not a catalog); cli-design skill at `~/.config/devin/skills/cli-design/SKILL.md` (default-behavior opportunities + npm convention review).
  - **Details**: (1) audit `git log --since='30 days ago' --grep='pnpm '` to identify per-script invocation frequency; (2) move <1x/quarter scripts to `docs/runbook.md` under their categorical headers; (3) update `.github/workflows/*.yml` if any CI uses the retired scripts; (4) add a stub script per retired name that prints `pnpm <name> is retired — see docs/runbook.md § <section>` and exits 1 (one-month transition).
  - **Files**: `package.json` (remove 8 scripts + add 8 deprecation stubs), `docs/runbook.md` (new sections), `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (update if needed), `.lefthook.yml` (update if needed).
  - **Acceptance**: (1) `jq '.scripts | keys | length' package.json` returns ≤20; (2) each retired script's stub prints the deprecation hint; (3) `docs/runbook.md` has the migrated commands; (4) `pnpm test` + `pnpm pre-pr-lint` still pass; (5) 30 days post-merge, retire the stubs entirely.

- [ ] `bash-skeleton-multi-worker-numeric-ids` — the bash `bin/minsky` shim lacks the TS wrapper's `minsky 1` / `minsky 2` per-worker numeric IDs (the TS `novel/tick-loop/bin/minsky.mjs` mapped them to `.minsky/workers/<id>.pid` / `.minsky/workers/<id>.log` files for parallel-daemon scenarios). Scout found while landing PR #887 (phase-11b step 4 binstub flip) — the binstub now points at the bash shim; an operator who tries `pnpm minsky 1` gets an "unknown subcommand 1" error instead of spawning worker #1. Not blocking phase-11b deletion because (a) the bash skeleton's launchd `KeepAlive=true` provides single-daemon survival, (b) no current operator depends on multi-worker, (c) the TS `minsky.mjs` is being deleted in step 8 either way
  - **ID**: bash-skeleton-multi-worker-numeric-ids
  - **Tags**: p3, scout-finding, bash-skeleton, cli-ux, observed-2026-05-25
  - **Milestone**: M2
  - **Competitive-goal**: parity surface for the rare operator who runs multiple parallel Minsky daemons (e.g. one against minsky-on-itself, one against a contractor repo). Lost when phase-11b deletes the TS wrapper; restore as a P3 follow-up if 1+ operator files the gap.
  - **Hypothesis**: multi-worker parallelism is a rare use case (operators usually run one minsky per machine). The bash shim's single-daemon model is the canonical post-phase-11b shape; per-worker IDs become a special case rather than the default. Pre-registration: if ≥1 operator files a "I need multiple minsky daemons running on this machine" task within 30 days of phase-11b shipping, port the multi-worker UX (`.minsky/workers/<id>.{pid,log}` convention + bash launchctl-per-worker plist) from the deleted TS wrapper. Falsifiable: if 0 operators report the gap in 30 days, the multi-worker path was vestigial.
  - **Success**: `pnpm minsky 1` (or `bash bin/minsky 1`) spawns a separate worker on the canonical host with `.minsky/workers/1.pid` recorded. `pnpm minsky logs 1` tails that worker's log. `pnpm minsky stop 1` SIGTERM's the worker without touching worker 0. Tested via `tests/minsky-run.bats` (new `@test "bin/minsky <id> spawns worker <id>"` cases). Threshold: 3+ paired bats tests cover the contract.
  - **Pivot**: if ≥1 operator reports the gap within 30 days AND the launchctl-per-worker plist shape requires >2 days to port from the deleted TS path, escalate to P2 and budget a week. If ≤30 days pass with no operator reports, mark this task `wontfix` and remove.
  - **Measurement**: `bash bin/minsky 1` exits 0 with `.minsky/workers/1.pid` present; `bash bin/minsky logs 1` exits 0; `bash bin/minsky stop 1` exits 0 without affecting `.minsky/workers/0.pid`. Tests: `tests/minsky-run.bats` line count grows by ≥3 with `@test "bin/minsky <id> ..."` cases.
  - **Anchor**: rule #6 (let-it-crash AT the right boundary — the boundary is bash, not TS); operator-feedback-driven P3 (no current report); deleted `novel/tick-loop/bin/minsky.mjs` § "Behaviour of `minsky [<id>]`".
  - **Details**: The TS wrapper's per-worker UX is at `novel/tick-loop/bin/minsky.mjs` lines 25-50 (the operator-facing block comment) and the `parseArgsAndAction` function. After phase-11b step 8 deletes the file, the reference becomes the git history. To restore: (a) add a `case [0-9]*)` branch to `bin/minsky` that resolves to `.minsky/workers/${1}.{pid,log}` paths; (b) update the launchctl/systemd unit template to accept a `MINSKY_WORKER_ID` env var; (c) add bats coverage. Estimated 1-2d if the launchctl side is straightforward.
  - **Files**: `bin/minsky` (new `case [0-9]*)`); `distribution/launchd/com.minsky.tick-loop.plist` (template per-worker ID); `distribution/systemd/run-tick-loop.sh` (read `MINSKY_WORKER_ID`); `tests/minsky-run.bats` (3+ paired tests).
  - **Acceptance**: GIVEN an operator runs `pnpm minsky 1` after running `pnpm minsky` (worker 0), THEN both workers are alive AND each has its own pid + log file. GIVEN `pnpm minsky stop 0`, THEN worker 0 stops, worker 1 keeps running.
  - **Risk**: low. Per-worker isolation is a well-understood pattern; the bash skeleton's launchctl integration is already in place for single-worker; the per-worker variant is a templating change.
  - **Surfaced-by**: PR #887 (phase-11b step 4) — the binstub flip surfaced the TS wrapper's multi-worker feature didn't have a bash equivalent.
- [ ] `bash-skeleton-tick-interval-ms-flag` — add `--tick-interval-ms` flag to `bin/minsky-run.sh` that introduces an inter-iteration sleep matching the TS daemon's old 5-min cadence. Scout found while landing PR #888 (phase-11b step 5 supervisor migration) — the TS daemon had an in-process 5-min sleep; the bash skeleton has none, so launchd's `KeepAlive=true` respawns after each iteration batch with only `ThrottleInterval=5` (5s) between respawns. Net cadence: ~30-60s per iteration (real openhands) vs ~5min in the TS daemon. Not blocking; only needed if production cadence proves too aggressive (excessive API calls, excessive spawn overhead)
  - **ID**: bash-skeleton-tick-interval-ms-flag
  - **Tags**: p3, scout-finding, bash-skeleton, cadence, observed-2026-05-25
  - **Milestone**: M2
  - **Competitive-goal**: matches the TS daemon's tunable iteration cadence; preserves operator's ability to throttle agent invocations on per-machine basis.
  - **Hypothesis**: the bash skeleton's "as-fast-as-possible + launchd ThrottleInterval=5s" cadence is acceptable for production. Pre-registration: if production observability shows excessive agent-spawn cost or hitting Anthropic 5h-rate limits within 30 days of phase-11b shipping, add the flag with a default sleep matching the TS daemon's 5-min value. Falsifiable: if 30 days pass with no rate-limit/cost spike, the no-sleep cadence is the correct shape and this task is closed `wontfix`.
  - **Success**: `bin/minsky-run.sh` accepts a `--tick-interval-ms N` arg that triggers `sleep N/1000` between iteration batches. `distribution/systemd/run-tick-loop.sh` re-enables the `MINSKY_TICK_INTERVAL_MS` env-var → CLI arg mapping (currently a no-op with stderr warning). Bats test in `tests/minsky-run.bats` exercises the sleep path with `--tick-interval-ms 1` (1ms) for fast verification.
  - **Pivot**: if 30 days pass with no operator-reported issue, close `wontfix`.
  - **Measurement**: `bash bin/minsky-run.sh --tick-interval-ms 1000 --max-iterations 2 --host <fixture>` records ≥1 second elapsed between the 2 iteration ledger entries. Bats test exists.
  - **Anchor**: PR #888 phase-11b step 5 migrated the supervisor; the TS daemon's `--tick-interval-ms` flag had 5-min default; deleted in this PR's step 8. operator-feedback-driven P3.
  - **Details**: Add a `--tick-interval-ms` flag parse at line ~49 of `bin/minsky-run.sh`. Insert a `[[ $TICK_INTERVAL_MS -gt 0 ]] && sleep $((TICK_INTERVAL_MS/1000))` after the host walk completes. Re-enable the env-var → CLI arg mapping in `distribution/systemd/run-tick-loop.sh`.
  - **Files**: `bin/minsky-run.sh` (add flag + sleep), `distribution/systemd/run-tick-loop.sh` (re-enable env-var mapping), `tests/minsky-run.bats` (paired test).
  - **Acceptance**: GIVEN `--tick-interval-ms 1000 --max-iterations 2`, WHEN the script runs against a fixture host with ≥1 eligible task, THEN ≥1 second elapses between consecutive ledger entries. GIVEN `MINSKY_TICK_INTERVAL_MS=5000`, WHEN `run-tick-loop.sh` runs, THEN the bash skeleton receives `--tick-interval-ms 5000`.
  - **Risk**: low. Additive flag with default 0 (no sleep) — preserves current behavior.
  - **Surfaced-by**: PR #888 (phase-11b step 5) — the env-var → CLI arg mapping had to drop `MINSKY_TICK_INTERVAL_MS` because the bash skeleton doesn't support the flag yet.

- [ ] `metric-freshness-expected-should-read-from-success-metrics` — `.github/workflows/ci.yml`'s `metric-freshness` job hard-codes the `--expected` list of metric IDs inline (14 IDs as a single comma-separated string on the `node scripts/check-metric-freshness.mjs --expected ...` line). When a PR adds a new entry to `SUCCESS_METRICS` in `novel/dashboard-web/src/metrics.ts`, the metric-freshness gate fails CI with "unexpected metric `<id>` rendered — not present in `SUCCESS_METRICS` (drift)" until the operator ALSO edits the ci.yml line. Scout found while landing PR #873 — Path A LOC scoreboard added 3 IDs and the gate fired
  - **ID**: metric-freshness-expected-should-read-from-success-metrics
  - **Tags**: p3, scout-finding, ci, drift-protection, observed-2026-05-25
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Competitive-goal**: closes the operator-burden cost of "adding a metric to SUCCESS_METRICS" — today it's a 3-file change (metrics.ts + dashboard-metric-ids.json + ci.yml --expected list). The dashboard-metric-ids drift gate catches the JSON; the metric-freshness gate catches the ci.yml. Both should be reading SUCCESS_METRICS directly so adding a metric is a 1-file change (with the drift-protection tests verifying everywhere it's read from).
  - **Hypothesis**: the metric-freshness script's `--expected` flag exists because the CI environment doesn't (or didn't) have node + dashboard-web built before the lint step. But the ci.yml job DOES install pnpm + run `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` before the lint, so dashboard-web's compiled `dist/metrics.js` IS available to import — the `--expected` flag is vestigial. Pre-registration: post-fix, the metric-freshness script defaults to reading `SUCCESS_METRICS` directly via the same `await import(novel/dashboard-web/dist/metrics.js)` pattern that `scripts/metrics-render.mjs` already uses (line ~208). The `--expected` flag stays as an escape hatch for non-CI invocations. Falsifiable: if the `dist/metrics.js` isn't available at the lint step's working dir for any reason (e.g. dashboard-web build was skipped), the script falls back to the hard-coded list with a stderr warning naming the new-metric onboarding workflow.
  - **Success**: `.github/workflows/ci.yml`'s metric-freshness job runs `node scripts/check-metric-freshness.mjs` WITHOUT the `--expected` arg. The script reads `SUCCESS_METRICS.map(m => m.id)` from `novel/dashboard-web/dist/metrics.js`. Paired test added in `scripts/check-metric-freshness.test.mjs` that exercises the default-from-source path. The drift-protection test (`dashboard-metric-ids-sync.test.mjs`) STILL passes because it independently asserts the JSON↔TS mirror.
  - **Pivot**: if reading from `dist/metrics.js` adds CI latency >5s or fails on cold-cache runs ≥1× per week, revert to a JSON-source-of-truth pattern — the `dashboard-metric-ids.json` already mirrors the IDs; metric-freshness should read THAT instead of the inline flag.
  - **Measurement**: `git log --since='30 days ago' --oneline -- .github/workflows/ci.yml | grep -ci 'metric-freshness'` ≤1/month post-fix (today: ≥3 in the last 30d because the inline list churns every time SUCCESS_METRICS grows). AND `node scripts/check-metric-freshness.mjs` (no --expected) exits 0 against main without manual list maintenance.
  - **Anchor**: 2026-05-25 PR #873 — metric-freshness fired with "unexpected metric `path-a-loc-novel-tree`" until ci.yml was manually edited; rule #2 (single source of truth — SUCCESS_METRICS is the canonical list, the lint should read from it); rule #10 (deterministic enforcement — the lint already runs in CI, removing the manual sync step doesn't reduce the gate's coverage); `scripts/metrics-render.mjs` line ~208 (existing pattern for importing from `dist/metrics.js`).
  - **Details**: (a) Read `scripts/check-metric-freshness.mjs` for the current `parseArgs` shape — the `--expected` flag is the entry point. (b) Add a `loadExpectedFromSource(repoRoot)` helper that imports `novel/dashboard-web/dist/metrics.js` and returns `SUCCESS_METRICS.map(m => m.id)`. (c) When `--expected` is absent, call the helper; fall back to hard-coded baseline with stderr warning if `dist/metrics.js` doesn't exist. (d) Update `.github/workflows/ci.yml`'s metric-freshness job to drop the `--expected` arg. (e) Paired test in `scripts/check-metric-freshness.test.mjs` for the default-from-source path. (f) Add a comment in `metrics.ts` near the SUCCESS_METRICS export naming the consumers (metrics-render, dashboard-metric-ids JSON, metric-freshness lint) — operator-visible "what to update when adding a metric".
  - **Files**: `scripts/check-metric-freshness.mjs` (add helper + default path), `scripts/check-metric-freshness.test.mjs` (paired test for default path), `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (drop --expected arg), `novel/dashboard-web/src/metrics.ts` (consumer comment).
  - **Touches**: `scripts/check-metric-freshness.mjs`, `scripts/check-metric-freshness.test.mjs`, `.github/workflows/ci.yml`, `novel/dashboard-web/src/metrics.ts`.
  - **Acceptance**: (1) Default invocation reads from `dist/metrics.js`. (2) Existing `--expected` flag still works (no breaking change). (3) Paired test covers the default-from-source path. (4) `ci.yml` job line is `node scripts/check-metric-freshness.mjs` (no extra flag). (5) Next SUCCESS_METRICS addition (e.g. M2 metric onboarding) requires zero ci.yml edits.
  - **Risk**: low. Additive default — explicit `--expected` flag still wins. The `dist/metrics.js` import is already exercised by `scripts/metrics-render.mjs` so the path is proven.

- [ ] `pr-body-self-grade-shape-lint` — `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md` ALREADY has the correct `## Hypothesis self-grade` block shape (Predicted/Observed/Match/Lesson with placeholder cells). The bug is that agents bypassing the template via `gh pr create --body-file <foo>` (the common script-driven path) tend to write the rule-#9 task-pre-registration form (Hypothesis/Success/Pivot/Measurement/Anchor) by reflex — which `pr-self-grade` rejects. Solution: extend `scripts/check-pr-self-grade.mjs` to detect the alternative 5-field form and emit a helpful pointer in the error message rather than the generic "missing Predicted" message. The agent then sees "you wrote the task-pre-reg form; use the PR-template form (Predicted/Observed/Match/Lesson)" and can self-correct without re-reading the template
  - **ID**: pr-body-self-grade-shape-lint
  - **Tags**: p3, scout-finding, ci, pr-template, observed-2026-05-25
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Competitive-goal**: closes the operator-feedback-loop tightening cost: every "PR body needs Predicted/Observed/Match/Lesson but I wrote Hypothesis/Success/Pivot/Measurement/Anchor" round-trip burns ~3 minutes of fixup time per PR. Across a 24h marathon (10 PRs at this rate) that's 30 min of preventable churn. The fix is a clearer lint message, not a template edit (template is already correct).
  - **Hypothesis**: extending `scripts/check-pr-self-grade.mjs` to detect `Hypothesis:` / `Success:` / `Pivot:` / `Measurement:` / `Anchor:` field names (the task-pre-reg form) and emit a clear error like "you wrote the task-pre-registration form; the PR-self-grade gate wants the PR-template form (Predicted/Observed/Match/Lesson). See .github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md" makes the failure self-explanatory. Pre-registration: post-fix, the next 5 agent-authored PRs that ship with the wrong form pass on the SECOND push (not the third) because the error message is direct enough to fix without reading the template source.
  - **Success**: (1) `scripts/check-pr-self-grade.mjs` detects the 5-field task-pre-reg form when ≥3 of the 5 field names appear in the body. (2) When detected, the error message names the alternative form AND points at the PR template. (3) Paired tests in `scripts/check-pr-self-grade.test.mjs` cover both the detection case and the existing missing-field cases. (4) The next 5 agent-authored PRs that originally would have shipped the wrong shape get a clearer error.
  - **Pivot**: if the 5-field detection produces ≥1 false positive (a PR body legitimately uses "Hypothesis:" in a non-task context, e.g. in a commit message quote), tighten the heuristic to require the cells appear as their own `- **Hypothesis**:` list bullets, not embedded prose.
  - **Measurement**: count of PRs in the last 14 days where `pr-self-grade` failed on the first push (today: ≥2 — #869, #870). Goal: 0 unique failure-causes within 2 weeks of the lint update landing. Tracked via `gh pr list --limit 50 --json number,title,statusCheckRollup | jq '[.[] | select(.statusCheckRollup[]? | select(.name == "pr-self-grade" and .conclusion == "FAILURE"))] | length'`.
  - **Anchor**: 2026-05-25 PR #870 (this PR) + PR #869 — both hit the same self-grade rewrite. Operator practice: AGENTS.md § "Orchestrator discipline" rule (2). The PR template at `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md` lines 28-33 already encodes the correct shape; the gap is in the LINT error message, not the template.
  - **Details**: (a) Read `scripts/check-pr-self-grade.mjs` lines 35-42 for the current regex shape. (b) Add a `checkAlternativeForm(body)` helper that scans for ≥3 of `Hypothesis:`, `Success:`, `Pivot:`, `Measurement:`, `Anchor:` as field markers. (c) When detected AND any of the required fields are missing, prepend the error list with a special pointer: "looks like you used the rule-#9 task-pre-registration form; the PR-self-grade gate wants the simpler 4-field form per .github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md". (d) Add a paired test in `scripts/check-pr-self-grade.test.mjs` that uses a fixture body with the alternative form and asserts the pointer appears in the error output.
  - **Files**: `scripts/check-pr-self-grade.mjs` (add detection + pointer), `scripts/check-pr-self-grade.test.mjs` (paired test for the detection case).
  - **Touches**: `scripts/check-pr-self-grade.mjs`, `scripts/check-pr-self-grade.test.mjs`.
  - **Acceptance**: (1) Test for the alternative-form pointer exists and passes. (2) Running the script against a body with `Hypothesis:` / `Success:` / `Pivot:` (no `Predicted:`) emits the pointer text. (3) Existing tests still pass.
  - **Risk**: low. Additive — only changes the error message wording when detection fires. Doesn't change the pass/fail decision.

- [ ] `daemon-pre-pr-lint-gate-prose-citation-migration` — `daemon-pre-pr-lint-gate` substrate is fully shipped (PR #863 closed Acceptance (e)) but the task block can't be removed because 4 parity tests in `scripts/daemon-pr-lint-metrics.test.mjs` + 1 test in `scripts/self-diagnose.test.mjs` cite the task block as the canonical citation site for the `ROLLING_30D_MIN_PASS_RATE` threshold (80%) + `daemon-pr-lint-pass-rate` jq selector. Migrate those citations to `docs/daemon-pre-pr-gate.md` so the task block can be removed without breaking the prose-↔-constant parity contract. After this lands, the orphan task block is safe to delete.
  - **ID**: daemon-pre-pr-lint-gate-prose-citation-migration
  - **Tags**: p3, scout-finding, orphan-cleanup-blocker, observed-2026-05-25
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Hypothesis**: moving the threshold + selector prose from TASKS.md's `daemon-pre-pr-lint-gate` block into `docs/daemon-pre-pr-gate.md` (which is already in the test's read path for the self-diagnose parity test) makes the citation source stable across orphan-cleanup cycles. Pre-registration: post-migration, the orphan-cleanup sweep can remove the task block without breaking any parity test in the test suite.
  - **Success**: 5 parity tests pass when `daemon-pre-pr-lint-gate` task block is removed from TASKS.md.
  - **Pivot**: if the parity tests genuinely need to compare prose IN TASKS.md (not docs/), keep the task block but add a comment `<!-- DO NOT REMOVE — parity test citation site -->` so future orphan-cleanup sweeps skip it.
  - **Measurement**: `git rm TASKS.md && pnpm vitest run scripts/daemon-pr-lint-metrics.test.mjs scripts/self-diagnose.test.mjs` in a dry-run branch — both files pass.
  - **Anchor**: 2026-05-25 PR #864 — first attempt removed `daemon-pre-pr-lint-gate` block alongside two other shipped orphans, broke 5 parity tests, had to restore the block. Anchor: rule #9 (pre-registered HDD); rule #2 (canonical-citation-site is the Strategy boundary — moving it to docs/ keeps the test's contract stable).
  - **Files**: `docs/daemon-pre-pr-gate.md` (add threshold + selector citation section with the same text now in the task block), `scripts/daemon-pr-lint-metrics.test.mjs` (update `extractDaemonPrePrLintGateBlock` to read from docs/ instead of TASKS.md), `scripts/self-diagnose.test.mjs` (update the selector parity test similarly), `TASKS.md` (remove the orphan block in the same commit).
  - **Acceptance**: (1) `docs/daemon-pre-pr-gate.md` contains the `ROLLING_30D_MIN_PASS_RATE` threshold prose in both percent (≥80%) and decimal (≥0.80) forms. (2) `docs/daemon-pre-pr-gate.md` contains the literal `select(.id == "daemon-pr-lint-pass-rate")` selector. (3) `scripts/daemon-pr-lint-metrics.test.mjs` parity tests read from docs/ not TASKS.md. (4) `scripts/self-diagnose.test.mjs` selector parity test reads from docs/ not TASKS.md. (5) `daemon-pre-pr-lint-gate` task block is removed from TASKS.md.

- [ ] `ci-yml-add-edited-trigger-type` — `.github/workflows/ci.yml` declares `on: pull_request:` without explicit `types:`, so the default set (`opened, synchronize, reopened`) misses `edited`; PR-body-only fixes (e.g., reformatting the `## Hypothesis self-grade` block to match `scripts/check-pr-self-grade.mjs`'s `Predicted / Observed / Match / Lesson` schema, or fixing `pr-vision-trace`'s `**Vision goal**:` label) don't re-trigger `pr-self-grade` / `ci` jobs, forcing the agent to push an empty commit to force `synchronize`. `pr-vision-trace.yml` already lists `types: [opened, edited, synchronize, ready_for_review, reopened]` — apply the same explicit type list to `ci.yml` so body lints close the feedback loop without an empty-commit hack.
  - **ID**: ci-yml-add-edited-trigger-type
  - **Tags**: p3, scout-finding, ci, feedback-loop, observed-2026-05-25
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Hypothesis**: adding `types: [opened, edited, synchronize, ready_for_review, reopened]` to `ci.yml`'s `on: pull_request:` block makes body-only PR fixes re-trigger `pr-self-grade` and other body-lint jobs automatically. Pre-registration: post-fix, the count of `chore(ci): retrigger` empty commits per rolling 30d window drops to 0; if it stays >0, the underlying check is reading something other than PR body.
  - **Success**: ≤1 `chore(ci): retrigger` style empty commit per rolling 30d window on agent-authored PRs.
  - **Pivot**: if `edited` re-triggers cause unwanted check churn (e.g., bot edits trigger N CI re-runs), narrow the type list to `[opened, synchronize, ready_for_review, reopened]` AND add a separate body-lint workflow with `types: [opened, edited, reopened]` that only runs the cheap body lints.
  - **Measurement**: `gh pr list --search 'author:@me is:merged' --limit 100 --json commits | jq '[.[] | .commits[] | select(.messageHeadline | test("chore.+retrigger"))] | length'` — count of retrigger empty commits in recent merged PRs.
  - **Anchor**: 2026-05-25 PR #863 — body edit to fix `pr-self-grade` template didn't re-trigger `ci.yml`'s `pr-self-grade` job because `edited` isn't in default `pull_request` event types; agent had to push an empty commit. `pr-vision-trace.yml` got this right; `ci.yml` didn't. Anchor: agentbrew rule #10 (deterministic enforcement); zernie.com/blog/feedback-loop-is-all-you-need (close the body-edit feedback loop in CI).
  - **Files**: `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (add explicit `types:` to the `on: pull_request:` block).
  - **Acceptance**: (1) `ci.yml`'s `on: pull_request:` carries `types: [opened, edited, synchronize, ready_for_review, reopened]` matching `pr-vision-trace.yml`'s pattern. (2) Body-only PR edit (e.g., fixing the self-grade block) re-triggers `pr-self-grade` job within ≤60s without an empty commit.

- [ ] `daemon-test-biome-useawait-cleanup` — 17 pre-existing `lint/suspicious/useAwait` warnings in `novel/tick-loop/src/daemon.test.ts` block `pre-commit` biome check (`--error-on-warnings`) when the file is touched; convert each `async (n) => { ...; return X }` to `(n) => Promise.resolve(X)` so the file becomes biome-clean and any future test addition can ship without warning-debt friction
  - **ID**: daemon-test-biome-useawait-cleanup
  - **Tags**: p3, scout-finding, biome, test-hygiene, observed-2026-05-23
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Competitive-goal**: closes a sticky friction point in the slice-by-slice ship cadence — every time the daemon brief surface changes, the matching test additions can't ship in the same commit because biome's `--error-on-warnings` blocks. Eliminating the 17 warnings restores the "edit + test + ship together" idiom for `daemon.test.ts`.
  - **Touches**: `novel/tick-loop/src/daemon.test.ts` (17 sites — lines 331, 362, 601, 648, 659, 837, 842, 988, 993, 1038, and 7 more per `pnpm exec biome check novel/tick-loop/src/daemon.test.ts 2>&1 | grep useAwait`). No production code touched.
  - **Details**: Each warning is the same shape: `someMethod: async (n: T) => { ...; return value; }` inside a test stub object. Replace with `someMethod: (n: T) => Promise.resolve(value)` when the body has only a return, or `someMethod: (n: T) => { ...; return Promise.resolve(value); }` when the body has side effects. Behaviour is identical (Promise.resolve = async-without-await). Run `pnpm exec biome check novel/tick-loop/src/daemon.test.ts` after each batch to confirm warnings drop.
  - **Hypothesis**: converting 17 `async`-without-`await` patterns to `Promise.resolve(...)` is a mechanical pre-existing-warning sweep that drops the file's biome-error count to 0. No test behaviour changes; the test count stays at 105.
  - **Success**: `pnpm exec biome check novel/tick-loop/src/daemon.test.ts` exits 0 with zero warnings; all 105 tests still pass; `git diff` shows ~17 single-line refactors of the form `async (n) => { return X }` → `(n) => Promise.resolve(X)`.
  - **Pivot**: if the conversion changes test behaviour anywhere (e.g., the async wrapper was load-bearing for a microtask-ordering edge case), revert and add `// biome-ignore-next-line lint/suspicious/useAwait: <reason>` on the affected line(s) instead. Don't ship behaviour changes.
  - **Measurement**: `pnpm exec biome check novel/tick-loop/src/daemon.test.ts 2>&1 | grep -c "useAwait"` returns 0 (was 17 as of 2026-05-23); `pnpm exec vitest run novel/tick-loop/src/daemon.test.ts --reporter=basic 2>&1 | grep "Tests"` shows `105 passed (105)`.
  - **Anchor**: rule #11 (no flaky metric is load-bearing — the warnings are a metric of test-fake quality, currently masked by the file-wide biome opt-in; making it iron means making the warnings 0); discovered 2026-05-23 in PR #761 when slice 4 of `minsky-human-comm-via-file` tried to ship 2 new tests in `daemon.test.ts` and the pre-commit biome stage blocked the commit despite the new tests being warning-clean. Scout finding per the next-task protocol's HARD RULE for in-PR discoveries.

- [ ] `user-story-for-m1-11-honest-readme` — M1.11 ("Honest README in <5 min reading time") has no user story; create `user-stories/014-honest-readme.md` so the alignment gate stops reporting M1.11 as a user-story miss
  - **ID**: user-story-for-m1-11-honest-readme
  - **Tags**: p3, scout-finding, alignment, user-story, m1-11, observed-2026-05-23
  - **Milestone**: M1.11
  - **Competitive-goal**: closes the only remaining user-story gap surfaced by `pnpm milestone:check` (1/13 after PR #765) — M1.11 is the meta-criterion about the README itself; without a user story, the alignment gate's ≥10/13 threshold is harder to reach.
  - **Touches**: `user-stories/014-honest-readme.md` (new), `user-stories/tsconfig.json` (extend include glob if needed).
  - **Surfaced-by**: `node scripts/check-milestone-alignment.mjs` after PR #765 — reports M1.11 still missing user-story + sections + integration-test surfaces. Other 12 M1 criteria have at least one user story.
  - **Details**: write a short story (~30 lines) framed as "a developer reads the README in <5 min and knows: what minsky is, install in one command, one-command run, safety story, M1 progress". `## Acceptance criteria` lists the 3-developer user test from M1.11. `## Metric` references `readme-reading-time` proposed metric (≤5 min p95 across 3 devs). `## Integration test` file path can stub to `user-stories/014-honest-readme.test.ts` (paired test that asserts README.md remains under a byte budget — the simplest measurable proxy for reading time).
  - **Hypothesis**: a 30-line user story documenting the M1.11 acceptance criteria + a paired byte-budget test gives the alignment gate the 4 surfaces it needs (user-story + sections + integration-test + metric). After this task, M1.11 should report `user-story ✓ · sections ✓ · test-file ✓` and only metric remains.
  - **Success**: `node scripts/check-milestone-alignment.mjs --json | jq '.per_criterion["M1.11"].userStory.ok and .per_criterion["M1.11"].sections.ok'` returns `true`.
  - **Pivot**: if the byte-budget test is too brittle (README must legitimately grow above the budget for clarity), drop the budget-test pattern and replace with a manual `Status: pending-3-developer-user-test` field on the story. Don't fake the metric to fit the gate.
  - **Measurement**: `node scripts/check-milestone-alignment.mjs --json | jq '.per_criterion["M1.11"].sections.ok'` returns `true`.
  - **Anchor**: parent task `milestone-alignment-fill-gaps-m1` (P1) — this task is one of its sub-clusters.

- [ ] `watch-claude-native-task-standard-monthly` — monthly re-evaluation of Claude Code's agent-teams + interactive-mode task standard against Minsky's TASKS.md owned-surface; trigger a re-analysis if any of four unblock conditions fire
  - **ID**: watch-claude-native-task-standard-monthly
  - **Tags**: p3, standing-loop, watch, rule-14, agent-teams, follow-up, observed-2026-05-23
  - **Milestone**: M2
  - **Cadence**: monthly — first task pick of each calendar month
  - **Competitive-goal**: rule #14 watch-task discipline — when an upstream platform ships a primitive that supersedes a Minsky-owned subsystem, Minsky must detect within 30 days, not whenever someone next reads the docs. This task is the detection mechanism for the Claude Code native task standard specifically; equivalent watches exist (or should exist) for every "investigated, reject-for-now" competitive analysis.
  - **Touches**: `docs/research/claude-native-task-standard.md` (append monthly status section), `TASKS.md` (this task block + possibly unblock `revisit-claude-native-task-standard-when-persistence-fixed` if a trigger fires).
  - **Trigger conditions** (any one fires the revisit task to be unblocked): (1) GitHub issue #33764 (Agent Teams state wiped on session restart) marked closed/resolved; (2) `code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-teams` removes the "experimental" warning; (3) Anthropic announces a stable contract for `~/.claude/tasks/`; (4) `CLAUDE_CODE_TASK_LIST_ID` becomes available in `-p` mode (GH #20424 closes).
  - **Details**: This is a standing-loop task per the next-task protocol § "Standing audit loop tasks". On each invocation: (a) read the four trigger conditions above; (b) for each, check the source (gh issue API + the live docs page); (c) append a dated section `## Status YYYY-MM-DD` to `docs/research/claude-native-task-standard.md` recording the current state of all four; (d) if any trigger has fired, remove the `**Blocked**` field from `revisit-claude-native-task-standard-when-persistence-fixed` (P2) and append a "trigger fired" note; (e) re-file this watch task block with the **Last-evaluated** field updated; (f) if no triggers fired, just bump the date — the watch continues. Standing-loop tasks are NOT removed after invocation; they re-file themselves.
  - **Hypothesis**: most months produce a one-line "no triggers fired, watch continues" status entry. The signal-to-noise ratio is intentionally low — watch tasks are insurance against missing platform shifts. When a trigger fires, the value is high (an inverted owned-surface delta becomes shippable); when no trigger fires, the cost is low (~5 min of doc-checking).
  - **Success**: `docs/research/claude-native-task-standard.md` gains exactly one `## Status YYYY-MM-DD` section per calendar month. If a trigger fires in that month, `revisit-claude-native-task-standard-when-persistence-fixed` becomes unblocked in the same commit.
  - **Pivot**: if 6 consecutive months produce no trigger AND Anthropic announces deprecation of Agent Teams entirely, escalate this watch task to a `wontfix`-flavored close: file `close-claude-native-task-standard-investigation` to delete `docs/research/claude-native-task-standard.md` from `docs/research/` (moving it to `docs/research/archive/` instead) and remove the P2 revisit + P3 watch tasks. The investigation is permanently closed at that point.
  - **Measurement**: `grep -c "^## Status " docs/research/claude-native-task-standard.md` rises by exactly 1 per calendar month after this task ships. Trigger detection: `gh issue view 33764 --repo anthropics/claude-code --json state` returns `closed` is the unblock condition for trigger 1. `curl -sf https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-teams | grep -c experimental` returns 0 is the unblock for trigger 2.
  - **Anchor**: `docs/research/claude-native-task-standard.md` § 6.1 (this task's filing record); vision.md rule #14 (delegate / adopt-native / shrink — the bar this loop re-evaluates); next-task protocol § "Standing audit loop tasks" (the self-re-filing mechanic); operator directive 2026-05-17 ("claude has a standard for tasks — investigate it" — the original directive this watch loop continues).

- [ ] `prompt-optimizer-malformed-json-chaos-test` — add a chaos test for the prompt-optimizer adapter's behavior when the model returns malformed JSON inside `structured()` (row 3 of `novel/adapters/prompt-optimizer/README.md` § Failure modes)
  - **ID**: prompt-optimizer-malformed-json-chaos-test
  - **Tags**: p3, chaos, prompt-optimizer, rule-7, observed-2026-05-23, scout-finding
  - **Milestone**: M2
  - **Surfaced-by**: 2026-05-23 PR #747 (consent subcommand) — `pre-pr-lint --stage=fast` failed on `rule-7-chaos-coverage` because the README row 3 deferred to `mape-k-plan-execute-phases`, a task id that doesn't exist in TASKS.md or git-closed history. Fixed by renaming the deferral to this task and filing this task. Pre-existing dangling reference, not introduced by PR #747 — scout finding caught while shipping unrelated work.
  - **Competitive-goal**: closes the rule-7 chaos-coverage gap for the prompt-optimizer adapter, currently the only row in `novel/adapters/*/README.md` § Failure modes that has neither a test reference nor a resolvable deferral. Every other competitor framework (OpenHands, CrewAI, AutoGen) bundles model-output-malformed handling as part of the agent loop; Minsky's `structured()` helper currently throws synchronously and relies on the supervisor's let-it-crash policy — that's correct per rule #6, but the chaos test that PROVES it throws synchronously (and recovers cleanly) doesn't exist.
  - **Touches**: `novel/adapters/prompt-optimizer/src/structured.test.ts` (new — a sibling test file), `novel/adapters/prompt-optimizer/README.md` § Failure modes row 3 (update the deferral cell to reference the new test path once the test ships), `vision.md` § "Pattern conformance index" (no change — rule #7 row already lists this adapter as a chaos-coverage producer).
  - **Hypothesis**: a 1-test fixture that feeds malformed JSON into `structured()` and asserts `JSON.parse` throws synchronously closes the chaos-coverage gap for row 3 with ~30 LOC of test code. The test does not need to assert recovery behavior — recovery is the supervisor's job per rule #6 (let-it-crash); the chaos test only proves the throw-synchronously contract.
  - **Success**: `pnpm exec vitest run novel/adapters/prompt-optimizer/src/structured.test.ts --reporter=basic 2>&1 | grep -E "Test Files.*passed"` exits 0; AND `node scripts/check-rule-7-chaos-coverage.mjs 2>&1 | grep -q "row 3.*test"` (the README row 3 now resolves to a test path, not a deferral).
  - **Pivot**: if `structured()`'s implementation has changed by the time this task is picked (e.g., it now catches JSON.parse internally and returns a Result type), the chaos shape is different — assert the Result-typed error variant instead of the synchronous throw. Threshold: re-read `novel/adapters/prompt-optimizer/src/structured.ts` before writing the test; if its shape diverged from the README, update the README row first to match reality.
  - **Measurement**: `pnpm exec vitest run novel/adapters/prompt-optimizer/src/structured.test.ts --reporter=basic` exits 0 with ≥1 test passing.
  - **Anchor**: rule #6 (let-it-crash — JSON.parse throwing synchronously is the correct shape for upstream-malformed inputs); rule #7 (chaos coverage — every failure-mode row needs a chaos test or a resolvable deferral); Armstrong, J., *Programming Erlang*, 2007 ch. 13 (the let-it-crash supervisor pattern).

- [ ] `verify-spawn-failed-exit-minus-one-substrate-resolves` — confirm the env-propagation + signal-capture substrate (PRs #666, #694) eliminates `verdict=spawn-failed exit=-1 stderr=(empty)` from cross-repo-runner spawns
  - **ID**: verify-spawn-failed-exit-minus-one-substrate-resolves
  - **Tags**: p3, longitudinal-verification, spawn, devin, observed-2026-05-23, follow-up
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Competitive-goal**: closes the runtime-verification half of `spawn-failed-exit-minus-one-silent-empty-stderr` (P0, resolved by substrate). The code-level fixes shipped via PR #666 (env propagation for DEVIN_*/CLAUDE_*/OPENAI_*/ANTHROPIC_* in the launchd plist generator) + PR #694 (`spawn-error-capture.test.ts` seam test asserting signal/stderr/stdout capture through `ProcessSpawnStrategy` → `runLive` → `LiveSpawnOutcome`). Hypothesis (a) from the parent task was the most likely cause and the code-level fix is in `bin/minsky:622-664`. This task confirms the fix is effective in production by collecting the longitudinal runtime data the parent task's Acceptance #1 + #2 require.
  - **Touches**: read-only — no source changes. Inspects `~/.minsky/daemon.log` + the daemon's launchd plist + `.minsky/experiment-store/cross-repo/*.jsonl` to count `spawn-failed exit=-1 stderr=(empty)` records.
  - **Details**: 2026-05-23 — `spawn-failed-exit-minus-one-silent-empty-stderr` (P0) closed without new code because both code-level fixes were already shipped: (1) `bin/minsky install-daemon` propagates `DEVIN_*|CLAUDE_*|OPENAI_*|ANTHROPIC_*` env vars from the operator's shell into the launchd plist's `EnvironmentVariables` dict (PR #666, lines 622-664 — addresses hypothesis (a) DEVIN_API_KEY not propagated); (2) `ProcessSpawnStrategy` + `runLive` + `host-loop` thread the `signal` field through to `LiveSpawnOutcome` so future spawn-failed events have non-empty `signal:` or `stderr:` for diagnosis (PR #694's `spawn-error-capture.test.ts` seam test). Hypotheses (b) TTY detect, (c) tmpfs GC, (d) EPM/zscaler, (e) PATH-missing were never reached because (a) was the most likely cause and the fix proved sufficient (or so the substrate's deployment suggests; this task confirms). To verify: run the operator's daemon for ≥1 hour with ≥3 successful task spawns; assert no `spawn-failed exit=-1 stderr=(empty)` records appear in the daemon log. If they DO appear post-substrate, the underlying cause is not (a) — re-open the parent task as a multi-hypothesis investigation focused on (b)-(e).
  - **Hypothesis**: post-substrate (PRs #666 + #694 merged into main), the daemon log over a 1-hour window shows ZERO `verdict=spawn-failed exit=-1 stderr=(empty)` records, AND every spawn-failed record (regardless of cause) includes a non-empty `signal:` or `stderr:` field. Falsifiable: if any spawn-failed record over 50 iterations has empty signal AND empty stderr, the signal-capture seam (PR #694) has a regression — re-open the parent task as a wiring bug. If signal/stderr ARE populated but `exit=-1 stderr=(empty)` records still occur, the env-propagation fix didn't catch the cause class — pursue hypotheses (b)-(e).
  - **Success**: `grep -c 'spawn-failed.*exit=-1.*stderr.*\(empty\)' ~/.minsky/daemon.log` returns 0 over a 1-hour window with ≥3 successful spawns; `pnpm stability-number <minsky-repo>` returns ≥50%. Companion: `launchctl print gui/$(id -u)/com.minsky.daemon | grep -E 'DEVIN_|CLAUDE_|OPENAI_|ANTHROPIC_'` shows ≥1 propagated env var (confirms install-daemon's emit reached the live plist).
  - **Pivot**: if the daemon log shows ≥1 `exit=-1 stderr=(empty)` record post-substrate AND the env propagation is confirmed live (`launchctl print` shows the propagated vars), the underlying cause is NOT (a). Re-open the parent task at hypothesis (b) — TTY detect-and-bail — and run the test in isolation: `bash -c 'unset $(env | grep -E ^MINSKY_ | cut -d= -f1); printf "echo hi" | devin --print --prompt-file /tmp/p.md --permission-mode dangerous </dev/null 2>&1'`.
  - **Measurement**: `grep -c 'spawn-failed.*exit=-1.*stderr.*\(empty\)' ~/.minsky/daemon.log | awk '{exit ($0 == 0) ? 0 : 1}'`; `launchctl print "gui/$(id -u)/com.minsky.daemon" 2>/dev/null | grep -cE '(DEVIN_|CLAUDE_|OPENAI_|ANTHROPIC_)_API_KEY' | awk '{exit ($0 >= 1) ? 0 : 1}'`.
  - **Anchor**: rule #11 (no flaky metric is load-bearing — daemon stability is load-bearing; this follow-up confirms the substrate fix is non-flaky); rule #17 (proactive healing — runtime verification IS the close-out of the substrate fix); PR #666 (env propagation, commit `cb11781`); PR #694 (signal-capture seam test, commit `2cfdde1`); the now-closed `spawn-failed-exit-minus-one-silent-empty-stderr` parent task block in git history.

- [ ] `verify-watchdog-substrate-eliminates-900s-spawn-failures` — confirm the dynamic-timeouts substrate (commit `2d2c15b`, 2026-05-18) eliminates the 900s watchdog kill class from cross-repo-runner spawns
  - **ID**: verify-watchdog-substrate-eliminates-900s-spawn-failures
  - **Tags**: p3, longitudinal-verification, watchdog, dynamic-timeouts, observed-2026-05-23, follow-up
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Competitive-goal**: closes the runtime-verification half of `watchdog-timeout-kills-productive-devin` (P0, resolved by infrastructure that landed the same day as the original observation). The code-level fix exists (dynamic-timeouts default 20min > observed 15min kill, max 45min, p95×1.5 scaling); operator-side runtime data confirms zero 900s spawn-failures over 10 consecutive iterations.
  - **Touches**: read-only — no source changes. Inspects `.minsky/experiment-store/cross-repo/*.jsonl` for spawn-failed records at 900s ±10ms.
  - **Details**: 2026-05-23 — `watchdog-timeout-kills-productive-devin` (P0) closed without a code change because the 900s kill class was already resolved by `novel/cross-repo-runner/src/dynamic-timeouts.ts` (PR commit `2d2c15b`, 2026-05-18). The substrate uses `DEFAULT_WATCHDOG_MS = 20 * 60 * 1000` (20min) when iteration history is insufficient + p95×1.5 scaling clamped to [2min, 45min] with sufficient history. The 900s observation predates the substrate landing on the same day. To confirm the fix is fully effective: run the operator's daemon for ≥10 iterations producing `spawn-failed` verdicts; assert NONE of them have `durationMs === 900_000 ± 10ms` (the original kill signature). The substrate's env-override (`MINSKY_LIVE_SPAWN_TIMEOUT_MS`) should also be inspected for accidental 900000 values in launchd plists, bootstrap scripts, or operator shell rc (already grepped clean as of 2026-05-23 — no production references, only test fixtures).
  - **Hypothesis**: post-substrate (PR commit `2d2c15b`), `spawn-failed` verdicts in `.minsky/experiment-store/cross-repo/*.jsonl` show ZERO records with `durationMs` in the range [899_990ms, 900_010ms]. Falsifiable: if any spawn-failed record across 10 consecutive iterations has durationMs ≈ 900_000ms, the substrate has a wiring gap that bypasses the dynamic settings (e.g., a different spawn path missed during the 2026-05-18 refactor).
  - **Success**: `jq 'select(.verdict=="spawn-failed") | select(.durationMs >= 899990 and .durationMs <= 900010)' .minsky/experiment-store/cross-repo/*.jsonl | wc -l` returns 0 across ≥10 iterations on ≥3 hosts. Companion: `pnpm minsky watch` shows the dynamic watchdog value (computed from history) is logged per iteration banner.
  - **Pivot**: if the measurement returns ≥1 hit (i.e., the 900s kill still occurs post-substrate), inspect the iteration's environment for `MINSKY_LIVE_SPAWN_TIMEOUT_MS=900000` accidentally set in a bootstrap script or launchd plist. If env is clean, the dynamic-timeouts wiring has a bypass — re-open the parent task as a code-level bug, identify the bypass path, and ship a fix.
  - **Measurement**: `jq -r 'select(.verdict=="spawn-failed") | select(.durationMs >= 899990 and .durationMs <= 900010) | .ts' .minsky/experiment-store/cross-repo/*.jsonl | wc -l` returns 0; `grep -rE "MINSKY_LIVE_SPAWN_TIMEOUT_MS\s*=\s*['\"]?900_?000" ~/.minsky/ /Library/LaunchAgents/com.minsky*.plist 2>/dev/null | wc -l` returns 0 (no accidental hardcoded 900s overrides).
  - **Anchor**: rule #11 (no flaky metric is load-bearing — watchdog timeout is load-bearing; this follow-up confirms the dynamic-substrate fix is non-flaky); rule #17 (proactive healing — runtime verification IS the close-out of the substrate fix); `novel/cross-repo-runner/src/dynamic-timeouts.ts` commit `2d2c15b` 2026-05-18 (the substrate this task verifies); the now-closed `watchdog-timeout-kills-productive-devin` parent task block in git history.

- [ ] `wire-duplicate-detector-longitudinal-verification` — verify the wired-in `decideDuplicate` detector eliminates duplicate close-out PRs over 100 live daemon iterations
  - **ID**: wire-duplicate-detector-longitudinal-verification
  - **Tags**: p3, longitudinal-verification, daemon, observed-2026-05-23, follow-up
  - **Milestone**: M1
  - **Competitive-goal**: closes the residual runtime verification for `wire-duplicate-pr-detector-into-cross-repo-runner` (P0, shipped). The code wiring landed without operator-side daemon-runtime data; this task collects that data to confirm the hypothesis ("zero duplicate close-out PRs in the next 100 daemon iterations") is borne out in production. If duplicates STILL occur post-wire, the pivot path (per-iteration gh-result caching, or a `~/.minsky/recently-merged.jsonl` fallback) gets surfaced.
  - **Touches**: read-only — no source changes. Inspects `~/.minsky/daemon.log` + `gh pr list --state merged --search "in:title"` outputs to count duplicate close-out PRs.
  - **Details**: 2026-05-23 — `wire-duplicate-pr-detector-into-cross-repo-runner` (P0) shipped the code wiring (`isDuplicatePrForTask` + `listAllPrsForTaskId` + integration into `pickTask` callback of `runHostLoop`; gated on `live` mode). The detector kicks in for every live iteration. To confirm it works in production: (a) run the daemon for ≥100 iterations across ≥3 hosts; (b) for each task that produced a close-out PR, count the open + recently-merged sibling PRs that match `prTitleNamesTask`; (c) the count should be 1 per task (no duplicates); (d) the per-iteration daemon log should show `↩ skipping <task-id>: PR #N already open|merged Nd ago (decideDuplicate)` lines for tasks that the detector caught. If any duplicates appear post-wire, escalate to the Pivot path (per-iteration gh-result caching, or recently-merged.jsonl fallback).
  - **Hypothesis**: post-wire (PR for `wire-duplicate-pr-detector-into-cross-repo-runner`), the daemon detects merged-recent + open duplicates correctly and emits `↩ skipping` log lines for ≥1 daemon iteration that would have created a duplicate pre-wire. Falsifiable: if `daemon.log` over 100 iterations shows zero `↩ skipping` lines for tasks with `decideDuplicate` verdicts, the detector path isn't being exercised — either the daemon isn't running long enough (extend the window) OR there's no actual duplicate-candidate work in the iteration sample (re-pick a different host with known re-pick churn).
  - **Success**: `daemon.log` over a 100-iteration window shows ≥1 `↩ skipping <task-id>: PR #N (decideDuplicate)` log line; `gh pr list --state merged --search "daemon-survives-machine-restart in:title" --limit 50 | jq 'length'` returns 1 (no new duplicates added since the wire-in PR landed).
  - **Pivot**: if the detector emits zero `↩ skipping` lines over 100 iterations AND duplicate close-out PRs are still being created post-wire, the gh-search query may not be returning the right PRs (Slack/GHE search-index lag). Switch to the Pivot path documented in the parent task block: write a `~/.minsky/recently-merged.jsonl` cache and consult it instead.
  - **Measurement**: `grep -c '↩ skipping.*decideDuplicate' ~/.minsky/daemon.log | awk '{exit ($0 >= 1) ? 0 : 1}' && gh pr list --state merged --search 'daemon-survives-machine-restart in:title' --limit 50 --json number,closedAt | jq -e '. | length == 1'`
  - **Anchor**: rule #11 (no flaky metric is load-bearing — the wire-in PR's Success criterion is runtime-only; this follow-up collects the longitudinal data the PR couldn't verify in-session); rule #17 (proactive healing — runtime verification IS the close-out of the proactive-heal loop); the parent task's Pivot field at the now-shipped `wire-duplicate-pr-detector-into-cross-repo-runner` block (referenced in git history for this repo).

- [ ] `research-finding-edobry-minsky-cross-citation` — evaluate cross-citation with edobry/minsky (architectural sibling using organizational cybernetics)
  - **ID**: research-finding-edobry-minsky-cross-citation
  - **Tags**: p3, research-followup, observed-2026-05-22, prior-art, cross-citation
  - **Milestone**: M2
  - **Competitive-goal**: closes the "are there other Minsky-shaped projects?" question raised by docs/prior-art-and-name-collisions.md. edobry/minsky shares the "task queue + agent + feedback loop" shape using organizational cybernetics (Stafford Beer's VSM) for HITL coordination — different focus (HITL) but adjacent enough that operators searching "Minsky autonomous coding agent" will find both. Mutual cross-citation benefits both projects' positioning + protects against accidental territorial conflict.
  - **Touches**: `docs/prior-art-and-name-collisions.md` (already cross-references in the disambiguation table), `competitors/edobry-minsky.md` (new — if the deep-dive justifies it), `vision.md` § "Pattern conformance index" (add row if architectural overlap is substantive).
  - **Details**: 2026-05-22 prior-art research surfaced edobry/minsky (<https://github.com/edobry/minsky>, 5★, early-stage). The project applies organizational cybernetics + progressive gates + visibility mechanisms to HITL agent workflows. Read its vision/architecture docs end-to-end. Document: (a) where the architectural shapes overlap (both have feedback loops; both are markdown-driven); (b) where they diverge (HITL vs autonomous; cybernetics-as-explicit-pattern vs cybernetics-as-implicit-substrate); (c) whether mutual citation in each project's README improves operator discovery for both. Consider opening a GitHub issue on edobry/minsky offering cross-citation.
  - **Hypothesis**: edobry/minsky and fyodoriv/minsky are complementary tools that both apply cybernetic principles, with different operator audiences (HITL vs autonomous). Mutual cross-citation increases discoverability for both without competitive cannibalisation.
  - **Success**: `docs/prior-art-and-name-collisions.md` has a deep-link to edobry/minsky's specific architecture sections (not just the repo root); edobry/minsky has been contacted via a GitHub issue or discussion offering mutual citation; if accepted, both READMEs link to each other.
  - **Pivot**: if edobry/minsky has gone dormant (no commits in 90 days at evaluation time) OR if its architectural focus has diverged from the original cybernetics framing, downgrade to a one-line mention in the disambiguation table without proposing collaboration.
  - **Measurement**: `[ -f docs/prior-art-and-name-collisions.md ] && grep -c "edobry/minsky" docs/prior-art-and-name-collisions.md` ≥2 (one in disambiguation table + one in lineage), AND a comment on either project's GitHub from the other project's maintainer acknowledging the cross-citation.
  - **Anchor**: rule #1 (don't reinvent — adjacent project already exists with overlapping ideas); competitors/README.md § "What Minsky uniquely does" (mutual citation strengthens the moat framing); operator directive 2026-05-22 ("research what else exists with minsky word in it"); edobry, *Minsky — development workflow orchestration*, github.com/edobry/minsky, observed 2026-05-22.

- [ ] `research-finding-reflexion-episodic-memory-pattern` — adopt Reflexion's episodic-memory pattern for Minsky's iteration ledger
  - **ID**: research-finding-reflexion-episodic-memory-pattern
  - **Tags**: p3, research-followup, observed-2026-05-22, prior-art, mape-k, m2
  - **Milestone**: M2
  - **Competitive-goal**: closes the "no concrete prior art for our self-improvement loop" framing weakness. Reflexion (Shinn et al., NeurIPS 2023) is the closest LLM-self-improvement prior art — 91% pass@1 on HumanEval via episodic memory + linguistic self-reflection. Minsky's experiment-store substrate is a generic iteration ledger; structuring it around Reflexion's reflection/memory/action loop would (a) give the closed-loop MAPE-K work in user-story-003 a published pattern to instantiate, (b) let Minsky cite a peer-reviewed result rather than inventing the wheel.
  - **Touches**: `novel/experiment-record/src/reflexion-schema.ts` (new — episodic memory schema), `user-stories/003-mape-k-improves-prompts.md` (update Plan section to cite Reflexion's reflection step), `vision.md` § "Pattern conformance index" row for the closed-loop MAPE-K should cite Reflexion as the implementation pattern.
  - **Details**: 2026-05-22 prior-art research confirmed Reflexion is the closest published prior art for "LLM agent learns from its own failures via episodic memory" — currently cited in user-story-003 § "Pattern conformance" but not deeply integrated. Read the paper (<https://arxiv.org/pdf/2303.11366>) end-to-end. Document: (a) Reflexion's memory schema (what fields per memory entry, how scoped), (b) the reflection prompt (how the agent's failure is converted to a memory), (c) the recall step (how memories are surfaced for the next attempt). Then prototype `.minsky/experiment-store/reflexions/` populated by a per-iteration reflection step in the tick-loop.
  - **Hypothesis**: Structuring Minsky's experiment-store entries around Reflexion's reflection schema (state → action → outcome → reflection) makes the closed-loop MAPE-K in user-story-003 cheaper to ship by ~50% (the substrate already exists; what's missing is the reflection step).
  - **Success**: `novel/experiment-record/src/reflexion-schema.ts` exists with a Zod schema mirroring Reflexion's memory entry; one tick-loop iteration writes a reflection entry; the integration test asserts the entry has the four required fields.
  - **Pivot**: if Reflexion's schema doesn't transfer (e.g., Minsky's iterations are too coarse-grained or the reflection step adds >2x latency), drop the schema integration and keep Reflexion as a citation-only prior art in vision.md.
